<a id="t-H1847"></a>
### H1847 da.at — knowledge (`mti_id=955`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `H1847` · Lang: Hebrew · Owner: —
2. Registry: R100 knowledge · Legacy C-cluster: C02
3. Sub-group: `M15-C` (Knowledge as inner content and covenantal knowing)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 91
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=955` · `md_version=2` · `vc_status=vc_completed`

**Verses:** 89 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (6)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 360 | 1838-001 | Term names culpable spiritual ignorance — the inner condition of not knowing God's character, ways, or will, which becomes the basis of moral failure or self-deception |  |
| 1504 | 955-001 | Knowledge as the foundational inner capacity — rooted in the fear of the Lord (Pro 1:7) and experienced inwardly as delight in the soul (Pro 2:10); the cognitive-spiritual capacity that grounds wisdom. | Revised during Dimension Review Phase C/anchor consultation: description corrected — knowledge 955-001 |
| 1505 | 955-002 | Knowledge of God as defining inner orientation — presence = faithfulness, absence = ruin |  |
| 1506 | 955-003 | Knowledge as Spirit-given inner endowment — bestowed from above |  |
| 1507 | 955-004 | Moral condition of inner intent — presence or absence determining accountability |  |
| 1508 | 955-005 | Limits and failures of human knowledge as inner condition producing ruin |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (20 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-174-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Pro 1:4 | Registry 174 (wisdom) contains multiple directionally-determined term pairs: (1) a.rum (H6175) and or.mah (H6195) — prudence as cognitive faculty vs. craftiness as moral orientation toward deception (Sub-pattern B); (2) sofos/sofia (G4680/G4678) — wisdom toward God (4458-001, Moral/Conscience character type) vs. wisdom turned inward as self-deception (4458-002, Moral/Conscience distortion); (3) cha.kham (H2449/H2450) — wisdom from fear of Lord (528-003, Spiritual/God-ward) vs. wisdom self-attributed (528-003 note). The cluster reveals that wisdom vocabulary in Scripture is structurally shaped by the direction of the soul's orientation. Session B should treat wisdom terms as directionally-sensitive throughout. |
| OBS-103-T2-019 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Hos 6:6 | What love is not, from the data: love is not sentiment — it is commanded (Mat 22:37) and exercised irrespective of feeling (enemy-love, Mat 5:44). Love is not mere natural affection — phileō is insufficient as the full standard; agapē and chesed exceed phileō in obligatory reach. Love is not the same as desire (R043) — the desire registry owns the primary ahav verbal form (H0157G); desire and love share a mechanism but the programme treats them as distinct (OBS-103-010, OBS-103-022). Love is not the same as goodness (R067) — the tov family is XREF; love produces goodness but is not identical with it (OBS-103-014). The precise boundary between love and compassion (R023), kindness (R099), and mercy (R111) cannot be fully drawn from Stage 2a evidence alone — these are the programme's adjacent characteristics whose distinctions require dedicated treatment in Session D. Partial: the internal boundary is evidenced; the inter-registry distinctions are named as gaps deferred to Session D. |
| OBS-103-T2-040 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Hos 6:6 | Heart-location reveals love's engagement with all four of the heart's functions: (a) **knowing** — the heart that loves also knows (Joh 15:15 — friendship constituted by knowledge-disclosure, OBS-103-052; Hos 6:6 — God desires knowledge of God alongside chesed, OBS-103-091); (b) **willing** — the heart is the seat of intention, and love is commanded at the heart level (Mat 22:37; 1Ki 8:18 — 'it was in your heart to build'); (c) **feeling** — Song 5:2 ('my heart was awake') — the heart's affective persistence in love; (d) **moral awareness** — Pro 3:3 (chesed and faithfulness written on the heart as moral character). Love engages the heart's full integrating function — it is not a partial heart-engagement but a total one, consistent with the 'with all your heart' formulation of the commandment. |
| OBS-103-T2-041 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Hos 6:6 | Yes — the great commandment includes 'with all your mind' (dianoia — Mat 22:37, OBS-103-139), explicitly locating love at the mind level. Phili 4:8 (OBS-103-133) directs the mind to attend to what is lovely (prosfilēs) — love's aesthetic quality becomes the object of cognitive direction. Joh 15:15 (OBS-103-052) — love constituted by knowledge-disclosure — places the cognitive (knowledge) at the centre of the love-relationship. Hos 6:6 (OBS-103-091) — God desires 'knowledge of God' alongside chesed — the knowledge-love co-priority is a mind-engagement. The mind's engagement is primarily as the organ of knowledge that love opens access to (friendship = mutual knowledge) and as the faculty directed to attend to the lovely. |
| OBS-103-T2-042 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Hos 6:6 | Mind-engagement reveals that love has a genuine cognitive dimension — it is not sub-cognitive. The cognitive dimension operates in three ways: (a) **knowledge as love's medium** — friendship/love constituted by mutual knowledge-sharing (Joh 15:15) means love opens access to the inner world of the other and requires the mind's engagement to receive what is shared; (b) **cognitive direction toward the lovely** — Phili 4:8 makes the mind's attentiveness to what is lovely a cognitive act in service of love's orientation; (c) **knowledge of God as love's fruit** — Hos 6:6 and Jer 31:34 (OBS-103-072) place knowledge of God as the companion of love, with the new covenant completing the knowledge that love currently mediates. Love is cognitively demanding in the biblical data — it requires and produces knowledge. |
| OBS-103-T2-063 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Hos 6:6 | Yes — the desire-love boundary is analytically significant in the data. The dod and ahavah-intense vocabulary occupies the same semantic space as desire (R043 shares the ahav verbal root, OBS-103-022). Song 5:6 (OBS-103-062) — 'my soul failed me' — is desire-like in its intensity: the longing for the absent beloved. The yearning of God's inner parts (Jer 31:20, OBS-103-117) uses the same language-field as desire. Hos 6:6 (OBS-103-091): 'I desire (chaphets) steadfast love' — love becomes the object of God's desire. Heb 12:16 (OBS-103-078): Esau's appetite (trophe — food-desire) displacing love's covenantal inheritance is the paradigmatic desire-love conflict. The desire-faculty is love's closest adjacent faculty — love and desire share a mechanism (orientation toward an object) but are distinguished in the programme by the nature and stability of the commitment. |
| OBS-103-T2-099 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Hos 6:6 | Yes — three root-level connections are analytically significant: (a) **Racham root** — spans love (103), compassion (23), and mercy (111) as a unified etymological family. The womb-compassion-love nexus (OBS-103-007, OBS-103-029) is a root-level connection — the same bodily root generates three distinct registry characteristics; (b) **Tov root** — spans love (103) and goodness (67), crossing cluster boundaries (C17 and implied goodness cluster). The goodness-as-love-expression connection (SD-009) is root-level; (c) **Ahav root** — spans love (103) and desire (43). H0157H (friend-ahev, OWNER R103) and H0157G (lover-ahev, OWNER R043 via XREF placement) are the same root, different programme assignments. Root-sharing confirms that the programme's registries are not sealed semantic units but connected through shared etymological architecture. |
| OBS-103-T2-137 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Hos 6:6 | Yes — the epistemic dimension of love is one of the data's most distinctive findings. Joh 15:15 (OBS-103-052): love's definitive act is knowledge-disclosure — Jesus names the friends as those to whom he has made known all that he heard from the Father. The friend-love relationship is constituted by mutual knowledge-sharing. Hos 6:6 (OBS-103-091): 'knowledge of God' (da'at elohim) co-prioritised with chesed — love and knowing are structural companions in the OT. Jer 31:34 (OBS-103-072): in the new covenant, each person will know God directly — the eschatological love-community is one of immediate knowledge. Joh 21:17 (OBS-103-153): Peter's love-confession appeals to Christ's omniscience ('Lord, you know everything; you know that I love you') — being known by Christ is the ground of the love-confession. The epistemic dimension: love is the relational structure within which knowing becomes possible; knowledge is what love shares; being known is the most intimate expression of being loved. |
| OBS-103-T2-144 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Hos 6:6 | Love **enables and deepens** cognition in its righteous form. Joh 15:15 (OBS-103-052): love opens access to knowledge the beloved has — the cognitive gift is inseparable from the love-relationship. Hos 6:6 (OBS-103-091): God prioritises 'knowledge of God' alongside chesed — love and knowledge advance together. Jer 31:34 (OBS-103-072): the eschatological love-community is one of direct knowledge — love's fullness produces cognitive fullness. Disordered love **impairs** cognition: love-of-money produces departure from faith and consequent cognitive loss (1Ti 6:10, OBS-103-132); love-of-darkness refuses the cognitive reorientation that light would require (Joh 3:19, OBS-103-147). Love enables the kind of knowledge (da'at — relational knowing) that is inaccessible without the love-relationship; it does not bypass discursive reason but opens the relational dimension of knowledge that reason alone cannot reach. |
| OBS-103-T2-145 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Hos 6:6 | The cognitive engagement pattern reveals that love is the programme's primary access-condition for relational knowledge. This is not the knowledge available through observation or reason alone — it is the knowledge that flows within the love-relationship: what the Father tells the Son, what the Son tells his friends (Joh 15:15). The pattern also reveals that love and knowledge are not rivals but partners: Hos 6:6 places them as co-equal priorities above ritual. The eschatological vision (Jer 31:34) confirms that love's fullness and knowledge's fullness coincide — in the final state, perfect love produces direct knowledge. The nature of love is therefore partly epistemic: it is not merely emotional but a structural condition for the deepest form of knowing. |
| OBS-099-T2-007 | OBSERVATION | Hos 6:6 | Genuinely shared, with structural asymmetry. The same terms (chesed, chrēstos/chrēstotēs) are predicated of both without distinction of kind. 2Sa 22:26 makes the sharing explicit: "With the merciful you show yourself merciful" [XREG: H2616A, OBS-099-096]. Asymmetry: God's chesed is inexhaustible and eschatologically guaranteed; human chesed is creaturely and can be withdrawn (Hos 6:6 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-088]). The sharing is real; the asymmetry is structural. |
| OBS-099-T2-008 | OBSERVATION | Hos 6:6 | Presence: the chasid is set apart, heard, and eschatologically preserved — image functioning [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094/095]. Absence: Hos 6:6 names the absence of chesed as fundamental religious failure [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-088]; Mic 7:2 pairs its absence with universal corruption [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]; Gen 6:5 establishes total inner corruption as the context of maximum kindness-absence [OBS-099-046]. Conclusion: presence = functioning image; absence = diagnostic of image-damage or suppression. |
| OBS-099-T2-017 | OBSERVATION | Hos 6:6 | Three structural opposites at different levels: (a) Pride/self-glorification: Dan 4:30 — pride claims; kindness gives [OBS-099-054]; Isa 2:11/17 places creaturely pretension as opposite of humility and kindness. (b) Cruelty/severity: Rom 11:22 — apotomia is the contrasting face of divine kindness [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-091]. (c) Withdrawal of chesed: Hos 6:6 / Mic 7:2 name its absence as moral ruin [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-088]. Most structurally opposed inner state: the self-enclosed orientation — the person turned inward, claiming, rather than outward, giving. |
| OBS-099-T2-018 | OBSERVATION | Hos 6:6 | Four explicit resistances: (a) Self-serving motivation — chrēstologia fails because inner motivation is self-serving [OBS-099-074]. (b) Envy, boasting, arrogance — 1Cor 13:4 places kindness within love against these [OBS-099-072]. (c) Ritual without inner disposition — Hos 6:6 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-088]. (d) Withdrawal from the other — Gen 44:29/32 show that the shadow of kindness is the burden accepted in remaining present rather than withdrawing [OBS-099-062/063]. |
| OBS-099-T2-030 | OBSERVATION | Hos 6:6 | Three modes evidenced: (a) Pharaoh — hardens further; non-reception intensifies inner rigidity [OBS-099-059]. (b) Rom 2:4 — presumes comfortably; self-deceived [OBS-099-076]. (c) Hos 6:6 — substitutes ritual for inner disposition; outwardly religious while inwardly chesed-absent [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-088]. Gap: the evidence does not directly address the psychological state of the person who recognises and refuses human kindness specifically. |
| OBS-099-T2-047 | OBSERVATION | Hos 6:6 | Yes — two modes: (a) As prerequisite: Hos 6:6 pairs chesed with "knowledge of God" — genuine kindness requires cognitive grasp of who God is [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-088]. (b) As denied: Rom 2:4 — failure of cognition preconditions presumption: "not knowing that God's kindness is meant to lead you to repentance" [OBS-099-076]. Kindness requires knowing God to flow genuinely, and knowing God's intention to be received correctly. |
| OBS-099-T2-051 | OBSERVATION | Hos 6:6 | Yes — at two levels: (a) Kindness presupposes moral evaluation: Hos 6:6 — the evaluation that chesed ranks above sacrifice [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-088]; Pro 3:3 — moral instruction implies moral evaluation capacity [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-087]. (b) Kindness is itself a morally evaluative act: to show kindness is to discern that another's need warrants a response. The counterfeit fails moral evaluation precisely because inner assessment is self-serving [OBS-099-074]. Moral evaluation is both prerequisite of genuine kindness and capacity deepened by its exercise. |
| OBS-099-T2-052 | OBSERVATION | Hos 6:6 | Yes — structurally. (a) Gen 44:32 — Judah's surety burden anticipates guilt if he fails [OBS-099-063]. Inner cost of loving-kindness (bearing responsibility for another) is conscience-engaging. (b) Hos 6:6 — ritual without chesed produces a bad conscience in the theological sense: worship while withholding what God desires [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-088]. (c) Rom 2:4 — "not knowing" = moral cognitive failure that a properly functioning conscience should catch [OBS-099-076]. Kindness engages conscience as the inner witness monitoring whether one's orientation is aligned with what one knows to be right. |
| OBS-099-T2-060 | OBSERVATION | Hos 6:6 | Yes — both modes. (a) Change of orientation: Rom 2:4 — aimed at repentance (metanoia — change of mind/direction) [OBS-099-076]. (b) Change of condition (formation): the chasid formation arc — person whose character is shaped by chesed over time [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]. Reversibility: not explicitly addressed. Hos 6:6 (withdrawal of chesed as communal failure) and Mic 7:2 (godly has perished) suggest communal formation can be lost — individual reversibility not directly addressed. |
| OBS-099-T2-077 | OBSERVATION | Hos 6:6 | Although the mind is not explicitly named as kindness's constitutional location, the verse evidence shows kindness engaging the mind's cognitive-discernment function in two specific ways. First, Hos 6:6 — "I desire steadfast love and not sacrifice, the knowledge of God rather than burnt offerings" — pairs chesed with knowledge of God (da'at Elohim). Genuine chesed requires a cognitive grasp of who God is; without this knowledge, the person substitutes ritual for the real inner disposition. The mind's function of knowing and discerning is therefore prerequisite to genuine kindness [OBS-099-088]. Second, Rom 2:4 — "not knowing that God's kindness is meant to lead you to repentance" — names cognitive failure as the mechanism by which received kindness is misappropriated. A correctly functioning cognitive faculty recognises the purpose of divine kindness; a failing one presumes on it [OBS-099-076]. What this reveals: kindness is not a mind-level characteristic but it requires the mind's knowing-function to operate correctly — it is cognitively dependent without being cognitively located. |

<a id="t-H3289"></a>
### H3289 ya.ats — to advise (`mti_id=749`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `H3289` · Lang: Hebrew · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R032 counsel · Legacy C-cluster: C02
3. Sub-group: `M15-E` (Deliberative planning, counsel, and purposive intent)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 79
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=749` · `md_version=2` · `vc_status=vc_completed`

**Verses:** 73 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (3)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 405 | 749-001 | Counsel as wisdom given or received between persons, shaping inner orientation |  |
| 406 | 749-002 | Counsel as expression of inner moral character — noble, wicked, or hostile purposing |  |
| 407 | 749-003 | Divine counsel as God's inner intentionality directed toward persons or nations |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (10 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-175-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Isa 9:6 | Wonder vocabulary divides between the divine side (Theological/Divine-Human) and the human response (Affective/Emotional). Session B should examine wonder as the inner state at the interface between human capacity and divine transcendence — the point where the inner person's capacity to comprehend is exceeded by what God is and does. |
| OBS-117-T2-004 | OBSERVATION | Isa 9:6 | The evidence shows both. (1) Peace as part of original design: Psa 85:10 ("righteousness and peace kiss each other") presents the eschatological restoration as a reunion of characteristics that belong together — implying they were originally united. The messianic reign of peace (Isa 9:6-7, Mic 5:5, OBS-117-147) is framed as the restoration of what was intended, not the creation of something novel. (2) Peace as response to the fallen condition: the "covenant of peace" structure (Num 25:12, Isa 54:10, Eze 34:25/37:26, OBS-117-145) presupposes a broken relationship being restored — covenant peace is given because natural peace has been ruptured. Isa 53:5 ("the chastisement that brought us peace, and with his wounds we are healed") explicitly frames peace as purchased healing of a rupture. Gap: the evidence does not directly address the pre-fall condition — Scripture's earliest peace statements (Gen 28:21, Gen 37:4) already presuppose the fallen world. The precise relationship between created peace and covenant-restored peace requires Session D synthesis with other registries. |
| OBS-117-T2-005 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Isa 9:6 | Yes — and more strongly than almost any other registry. The eschatological orientation of peace is the most developed in the programme's data. Evidence: (1) H7965G group 2508-004 (DHC) — messianic peace is inherently future-oriented: "Prince of Peace" (Isa 9:6), "of his government and of peace there will be no end" (Isa 9:7), eschatological community dwelling in peaceful habitation (Isa 32:18), OBS-117-147; (2) H7965G group 2508-002 — the eternal covenant of peace (Isa 54:10, Eze 37:26) is a future-facing promise given in the present; (3) Psa 72:7 (peace abounding till the moon be no more), Isa 66:12 (peace extended like a river); (4) Jer 29:11 (plans for welfare, future, and hope — OBS-117-149) — God's peace-intention is explicitly teleological: "a future and a hope." Peace is not only what a person presently is but what they are moving toward. |
| OBS-117-T2-009 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Isa 9:6 | Yes — more explicitly than most characteristics in the programme. The typological structure is built into the vocabulary itself: YHWH-Shalom (Judg 6:24, 406-001) → Prince of Peace (Isa 9:6, 2508-004) → He shall be their peace (Mic 5:5, 2508-004) → He himself is our peace (Eph 2:14, G1515 group 426-003). Each stage names the same "X IS peace" ontological structure at a different historical moment. Isa 53:5 ("the chastisement that brought us peace... with his wounds we are healed") is the most compressed typological moment: the Servant's suffering produces peace as its direct result — and the NT reader recognises this as the mechanism of the peace Christ IS. The typology is not imposed on the text but inhabits it — the peace vocabulary itself carries the forward-pointing structure. DIM-117-SD003 (raised in DimReview session) targets this exactly. |
| OBS-117-T2-012 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Isa 9:6 | The primary Hebrew name is shalom (sha.lom, H7965G and sub-forms) — the most semantically wide term in the OT inner-being vocabulary. Its breadth is encoded in the STEP gloss: completeness, soundness, welfare, peace, quiet, tranquillity, contentment, friendship, covenant peace, peace from war (OBS-117-009). The name itself reveals that peace is not a simple state but a structural condition of completeness — everything in its proper place. Secondary Hebrew names: nachat (na.chat — quietness/quietude, H5183A), shaqat (sha.qat — to quiet/be quiet, H8252), nuach (nu.ach — rest, H5117), menuchah (me.nu.chah — resting place, H4496H). The primary Greek name is eirēnē (G1515) — which in the LXX became the translation word for shalom, absorbing its full range. The sub-names (YHWH-Shalom: "LORD is peace"; Prince of Peace: sar shalom) reveal that peace is not merely a human condition but a divine identity: the name carries what the named one IS. |
| OBS-117-T2-032 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Isa 9:6 | The most important single finding from T1: peace (shalom/eirēnē) is not primarily an emotional state or an absence of conflict, but an integrative wholeness grounded in divine identity and structured through covenant. The evidence establishes four dimensions that are not alternatives but a sequence: (1) Covenantal ground: shalom exists because God covenants it — the covenant of peace (Isa 54:10) is the basis; (2) Relational gift: God gives shalom to those in covenant (Num 6:26), speaks it to his saints (Psa 85:8), plans it for his people (Jer 29:11); (3) Inner condition: the received shalom becomes the experienced condition of the whole person — perfect peace for the trust-directed mind (Isa 26:3), soul at rest (Psa 4:8), welfare of the body and soul (Psa 38:3, 55:18); (4) Eschatological telos: the peace given and experienced is not complete until it is embodied in a Person — the Prince of Peace (Isa 9:6), he who shall be their peace (Mic 5:5), he who himself is our peace (Eph 2:14). DIM-117-002 (the pending finding about the nature of shalom) is hereby resolved: shalom is all four dimensions simultaneously, as a structured and connected reality. The central analytical finding is the sequence: divine identity → covenant gift → inner condition → eschatological embodiment in a Person. [RESOLVING DIM-117-002] |
| OBS-117-T2-136 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Isa 9:6 | Sha.lom (H7965G) functions as: (1) Noun (primary): the condition of peace — "in peace I will lie down" (Psa 4:8), "covenant of peace" (Isa 54:10); (2) Noun used as greeting formula: "Peace be to you" (1Sa 25:6, H7965K); (3) Noun functioning as an identity statement: "I am for peace" (Psa 120:7 — ani sha.lom, where sha.lom is the predicate nominative); (4) Noun used adverbially (H7965L): "wholly" (Jer 13:19 — all-verses-fail, no inner-being); (5) Noun in construct chain as descriptor: "covenant of peace" (brit sha.lom), "man of peace" (ish sha.lom, Psa 37:37), "Prince of Peace" (sar sha.lom, Isa 9:6). The identity-statement form (ani sha.lom — "I am peace") is analytically distinctive: sha.lom becomes a predicate nominative, identifying the person with the characteristic. This grammatical form is unique in the registry and is the most concentrated expression of the moral-character dimension. |
| OBS-117-T2-144 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Isa 9:6 | The full vocabulary arc — from SHA.L (root: completeness) through the shalom sub-forms (G/H/I/J/K/L) through the Hebrew silence/rest vocabulary (H5117/H5183A/H8252 etc.) through the Greek eirēnē family (G1515, G1514, G1516, G1517) — reveals that peace encompasses: (1) The root: completeness-as-ground; (2) The inner condition: wholeness, welfare, rest, quiet; (3) The relational form: friendship, greeting-blessing, covenant; (4) The divine identity: YHWH-Shalom, Prince of Peace; (5) The act: making peace (eirēnopoieō), being at peace (eirēneuō), pursuing peace (baqash sha.lom); (6) The person: man of peace, the peaceable (eirēnikos); (7) The eschatological telos: no end to his peace, eternal covenant. The arc confirms the T1 central finding (Q&A-033): shalom is a structured sequence (divine identity → covenant gift → inner condition → eschatological embodiment) whose vocabulary reflects every stage of that structure. |
| OBS-117-T2-146 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Isa 9:6 | The peace registry draws from all major biblical literary forms: (1) Prophecy (dominant in OT): Isa 9:6/7, 26:3, 30:15, 53:5, 54:10, Jer 29:11, Mic 5:5, Eze 34:25, 37:26 — the prophetic corpus carries the eschatological and messianic peace evidence and the refusal-of-peace pattern; (2) Psalms: Psa 4:8, 28:3, 32:3, 34:14, 62:5, 85:8, 119:165, 120:7, 131:2 — carries the experiential/devotional inner-peace evidence; (3) Wisdom: Pro 3:2/17, Ecc 9:17, Jam 3:17/18 — carries the peace-wisdom correlation; (4) Epistle: Rom 5:1, 8:6, Phili 4:7, Gal 5:22, 1Th 5:23, Col 1:20, Eph 2:14 — carries the NT theological synthesis; (5) Torah/Narrative: Num 6:26, Deu 28:65, Deu 29:19, Gen 28:21 — foundational covenant structures; (6) Lament: Lam 3:17 — the peaceless-soul at depth. The breadth of literary form is among the widest in the programme — peace is not a genre-specific concept but spans the full biblical literary spectrum. |
| OBS-117-T2-148 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Isa 9:6 | Primary contextual settings: (1) Covenantal: the covenant-of-peace texts (Num 6:26, Isa 54:10, Eze 34:25/37:26) — peace is given and confirmed in covenantal ceremony and prophetic announcement; (2) Liturgical/worship: Psa 4:8, 62:5, 85:8, 119:165, 131:2 — the psalmic context is personal worship and prayer; peace is experienced and expressed in the devotional posture; (3) Prophetic warning/lament: Isa 28:12, 30:15, Jer 6:14, Lam 3:17 — the prophetic context of peace refused, false peace proclaimed, and the soul bereft; (4) Eschatological vision: Isa 9:6/7, 32:18, Mic 5:5, Eze 34:25 — messianic and eschatological peace in prophetic vision; (5) Epistolary/communal: Rom 5:1, Phili 4:7, Eph 2:14, 1Th 5:23 — NT peace in the context of the Christian community and Paul's pastoral letters; (6) Crisis/encounter: Judg 6:23, Dan 10:19 — peace given in moments of personal encounter with the divine. The richest contextual setting is prophetic (both warning and vision) — the prophets carry the fullest peace theology. |

<a id="t-H7919A"></a>
### H7919A sa.khal — be prudent (`mti_id=519`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `H7919A` · Lang: Hebrew · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R174 wisdom · Legacy C-cluster: C02
3. Sub-group: `M15-D` (Discernment and practical judgment)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 63
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=519` · `md_version=2` · `vc_status=vc_completed`

**Verses:** 61 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (3)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 706 | 5099-002 | Term names the abstaining from taste as an expression of inner grief or penitence — the voluntary refusal to eat as the outward form of an inward state of mourning or contrition |  |
| 2490 | 519-001 | Term names prudent wisdom as an inner capacity — understanding, considering, and perceiving wisely as an orientation toward God and truth |  |
| 2491 | 519-002 | Term names the inner wisdom that produces wise conduct and success — the capacity for prudent discernment in action that governs how the person acts (Isa 52:13); wisdom as the inner quality expressed in competent, God-aligned conduct. | Revised during Dimension Review Phase C/anchor consultation: description corrected — wisdom 519-002 |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (52 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-174-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Isa 52:13 | Registry 174 (wisdom) contains multiple directionally-determined term pairs: (1) a.rum (H6175) and or.mah (H6195) — prudence as cognitive faculty vs. craftiness as moral orientation toward deception (Sub-pattern B); (2) sofos/sofia (G4680/G4678) — wisdom toward God (4458-001, Moral/Conscience character type) vs. wisdom turned inward as self-deception (4458-002, Moral/Conscience distortion); (3) cha.kham (H2449/H2450) — wisdom from fear of Lord (528-003, Spiritual/God-ward) vs. wisdom self-attributed (528-003 note). The cluster reveals that wisdom vocabulary in Scripture is structurally shaped by the direction of the soul's orientation. Session B should treat wisdom terms as directionally-sensitive throughout. |
| OBS-060-T2-001 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Neh 9:20 | The verse evidence reveals that giving (na.tan) is constitutive of God's character in multiple modes: God gives from covenantal faithfulness (OBS-060-018, OBS-060-035, OBS-060-036), from compassion responding to suffering (OBS-060-037, OBS-060-038), from anger in moral judgement (OBS-060-043), and from eschatological generosity (OBS-060-048, OBS-060-049). The anchor verses disclose that divine giving is never transactional — it is always the expression of an inner-being orientation. God gives land as the enactment of his covenantal word (Gen 12:7, Gen 15:18); God gives Spirit to instruct as the expression of his formative care (Neh 9:20); God gives eschatological transformation from depletion to praise (Isa 61:3). The character of God reflected in this registry is: a God whose inner commitments, compassions, and purposes necessarily express themselves as giving. Giving is the outward form of God's inner faithfulness. |
| OBS-060-T2-006 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Neh 9:20 | The evidence supports shared characteristic with asymmetric form. God gives as the originating and sovereign giver: his giving constitutes covenantal reality (Gen 15:18), endows inner capacities (Neh 9:20, Job 38:36), and enacts eschatological transformation (Isa 61:3). The human gives in responsive mode: Job releases what God gave (Job 1:21); the Servant offers himself in trust of divine vindication (Isa 50:6). The characteristic is genuinely shared — both God and the human give from inner-being orientation — but the asymmetry is structural: God gives primarily and the human gives responsively. Human giving is always downstream of divine giving, either responding to it (Job 1:21) or participating in its pattern (Isa 50:6). This is not exclusively a creaturely analogue — it is a shared characteristic with a directional structure: God → human → God. |
| OBS-060-T2-014 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | The characteristic is structurally complex — it has at minimum three constituent elements visible in the data. (1) An inner-being source: the orientation, commitment, or emotional state from which the giving proceeds (covenant faithfulness, compassion, anger, trust, obedience). (2) The act of giving itself: na.tan in its specific contextual configuration (bestowing, endowing, giving-over, self-offering, glory-ascribing). (3) The object given: what is transferred determines which dimension of faithfulness is expressed (land/promise → covenantal faithfulness; spirit/wisdom → formative endowment; glory → correspondence; praise/gladness → eschatological transformation). These three elements are always present in the classified verses and always in relationship — the source determines the act determines the object. This triadic structure (source → act → object) is the constituent architecture of faithfulness-as-giving in the data. |
| OBS-060-T2-015 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | Working description: Faithfulness in this registry is the inner-being quality by which a person (primarily God, secondarily the human image-bearer) enacts their inner commitments, orientations, and compassions through giving — giving that is always relational, directed, and sourced in an inner-being state rather than in external obligation. It operates at the intersection of moral character (covenant keeping), relational disposition (compassion enacted), cognition (endowing inner capacities), transformation (eschatological reversal of inner states), and divine-human correspondence (glory-ascription in its proper direction). The primary faculty engagement is the whole person acting from within: the heart (leb) as source (Hos 11:8), the soul (nephesh) as the seat of attachment that motivates giving (Jer 12:7), the spirit (ruach) as both the object of endowing-giving (Neh 9:20) and the faculty transformed by eschatological-giving (Isa 61:3). Impact on the person: receiving divine inner-being giving produces trust, praise, transformed inner states, and restored responsiveness; giving from inner orientation produces alignment with God and participation in the divine pattern of faithful action. |
| OBS-060-T2-019 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | The data reveals five distinct operational modes (OBS-060-025, OBS-060-051). (1) Performative giving: the act of giving constitutes a covenantal reality — "I will give this land" enacts the covenant (OBS-060-016, OBS-060-036). (2) Responsive giving: giving triggered by the recipient's inner condition — God gives in direct answer to suffering/need (OBS-060-037). (3) Endowing giving: giving that deposits inner-being capacity — Spirit, wisdom, understanding given directly to the inner life (OBS-060-040, OBS-060-041). (4) Judicial giving: giving as moral consequence — giving-over as the expression of inner moral response to faithlessness (OBS-060-042, OBS-060-043). (5) Transformative giving: giving that reverses inner-being states — replacing depletion with fullness, mourning with gladness (OBS-060-048, OBS-060-049, OBS-060-050). In the human direction, a sixth mode is also present: (6) Relinquishing giving: releasing what was received, recognising divine sovereignty over all given goods (OBS-060-044). |
| OBS-060-T2-020 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | Yes — significantly so. By direction: divine giving (6 of 7 groups) operates from sovereign initiative and covenantal obligation; human giving (1 group, 872-005) operates from responsive orientation and trust. The operational logic differs: God gives as the originating party; the human gives as the responsive, image-bearing party. By context: performative giving (covenant ratification, Gen 15:18) operates as a speech-act constituting reality; responsive giving (Gen 29:33) operates as compassionate provision; judicial giving (Hos 13:11) operates as moral consequence; transformative giving (Isa 61:3) operates as eschatological reversal. By constitutional level: endowing giving (Neh 9:20, Job 38:36) operates at the spirit/cognitive level, depositing inner-being capacity; self-giving (Isa 50:6) operates through the body as the expression of inner disposition; glory-giving (Isa 42:8, Jer 13:16) operates at the relational-correspondence level between inner-being orientation and its proper object. |
| OBS-060-T2-024 | VERSE_PATTERN | Neh 9:20 | Silence on immediate response is noted in three areas: (1) The endowing giving (Neh 9:20, Job 38:36 — group 872-003): no immediate human response to the giving of Spirit/wisdom/understanding is recorded in the anchor verses themselves. The effect (instruction, understanding) is named but not the experiential response. (2) The judicial giving-over (Hos 13:11, Jer 12:7 — group 872-004): no human response is recorded in the anchor verses — the human appears as the object of divine judicial giving, not as a responding agent. (3) The glory-withholding (Isa 42:8 — group 872-006): God's declaration that he will not give his glory to another records no human response. These silences are themselves informative: they suggest that endowing, judicial, and declarative giving are presented primarily from the divine side, with the human response either implicit or absent in the primary texts. |
| OBS-060-T2-025 | VERSE_PATTERN | Neh 9:20 | The sustained inner-being effects of divine giving in this registry cluster around three outcomes. (1) Identity formation: God's covenantal giving over generations produces a people who know themselves as recipients of promise — the land-giving texts (Gen 12:7 through Jer 17:4 in group 872-001) address communities who inherit a narrative of divine faithfulness accumulated across generations. (2) Inner-being capacity: the endowing giving (872-003) deposits Spirit, wisdom, and understanding that shape the person's cognitive and formative capacities over time (Neh 9:20, OBS-060-040). (3) Transformed inner states: the eschatological giving (872-007) produces lasting new inner-being orientations — not momentary experience but stable conditions: "oaks of righteousness" (Isa 61:3, OBS-060-049) names a durable identity, not a passing moment. |
| OBS-060-T2-026 | VERSE_PATTERN | Neh 9:20 | Sustained divine giving establishes: (a) Trust — the pattern of divine covenantal giving over generations grounds the expectation that God will continue to give as promised (group 872-001 corpus). (b) Praise orientation — Isa 61:3 names the endpoint as "garment of praise" (OBS-060-048) — sustained eschatological giving produces a stable praise-orientation as the characteristic inner-being state. (c) Responsiveness — Hos 2:15 ("she shall answer") names restored inner responsiveness as the sustained outcome (OBS-060-050). (d) Cognitive formation — sustained Spirit-giving and wisdom-giving produce shaped understanding and discernment over time (Neh 9:20, OBS-060-040). For the human giving outward: sustained practice of inner-being giving (self-giving, votive giving, glory-giving) produces the stable disposition that the group descriptions name — an inner orientation toward God and community that becomes characteristic of the person. |
| OBS-060-T2-027 | VERSE_PATTERN | Neh 9:20 | Yes — the sustained effect differs from the immediate response in the data. The immediate response is directional and affective: worship, blessing, naming (Gen 12:7, Gen 29:33, Job 1:21). The sustained effect is constitutional: identity as recipients of covenant promise, formed cognitive capacity, stable praise-orientation, restored responsiveness. The immediate response is an inner-being movement (toward God, in acknowledgement); the sustained effect is an inner-being state (who the person has become through accumulated receiving). The distinction is between the first response to receiving (immediate) and the person shaped by a pattern of receiving over time (sustained). This is consistent with the distinction between T1.5 and T1.6 being deliberately kept separate in the catalogue design — the two are structurally different and the data confirms they should not be conflated. |
| OBS-060-T2-033 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | Yes — four secondary dimensions are carried, each with substantial group-level evidence: (1) Relational Disposition (groups 872-002 and 872-005) — compassionate responsive giving (GOD) and self-giving as inner orientation (HUMAN). This dimension is the most quantitatively significant after Moral Character (30 + 19 = 49 relevant verses combined). (2) Cognition (group 872-003) — divine giving of inner-being capacities: Spirit, wisdom, understanding. (3) Divine-Human Correspondence (group 872-006) — glory-ascription in its proper directional structure. (4) Transformation (group 872-007) — eschatological reversal of inner states. These secondary dimensions do not compete with Moral Character as primary — they are distinct operational modes of the same characteristic, each revealing a different dimension of how inner-being giving functions in the biblical corpus. The DIM-60-SD001 pointer (OBS-060-033) identifies this dimensional breadth as a programme-level finding: na.tan illustrates that a single action-verb can carry the full breadth of inner-being dimensional engagement depending on context. |
| OBS-060-T2-034 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Neh 9:20 | Yes — in two distinct modes. (1) As the object of divine giving: Neh 9:20 (OBS-060-040) — "You gave your good Spirit to instruct them" — the ruach is given to the human inner life to form it cognitively. The Spirit is the content of God's endowing gift. (2) As the faculty transformed by eschatological giving: Isa 61:3 (OBS-060-048) — "garment of praise instead of a faint spirit (ruach kehah)" — the human spirit in its depleted state is the target of eschatological giving, resulting in transformation to a praise-orientation. In both cases ruach appears in explicit connection with na.tan: in Neh 9:20 as the object given; in Isa 61:3 as the faculty undergoing transformation through giving. |
| OBS-060-T2-035 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Neh 9:20 | The evidence is mixed. The endowing mode (872-003) is clearly spirit-level in its object: God gives the Spirit to the human inner life, and this giving deposits capacity at the spirit level (Neh 9:20, OBS-060-040). The eschatological mode (872-007) transforms the spirit directly (Isa 61:3). However, the other five groups do not show explicit spirit-level origination: the covenantal giving (872-001) and compassionate giving (872-002) operate from the divine heart and compassion (leb, rahamim — Hos 11:8, OBS-060-038), which are not identified as spirit-level in this data. The judicial giving (872-004) proceeds from divine anger/wrath — emotion-level, not spirit-level. Human self-giving (872-005) is not explicitly located at the spirit level in the anchor verses. The glory-giving (872-006) operates at the relational-correspondence level without explicit spirit-level identification. Assessment: spirit-level location is evidenced in two of seven groups; it is not the primary constitutional home of the characteristic across the full corpus. |
| OBS-060-T2-036 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Neh 9:20 | Where spirit-level location is evidenced, it reveals two things about the depth of this characteristic. (1) Giving at the spirit level is the deepest form of endowment: when God gives Spirit to instruct (Neh 9:20), the giving reaches into the foundational constitutional layer of the human person — forming the inner life from the inside out. This is not provision of external goods but constitution of inner capacity. (2) The faint spirit (ruach kehah, Isa 61:3) as the target of eschatological transformation reveals that the human spirit can be depleted — its vitality reduced — and that this depletion is the condition the transformative giving addresses. The eschatological gift therefore operates at the deepest level: renewing what is most fundamental to the person's inner-being vitality. Both instances confirm that spirit-level giving is the most constitutionally significant mode in the registry. |
| OBS-060-T2-042 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Neh 9:20 | Mind-location in Job 38:36 and group 872-003 engages the understanding and discernment aspect of the mind's function, not merely information-processing. God gives wisdom to the "inward parts" (tukhoth — hidden inner seat of understanding) and understanding to the "mind" (sekhwi). The cognitive function engaged is the capacity to perceive rightly — wisdom and understanding as the inner ability to process and interpret reality from within. The giving of wisdom/Spirit-to-instruct (Neh 9:20) also confirms this: the giving targets the formative-cognitive capacity, producing the ability to understand God's ways. Mind-location therefore reveals that this characteristic can deposit cognitive capacity: giving is not only material or relational — it can constitute the person's ability to know and discern. |
| OBS-060-T2-049 | SOMATIC_EVIDENCE | Neh 9:20 | The data provides one possible instance of generational consequence: the covenant-giving texts in group 872-001 (Gen 12:7, Gen 17:8, Gen 26:3) address Abraham and his offspring — the land-giving is explicitly intergenerational ("to you and to your offspring forever"). The covenantal giving carries across generations as a constitutive element of Israelite identity. Whether this constitutes a constitutional body-deposit in the technical sense the prompt intends (including DNA-level consequence) cannot be determined from this data. The covenantal transmission is relational-identity rather than somatic-constitutional in the evidence available. The Neh 9:20 Spirit-giving to the wilderness generation suggests each generation receives the endowing giving fresh rather than inheriting it somatically. No explicit body-level deposit is evidenced. |
| OBS-060-T2-050 | SOMATIC_EVIDENCE | Neh 9:20 | Supporting generational transmission (not body-deposit per se): the covenant-giving texts address Abraham's offspring across multiple generations — Gen 12:7, Gen 17:8, Gen 26:3, Neh 9:35-36. The land-giving is inherited identity, not re-given to each individual. Contradicting constitutional body-deposit: the Spirit-giving in Neh 9:20 is presented as a specific historical act to the wilderness generation, not as a permanent somatic inheritance. The eschatological giving in Isa 61:3 and Hos 2:15 is presented as new initiative, not as retrieval of an inherited deposit. The evidence does not support a constitutional body deposit in the somatic sense; what transmits generationally is covenantal identity and promise, which is relational-narratival rather than biological. |
| OBS-060-T2-052 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Neh 9:20 | The data shows multiple origins depending on subject and mode. (1) For divine giving: the characteristic originates from within God's own inner-being — from his covenantal commitment (heart-faithfulness, Neh 9:8), compassion (rahamim, leb, Hos 11:8), and moral character. It is self-generated in God. (2) For the endowing mode: inner-being capacities (wisdom, understanding, Spirit) are bestowed by God upon the human — received from outside, originating in divine initiative. (3) For human self-giving (Job 1:21, Isa 50:6): the inner orientation from which giving proceeds appears to originate within the person (trust, acceptance of divine sovereignty) but is itself formed by received divine giving — a secondary origin. (4) The covenantal-identity dimension carries generationally as received inheritance — the offspring inherits the promise and identity constituted by prior divine giving. |
| OBS-060-T2-053 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Neh 9:20 | The origin is multiple — and the multiplicity is structured by subject and mode (Q&A-062). For God: singular internal origin (divine inner-being character). For the human: multiple. The inner orientation that grounds human giving is received (formed by prior divine giving, covenant relationship, Spirit-endowment) — but it is expressed as though from within (Job's response is his own inner act, not a mechanically caused output). The multiplicity of origin reflects the characteristic's dual structure as disposition-in-action: the disposition's origin may be received while the act's origin is genuinely internal to the person. The data does not collapse this into a single origin. |
| OBS-060-T2-054 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Neh 9:20 | Yes — the origin shifts across contexts as follows. In covenantal giving contexts (872-001): origin is divine internal character, unconditional and self-generated. In responsive giving (872-002): origin is divine compassion triggered by the human's presented need — the origin is still internal to God but responsive to external condition. In endowing giving (872-003): origin is divine initiative depositing capacity in the human — giving constitutes the human's inner capacity from the outside. In judicial giving-over (872-004): origin is divine moral response to human faithlessness — external condition (human behaviour) shapes the mode of divine giving. In human self-giving (872-005): origin is the human's inner trust and acceptance, formed by but distinct from received divine giving. The characteristic's origin is not static — it is context-sensitive across the seven groups, reflecting the full range of divine-human interaction. |
| OBS-060-T2-055 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Neh 9:20 | Yes — constitutional movement is evidenced in multiple directions across the groups. (1) Spirit → expression: Neh 9:20 — God gives Spirit to instruct; the Spirit-giving operates at the spirit level and produces cognitive-formational capacity at the soul/mind level. Spirit → mind movement. (2) Inner → body: Isa 50:6 — inner disposition (trust, covenantal obedience) expressed through bodily surfaces (back, cheeks, face). Inner → body movement. (3) Body → inner (divine constitution): Isa 42:5 — God gives breath (neshamah) to constitute bodily life; the bodily breath-gift underlies the capacity for inner-being existence. (4) Eschatological: Isa 61:3 — the faint spirit receives a garment of praise; the spirit-level depletion is transformed into an embodied-inner orientation (garment = outer expression of inner state). Spirit ↔ body movement in the eschatological register. |
| OBS-060-T2-056 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Neh 9:20 | Two distinct movement patterns are evidenced. (1) The endowing pattern: divine giving moves from spirit level downward and outward — Spirit given → understanding formed in the hidden inner parts → person equipped to instruct/act. The sequence is constitutive: each level is enabled by the gift at the deeper level. (2) The expressive pattern: inner orientation (trust, covenantal commitment) moves from inner to outer — inner disposition → bodily act (Isa 50:6) or visible response (altar-building, Gen 12:7) or praise (Job 1:21). The sequence is expressive: the inner is prior, the outer is derivative. (3) The eschatological pattern: external divine act transforms internal state — God gives eschatologically → faint spirit becomes praising spirit → outer garment of praise expresses transformed inner state. The sequence is transformative: outside-in → inside renewed → inside-out again. These three patterns (constitutive, expressive, transformative) represent the full movement architecture of the characteristic. |
| OBS-060-T2-057 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | The hearing faculty is explicitly engaged in two instances. Gen 29:33 (OBS-060-037): "the Lord has heard (shama) that I am hated" — divine hearing is the perceptive act that precedes and grounds the compassionate giving. The perception of inner suffering is the precondition of the responsive giving. Neh 9:20 (OBS-060-040): God gives Spirit "to instruct them" — the Spirit-giving produces the capacity to hear/receive instruction, engaging the perceptive-receptive dimension at the cognitive-formative level. For the human direction: Jer 13:16 (OBS-060-047) — "before your feet stumble... while you look for light" — visual perception (looking for light) is present in the failure-to-give-glory context, though as the condition of the person who has not given glory rather than as a faculty engaged by giving itself. Hearing is the primary perceptive faculty engaged. |
| OBS-060-T2-058 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | Receiving divine inner-being giving enables perception in two ways. (1) The Spirit-giving (Neh 9:20) produces the capacity to receive instruction — the Spirit opens the inner ear/mind to formative input. The giving of Spirit is itself a perceptive-enabling gift. (2) The failure to give glory to God (Jer 13:16, OBS-060-047) impairs perception: "while you look for light he turns it into gloom." The non-giving of glory produces perceptive disorder — the light-seeking faculty encounters only darkness. These together show the characteristic enabling perception when received rightly and impairing it when the relational correspondence breaks down (failure to give glory). The Spirit-endowment is the most direct perceptive-enabling instance. |
| OBS-060-T2-059 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | The pattern reveals that this characteristic has a relational-perceptive architecture: inner-being giving is grounded in prior perception (God hears suffering before giving; Spirit-endowment enables the human to hear instruction) and it shapes subsequent perception (failure to give glory produces perceptive darkening; receiving eschatological giving restores responsiveness — Hos 2:15, OBS-060-050). The characteristic is not perception itself, but it operates through perception as both its precondition and its consequence. This reveals that inner-being giving is not blind action — it is responsive, attentive, and formative of the recipient's perceptive capacity. |
| OBS-060-T2-060 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | Yes — most directly in group 872-003. Neh 9:20 (OBS-060-040): Spirit given to instruct (sakal — to give insight, understand prudently). Job 38:36 (OBS-060-041): wisdom given to the inward parts, understanding to the mind. The cognitive faculty is the direct target of endowing giving in this group: the giving of wisdom and understanding constitutes cognitive capacity in the human. This is the most direct engagement of the cognitive faculty in the registry. Cognition is not merely present as a background faculty — it is the specific object of giving in group 872-003, confirming the Cognition dimension assignment for that group (OBS-060-020). |
| OBS-060-T2-061 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | It enables cognition in its endowing mode (Neh 9:20, Job 38:36) — the giving of Spirit, wisdom, and understanding constitutes the cognitive capacity that the person then possesses. It does not bypass or impair cognition in any evidenced instance. In the failure direction: Jer 13:16 shows that the failure to give glory produces darkening and stumbling — this is perceptive more than cognitive, but the loss of inner-being orientation (darkening) may imply cognitive disorder as well. The primary engagement is enabling: this characteristic, in its endowing mode, is constitutive of cognitive capacity. |
| OBS-060-T2-062 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | The pattern reveals that this characteristic has a constitutive relationship with cognition in its endowing mode: inner-being giving can literally create cognitive capacity where it did not previously exist — or could not sustain itself. Job 38:36 frames this as divine prerogative ("who has given...?") — confirming that human cognition at its deepest level is not self-generated but received. The giving that constitutes cognition is therefore the most fundamental form of the characteristic: not giving something the person might use, but giving the capacity to use anything. This reveals that inner-being giving, at its deepest, is constitutive rather than supplementary. |
| OBS-060-T2-064 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | The characteristic deepens memory in its covenantal mode: the accumulated record of divine giving across generations forms the memory-structure of Israel's inner-being identity. Each new giving adds to the memory of prior giving, deepening the narrative foundation of trust. Neh 9 (the long Levitical prayer) is itself a memory-act: reciting the history of divine giving as the basis of present covenantal renewal. Hos 2:15 invokes Exodus memory as the ground of eschatological hope — the memory of what God gave before shapes the expectation of what he will give again. The characteristic does not impair memory in any evidenced instance; it is constitutively tied to the faithfulness of remembered giving. |
| OBS-060-T2-072 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | Yes — agency is the primary external form of this characteristic. Every instance of na.tan in the inner-being register is an act — something is given, transferred, offered. Agency is intrinsic: the giving requires an agent who initiates and performs the transfer. In the divine direction: God acts to give land (Gen 12:7), Spirit (Neh 9:20), transformative gifts (Isa 61:3) — all are divine initiatives, sovereign acts of divine agency. In the human direction: Job acts (blesses — Job 1:21), the Servant acts (gives his back — Isa 50:6), humans are commanded to act (give glory — Jer 13:16). The characteristic is not merely a state or disposition — it is always an acted-out disposition. Agency is the faculty through which the inner-being orientation becomes external and real. |
| OBS-060-T2-073 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | The endowing giving (872-003) enables agency: Spirit-giving enables the capacity to instruct (Neh 9:20 — sakal). The giving of the tongue of the taught (Isa 50:4) enables the agentive act of sustaining the weary. The cognitive gifts (wisdom, understanding — Job 38:36) enable discerning action. In the eschatological mode: the transformation from faint spirit to garment of praise (Isa 61:3) restores the inner-being vitality that makes agentive participation possible — the depressed, depleted inner life cannot give glory; the restored spirit can and does. The characteristic enables and restores agency rather than bypassing it. The judicial giving-over (872-004) might be read as impairing the recipient's agency — being delivered into enemies' hands removes freedom of action — but this is consequence, not direct engagement of the agency faculty. |
| OBS-060-T2-074 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | Agency is not incidental to the characteristic — it is constitutive. The disposition-in-action structure (Q&A-016) requires agency: the inner-being orientation only becomes faithfulness when it produces action. The pattern reveals that this characteristic exists at the intersection of inner orientation and outward act — agency is the bridge. The endowing giving further reveals that where agency is depleted or absent (faint spirit, darkened perception), divine giving restores it. This implies that the characteristic has a self-sustaining property in the divine direction: God's giving enables the human capacity to respond and give in turn, which sustains the relational dynamic. |
| OBS-060-T2-076 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | The endowing giving (872-003) enables moral evaluation: wisdom and understanding given to the inward parts (Job 38:36) constitute the cognitive capacity for right moral discernment. Spirit given to instruct (Neh 9:20) forms the moral-cognitive faculty. The eschatological giving restores the inner-being orientation from which moral evaluation flows — "oaks of righteousness" (Isa 61:3) names a stable moral identity restored by the giving. Covenantal giving (872-001) deepens moral evaluation by grounding the person's identity in the narrative of divine faithfulness — the memory of God's reliable giving provides the moral framework against which actions are assessed. In the failure direction: the judicial giving-over (872-004) may represent a consequence of failed moral evaluation (Israel's failure to assess rightly what faithfulness required), though the data is more explicit about the consequence than the prior failure. |
| OBS-060-T2-077 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | The pattern reveals that this characteristic operates within and upon the moral evaluation faculty in two directions: it is the object of moral evaluation (Jer 17:10 — God gives according to moral assessment of deeds) and it is the source of moral evaluative capacity (the endowing of wisdom and Spirit constitutes the faculty that evaluates). Giving-as-faithfulness therefore has a reflexive relationship with moral evaluation: it both expresses moral judgement (in the judicial mode) and constitutes the capacity for moral judgement (in the endowing mode). This reveals that the moral character dimension of the registry is not merely descriptive — inner-being giving is tied to the structure of moral reality itself. |
| OBS-060-T2-079 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | The Spirit-giving (Neh 9:20) and prophetic warning (Neh 9:30) in the endowing and covenantal modes can be read as conscience-forming and conscience-engaging: God's Spirit and prophets address the inner moral witness. The refusal to give ear to prophetic warning represents a suppression of conscience-input that leads to judicial giving-over. In this sense the characteristic (Spirit-giving, warning-giving) enables conscience, and its rejection impairs it. The eschatological giving that produces "oaks of righteousness" (Isa 61:3) would imply a restored moral inner life including conscience. Gap: these are inferences from the data, not direct statements. The data is richer on moral evaluation (T3.8) than on conscience specifically. |
| OBS-060-T2-080 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | The pattern reveals that this characteristic engages conscience indirectly rather than directly: it is the framework (Spirit-giving, prophetic warning-giving, covenantal narrative) within which conscience is formed and addressed, rather than conscience itself. The judicial giving-over is the consequence of rejected conscience-engagement — when divine warning is not received, the trajectory moves toward giving-over. This reveals that inner-being giving is conscience-forming in its endowing and covenantal modes: it builds the inner moral environment within which conscience can function rightly. The characteristic is therefore not primarily a conscience-engaging act but a conscience-constituting one — the giving that forms the moral inner life from which conscience operates. |
| OBS-060-T2-082 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | The characteristic deepens conscientiousness in its endowing mode (Spirit and wisdom given constitute the moral-cognitive faculties that conscientiousness draws on) and demands it in its volitional-expressive mode (Job's blessing, the Servant's self-offering, the glory-imperative all require and express full conscientiousness). Covenantal narrative (Neh 9) — the memory of divine giving — deepens conscientiousness by grounding it in a received moral-relational framework. The judicial giving-over (872-004) is in one sense the consequence of failed conscientiousness: the failure to integrate moral awareness, will, and action in covenant faithfulness results in divine moral response. The characteristic therefore both demands and enables conscientiousness — it does not bypass it. |
| OBS-060-T2-085 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | The characteristic enables and restores relational capacity. The eschatological giving (Hos 2:15, OBS-060-050) directly restores relational responsiveness: "she shall answer" — the restored capacity to respond to God is the measure of the transformation. Relational capacity was lost (Israel had abandoned God); the eschatological giving restores it. The Spirit-giving (Neh 9:20) enables the formative capacity that undergirds relational wisdom. The covenantal giving (872-001) sustains the relational framework within which genuine connection is possible: the covenant is the relational structure that God's giving establishes and maintains. The characteristic does not impair relational capacity in any evidenced instance; where the relational correspondence breaks (glory withheld from God), the impairment is on the human side, not produced by the characteristic itself. |
| OBS-060-T2-087 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Neh 9:20 | Yes — extensively and across five of seven groups. God gives covenantally toward Israel (872-001: land, promise, blessing — Gen 12:7, Gen 15:18); compassionately toward those in need (872-002: responsive giving to suffering — Gen 29:33, Hos 11:8 withheld giving-over); formatively toward the inner life (872-003: Spirit, wisdom, understanding — Neh 9:20, Job 38:36); judicially toward the faithless (872-004: giving-over as moral consequence — Hos 13:11, Jer 12:7); and transformatively toward the mourning (872-007: ashes → beauty, faint spirit → praise — Isa 61:3, Hos 2:15). Every mode of God-to-human giving is evidenced in the anchor verses. The God-to-human direction is the dominant directional structure of the registry (6 of 7 groups, OBS-060-017). |
| OBS-060-T2-088 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Neh 9:20 | The data reveals four distinct bases operating across the groups: (1) Covenantal (872-001): God gives because the covenant is in force — the giving enacts prior covenantal commitment, regardless of immediate merit. Gen 15:18 — the covenant ratification is the basis; the giving follows from it. Mal 2:5 — "my covenant with him was one of life and peace, and I gave them to him" — the giving is covenantal through and through. (2) Responsive (872-002): God gives in response to presented inner need — Leah's condition triggers divine hearing and giving (Gen 29:33). The basis is the creature's condition, perceived by divine attentiveness. (3) Formative (872-003): God gives Spirit/wisdom as constitutive endowment — the basis appears to be divine formative purpose for the person, not contingent on their current state. (4) Moral-consequential (872-004): God gives-over as moral response to faithlessness — the basis is moral consistency: the covenant is executed in both directions. (5) Eschatological-grace (872-007): God gives to the mourning without stated precondition — grace and eschatological initiative are the basis. No single basis covers all modes; the characteristic operates across a spectrum from pure grace to moral consequence. |
| OBS-060-T2-089 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Neh 9:20 | God's giving reveals a complex of inner-being dispositions toward the human person. (1) Covenantal commitment: God's giving of land and promise over generations reveals that his orientation toward Abraham and his offspring is one of binding, persistent faithfulness — a commitment that does not lapse with time or circumstance. (2) Attentive compassion: God hears Leah's inner condition and gives in response — his orientation is one of attentiveness to the inner suffering of the creature, not detachment. (3) Formative care: God gives Spirit and wisdom to equip the person for formation and service — his orientation is developmental, not merely transactional. (4) Moral seriousness: God gives-over in response to faithlessness — his orientation includes moral consistency and covenantal accountability. He is not sentimentally lenient. (5) Hos 11:8 holds all of these in tension: the heart that recoils in compassion is also the heart that had determined judgement — God's orientation is a complex of justice and compassion held together by sovereign grace. |
| OBS-060-T2-096 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | Yes — every group with God as subject shows the human receiving divine inner-being giving. Reception operates differently by mode: (1) Covenantal reception (872-001): the human receives the promise/land as participant in an inherited covenant — reception is identity-constituting. Abram receives the promise and immediately responds with worship (Gen 12:7, OBS-060-035). (2) Compassionate reception (872-002): Leah receives a child in direct response to her inner suffering — reception is responsive and experiential (Gen 29:33, OBS-060-037). (3) Formative reception (872-003): the human receives Spirit, wisdom, and understanding — reception is constitutive, forming inner capacity. (4) Eschatological reception (872-007): those who mourn receive beauty/gladness/praise — reception is transformative, replacing depleted inner states with new ones. The mode of reception corresponds directly to the mode of giving: the characteristic is received as it is given. |
| OBS-060-T2-108 | VERSE_PATTERN | Neh 9:20 | The evidence shows three distinct mechanisms operating across the groups. (1) Encounter/event: the covenantal giving (Gen 12:7, Gen 15:18) is event-based — a specific divine appearance and speech-act constitutes the giving and its transformative effect. The encounter is sudden and definitional. (2) Gradual formation: the endowing mode (Neh 9:20 — Spirit given to instruct) implies sustained formative engagement — Spirit-guided instruction shapes the inner-cognitive life over time. This is gradual formation rather than sudden transformation. (3) Eschatological reversal: Isa 61:3 and Hos 2:15 operate through direct divine act — the giving replaces the prior state without a formative process. The mechanism is sovereign initiative producing immediate inner-being change. Hosea 11:8 (the heart recoiling) shows a divine inner-being mechanism of self-determined restraint — not discipline or encounter but the assertion of compassion over logic. |
| OBS-060-T2-109 | VERSE_PATTERN | Neh 9:20 | Yes — the mechanisms are context-specific and correspond directly to the group structure. Covenantal contexts (872-001): encounter/event mechanism — the divine appearance and speech-act is the mechanism. Endowing contexts (872-003): gradual formation — Spirit and wisdom work over time through instruction and application. Eschatological contexts (872-007): sovereign reversal — no process, direct divine initiative replacing one state with another. Judicial contexts (872-004): moral consequence — the mechanism is the enacted consequence of prior moral trajectory, not a transformative encounter. The data confirms that the mechanism varies by mode and cannot be reduced to a single transformative pathway. The characteristic's dimensional breadth (T1.8.3, Q&A-036) is reflected in its mechanistic plurality. |
| OBS-060-T2-112 | VERSE_PATTERN | Neh 9:20 | Yes — primarily through the endowing mode (872-003). The Spirit given to instruct (Neh 9:20) is a formative gift: its purpose is to shape the inner-cognitive-moral life over time, equipping the person for faithful walk. The wisdom given to the inward parts (Job 38:36) constitutes the deep cognitive capacity from which discerning character grows. The covenantal giving (872-001) participates in the formation of covenantal identity across generations — the Nehemiah corpus (Neh 9) is explicitly a rehearsal of divine giving as the ground of renewed covenantal commitment: the memory of God's giving shapes the present community's character orientation. The eschatological transformation ("oaks of righteousness," Isa 61:3) names the endpoint of the formation arc — the person shaped by divine giving reaches stable moral identity. |
| OBS-060-T2-113 | VERSE_PATTERN | Neh 9:20 | The characteristic plays two roles in the formation arc. (1) Constitutive: the endowing giving deposits the capacities (Spirit, wisdom, understanding) that make formation possible. Formation cannot proceed without the constitutive inner-being endowment — this is the starting gift. (2) Sustaining: the covenantal giving across generations sustains the community's formation context — the memory of divine faithfulness is the sustained environment within which individual and communal character is shaped. The Neh 9 corpus illustrates this: Israel's formation is narrated as the history of divine giving (bread, water, Spirit, law, land) — the long arc of formation is the long arc of received giving. The giving is not incidental to formation; it is the ongoing substance of it. The person shaped by received giving is the person who has been formed by the pattern of divine faithfulness over time. |
| OBS-060-T2-115 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Neh 9:20 | The present experience of covenantal giving anticipates: (1) Completeness — every partial giving (land, provision, endowment) is oriented toward the fullness of the eschatological inheritance where the giving is total and permanent. The "everlasting covenant" and "everlasting name" of the Isaian texts name a giving that does not reserve or withhold. (2) Inner-being fullness — the eschatological giving transforms the inner person completely: faint spirit to praise, mourning to gladness, ashes to beauty. The present partial experience of inner renewal (Spirit-giving for instruction, compassionate provision for need) anticipates a state where no inner-being faculty remains in depletion. (3) Communal identity — "oaks of righteousness... the planting of the Lord" is communal: the present individual experiences of receiving divine giving anticipate a communal eschatological state of shared righteous identity. The present experience is partial, individual, and provisional; the future fullness is total, communal, and permanent. |
| OBS-060-T2-121 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Neh 9:20 | Three produced characteristics are evidenced. (1) Worship/praise (R121, R176): divine covenantal giving produces worship (Gen 12:7 — altar-building; Isa 61:3 — garment of praise; Job 1:21 — "blessed be the name of the Lord"). The mechanism is responsive: received giving evokes worship as the proper inner-being response. (2) Hope (R078): eschatological giving produces hope — Hos 2:15 transforms the Valley of Achor into a door of hope. The mechanism is transformative: the act of giving replaces shame with hope. (3) Understanding/wisdom: endowing giving (Neh 9:20, Job 38:36) produces cognitive capacity — the mechanism is constitutive, depositing what was not there. Each produced characteristic is produced by a distinct mode: responsive → worship; transformative → hope; constitutive → wisdom. |
| OBS-060-T2-132 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Neh 9:20 | R060 carries five confirmed dimensions (T1.8): Moral Character (primary), Relational Disposition, Cognition, Transformation, Divine-Human Correspondence. All five are programme dimensions shared by multiple other registries. The most significant overlaps by co-occurrence signal: (1) Moral Character — shared with R067 (goodness), R090 (innocence), R015 (boastfulness — its opposite), R034 (covenant). (2) Relational Disposition — shared with R099 (kindness/hesed), R103 (love), R023 (compassion), R111 (mercy). (3) Cognition — shared with R166 (understanding), which co-occurs at 6 shared verses confirming the endowing-giving/understanding connection. (4) Transformation — shared with R071 (grief, via Isa 61:3 anchor), R151 (sorrow), R078 (hope). (5) DHC — shared with R176 (worship), R121 (praise), which co-occur at 6 and 5 shared verses respectively. All five dimensions show confirmed co-occurrence overlap with other registries. |
| OBS-060-T2-143 | VERSE_ANNOTATION | Neh 9:20 | The anchor verse corpus spans four literary forms. (1) Narrative (Gen 12:7, Gen 15:18, Gen 29:33, Job 1:21): responsible interpretation requires attention to plot and character — the giving is embedded in a specific relational and historical moment. (2) Prophecy (Isa 61:3, Isa 50:6, Hos 11:8, Hos 13:11, Jer 12:7, Jer 13:16): responsible interpretation requires attention to rhetorical address and the distinction between divine speech and prophetic interpretation. The first-person divine speeches ("I gave," "How can I give you up") require particular care about the nature of divine inner-being language. (3) Wisdom/divine speech (Job 38:36, Job 1:21): responsible interpretation requires attention to the dramatic frame — Job's blessing is within a frame where Satan has made a claim, and God's question from the whirlwind is within a legal disputation. (4) Liturgical/prayer (Neh 9:20): responsible interpretation requires attention to the retrospective narrative form — this is communal confession of divine faithfulness, not direct reportage. The diversity of literary forms means no single interpretive lens covers the whole corpus. |
| OBS-060-T2-150 | OBSERVATION | Neh 9:20 | Two points of divergence are analytically significant. (1) Virtue ethics assumes human agency as the primary locus of virtue formation — the person forms virtue through practice. The verse evidence places divine giving as the constitutive source of inner-being capacity (Spirit-giving, wisdom-given) — the human does not form virtue through unassisted practice but receives the constitutive capacities through divine giving. The divergence reveals: biblical faithfulness cannot be accounted for purely through a human-centred virtue framework; the received character of inner-being capacity is primary. (2) Attachment theory operates within dyadic (two-person) relational structures. Hos 11:8 shows divine inner-being dynamics that exceed the dyadic — God's compassion overriding God's judicial logic is an intra-divine resolution, not an interpersonal negotiation. The divergence reveals: the inner-being deliberation of God described in Hos 11:8 has no direct human analogue in attachment theory — it describes a complexity of divine inner-being that is qualitatively different from human relational experience. |
