<a id="t-G6063"></a>
### G6063 oida — to know (`mti_id=963`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G6063` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R100 knowledge · Legacy C-cluster: C02
3. Sub-group: `M15-C` (Knowledge as inner content and covenantal knowing)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 286
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=963` · `md_version=2` · `vc_status=vc_completed`
6. Mounce short-def: to know, understand, perceive

**Verses:** 56 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (5)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1495 | 963-001 | Settled inner conviction orienting moral, spiritual, and relational life toward God |  |
| 1496 | 963-002 | Self-knowledge of inner conflict — awareness of flesh, sin, and spiritual limitation |  |
| 1497 | 963-003 | Identity-shaping knowledge — who the person is in Christ, transforming inner orientation |  |
| 1498 | 963-004 | God's or another's knowledge of the inner person — hearts, thoughts, sincerity |  |
| 1499 | 963-005 | Knowledge divorced from love — spiritually empty inner-life capacity |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (10 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-97-002 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Rom 5:3 | Three groups name joy that persists through or is intensified by suffering: 364-001 (salad — defiant exultation), 381-002 (chairo — paradoxical joy in tribulation), and 3859-001 in Registry 42 (hēdista — most gladly in weakness). This convergent cross-term pattern names a distinct inner-being mode. Session B should examine joy-through-suffering as a structural feature rather than an anomaly. |
| DIM-43-003 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Rom 7:18 | Group 513-001 names the divided inner will — the person wills good but does what they do not will (Romans 7 pattern). This is analytically foundational for understanding desire's relationship to action. Session B should give this sustained attention as a key inner-being mechanism. |
| DIM-072-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Rom 8:26 | Group 899-001 (G4726 stenagmos, the Spirit's inarticulate groaning in Romans 8): assigned Spiritual/God-ward. This is a pneumatological dimension unique in the C05 cluster — the Spirit interceding within the inner being through groaning that is beyond the person's own capacity for articulation. Session B should examine this group as a distinctive case of the inner being as the locus of the Spirit's intercessory activity. |
| OBS-103-T2-092 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Cor 13:2 | Three surprising co-occurrences: (a) **Strength (R187, 105) and authority (R197, 69)** — love and power as the second and third highest co-occurrence registers. This is analytically unexpected — strength and authority are C20 cluster characteristics, not C17. The data suggests that love and power are not merely adjacent but structurally co-present in biblical contexts. The 1Cor 13 framework places love above gifts of power ('tongues of angels, gifts of prophecy') — the comparison implies they occupy the same evidential terrain. SD-026 (already raised) addresses this. (b) **Despair (R044, 55)** — despair appearing in the top 10 co-occurrences with love. The lament/petition pattern (despair crying to love for rescue) generates this co-occurrence. Love and despair are structurally linked as the inner state that appeals to love and the love that responds to despair. (c) **Guilt (R073, 32)** — less surprising given SD-028 (forgiveness-love chain) but notable that guilt appears in the top 25 — love and guilt are structurally related at the verse level through the forgiveness-love causal chain. |
| OBS-103-T2-155 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | 1Cor 13:2 | The agency engagement pattern reveals that love is the programme's primary motivating ground for ethical action. The pattern across the anchor verse corpus is consistent: love generates acts that exceed what duty, fear, or self-interest could reliably produce. Joh 15:13 names the limit: 'greater love has no one than this.' The greatest act of agency (dying for another) is grounded in and produced by love — not by command or duty alone. This reveals love's relationship to the rest of the ethical life: love is not simply an inner feeling that happens to produce acts. It is the constitutive motivational source from which the full range of agentive expression flows. The ethical architecture implied: acts without love are inadequate (1Cor 13:1-3 — tongues, prophecy, self-sacrifice without love = nothing); acts from love are constitutively different because their source is different. |
| OBS-062-T2-215 | OBSERVATION | 2Cor 1:7 | Yes — deeply. 2Cor 1:7: "as you share in our sufferings, you will also share in our comfort." Phili 3:10: the fellowship of Christ's sufferings. Hope (Ecc 9:4). The affective dimension of fellowship is constitutive — sharing in the fellowship object includes sharing in its affective reality. OBS-062-031, OBS-062-055, OBS-062-045. |
| OBS-062-T2-216 | OBSERVATION | 2Cor 1:7 | Fellowship deepens affect — shared suffering produces shared comfort (2Cor 1:7); shared hope (Ecc 9:4) is the affective product of existential fellowship. The affective deepening is proportionate: "as you share in our sufferings, so also you share in our comfort" — the depth of shared suffering determines the depth of shared comfort. OBS-062-031. |
| OBS-062-T2-242 | OBSERVATION | 2Cor 1:7 | Yes — richly evidenced. Ecc 4:10 (the friend who lifts when one falls), Psa 119:63 (companion of God-fearers as community formation), Mal 2:14 (covenant companion), Isa 1:23 (companion of thieves as moral-formation warning), 2Cor 1:7 (sharing comfort proportionate to sharing suffering). OBS-062-050, OBS-062-054, OBS-062-057, OBS-062-031. |
| OBS-062-T2-245 | OBSERVATION | 2Cor 1:7 | Yes — Ecc 4:10 ("woe to him who is alone when he falls — there is no companion to lift him") presents the person who has not received the fellowship of a companion as the structurally vulnerable condition. 2Cor 1:7 — the Corinthians receive the comfort that is proportionate to their sharing in the sufferings. OBS-062-050, OBS-062-031. |
| OBS-062-T2-260 | OBSERVATION | 2Cor 1:7 | Yes — the fellowship-through-suffering mode is central. Phili 3:10: "that I may know him and the power of his resurrection and the fellowship of his sufferings, becoming like him in his death." Isa 53:5: wounds as vehicle of healing-fellowship. 2Cor 1:7: shared suffering enabling shared comfort. Pro 20:30: strokes producing inner cleansing. OBS-062-055, OBS-062-032, OBS-062-031, OBS-062-039. |

<a id="t-G4678"></a>
### G4678 sofia — wisdom (`mti_id=532`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G4678` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R174 wisdom · Legacy C-cluster: C02
3. Sub-group: `M15-A` (Wisdom as holistic inner character and orientation)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 207
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=532` · `md_version=2` · `vc_status=vc_completed`
6. Mounce short-def: wisdom (either secular or divine). Christ is called "the wisdom of God" in <ref='1Co.1.24, 30'>1Co 1:24, 30</ref>. On the basis of the OT, wisdom can be personified

**Verses:** 49 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (5)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2466 | 4458-001 | sofia and sofos in 1 Corinthians 1–3, and in parallel passages, name human wisdom as a category that the cross overturns. God hides things from the wise; the wisdom of the world is folly with God. This is not wisdom inverted by the person claiming it (A-4) but wisdom as a category that stands in structural opposition to the wisdom of God. |  |
| 2462 | 532-001 | Term names wisdom as a divine attribute — the infinite and incomprehensible understanding belonging to God alone |  |
| 2463 | 532-002 | Wisdom as a gift — God gives it, persons receive it, it enables what the recipient could not produce from themselves. The giving and receiving is the characteristic dynamic of this group. | Revised during Dimension Review Phase C/anchor consultation: description corrected — wisdom 532-002 |
| 2464 | 532-003 | sofia naming wisdom as something visible in a particular person — their teaching, their speech, their deeds, their growth. This is not wisdom in the abstract but wisdom that can be seen and marvelled at in someone. |  |
| 2476 | 6696-001 | Term names wisdom as a divine attribute — belonging to God and bestowed by him as an inner capacity enabling understanding of mysteries |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (1)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-174-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Pro 1:4; Gen 3:1; Pro 3:7; Isa 52:13 | Registry 174 (wisdom) contains multiple directionally-determined term pairs: (1) a.rum (H6175) and or.mah (H6195) — prudence as cognitive faculty vs. craftiness as moral orientation toward deception (Sub-pattern B); (2) sofos/sofia (G4680/G4678) — wisdom toward God (4458-001, Moral/Conscience character type) vs. wisdom turned inward as self-deception (4458-002, Moral/Conscience distortion); (3) cha.kham (H2449/H2450) — wisdom from fear of Lord (528-003, Spiritual/God-ward) vs. wisdom self-attributed (528-003 note). The cluster reveals that wisdom vocabulary in Scripture is structurally shaped by the direction of the soul's orientation. Session B should treat wisdom terms as directionally-sensitive throughout. |

**Findings — via anchor verse (4 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-111-T2-123 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Jam 3:17 | Both directions are evidenced: (1) Mercy as constituent of wisdom: Jam 3:17 (mercy is named as a component of wisdom from above — OBS-111-025; SP-111-017). (2) Atonement as constituent of mercy: the atonement vocabulary is not a separate characteristic but the structural mechanism through which mercy operates — atonement is mercy's embodied mechanism (DIM-111-001, OBS-111-075). (3) Holiness as mercy's companion: the holiness-mercy axis within R111 (OBS-111-050, OBS-111-051) suggests holiness and mercy are constitutively related rather than external to each other. *New finding: mercy has both constituent status within wisdom (Jam 3:17) and constituent elements within itself (atonement as its mechanism, compassion as its motivation, holiness as its relational character).* OBS-111-025, OBS-111-050, OBS-111-075. |
| OBS-117-T2-095 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 3:17 | The data establishes several inner conditions for genuine peace-giving: (1) Having received peace: the apostolic blessing (Rom 1:7) is the outflow of the apostle's own experience of grace-and-peace from God; one extends what one has received — Joh 14:27's "my peace I give to you" is the paradigm: Christ gives what he IS; (2) Authentic inner orientation toward peace: Psa 120:7 ("I am for peace") — the person who genuinely extends peace is one whose fundamental orientation IS peace; the one whose heart is toward war cannot genuinely extend peace (Psa 28:3 — speaking peace while evil is in the heart is its corruption); (3) Seeking the other's welfare: Jer 29:7 — the extension of peace to the exile city requires an inner orientation of care for those who might be regarded as enemies; (4) Wisdom: G1516 eirēnikos — peaceableness is the fruit of heavenly wisdom (Jam 3:17) — wisdom is the inner condition enabling peaceful extension. |
| OBS-117-T2-140 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 3:17 | Yes — multiple person-type terms: (1) Ish sha.lom (Psa 37:37 — "man of peace") — the person whose character is peace; the construct chain ish + sha.lom makes peace the defining characteristic of the person; (2) Ani sha.lom (Psa 120:7 — "I am for peace/I am peace") — the most concentrated self-designation; sha.lom as personal identity not merely possession; (3) Anshei sha.lom (Jer 20:10, 38:22 — "men of sha.lom") — plural person-type term; men defined by their sha.lom-bond; used notably in the betrayal context; (4) G1516 eirēnikos — the peaceable person, one whose character is eirēnē (Jam 3:17, Heb 12:11); (5) Eirēnopoios (G1518 — "peacemaker," Mat 5:9) — owned by R103 but in the peace vocabulary's scope: the person whose characteristic act is making peace. The person-type vocabulary is rich: the registry defines not only the characteristic but the person whose nature is the characteristic. |
| OBS-117-T2-146 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 3:17 | The peace registry draws from all major biblical literary forms: (1) Prophecy (dominant in OT): Isa 9:6/7, 26:3, 30:15, 53:5, 54:10, Jer 29:11, Mic 5:5, Eze 34:25, 37:26 — the prophetic corpus carries the eschatological and messianic peace evidence and the refusal-of-peace pattern; (2) Psalms: Psa 4:8, 28:3, 32:3, 34:14, 62:5, 85:8, 119:165, 120:7, 131:2 — carries the experiential/devotional inner-peace evidence; (3) Wisdom: Pro 3:2/17, Ecc 9:17, Jam 3:17/18 — carries the peace-wisdom correlation; (4) Epistle: Rom 5:1, 8:6, Phili 4:7, Gal 5:22, 1Th 5:23, Col 1:20, Eph 2:14 — carries the NT theological synthesis; (5) Torah/Narrative: Num 6:26, Deu 28:65, Deu 29:19, Gen 28:21 — foundational covenant structures; (6) Lament: Lam 3:17 — the peaceless-soul at depth. The breadth of literary form is among the widest in the programme — peace is not a genre-specific concept but spans the full biblical literary spectrum. |

### H2803I cha.shav — to devise: devise (`mti_id=3334`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `H2803I` · Lang: Hebrew · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R112 mind · Legacy C-cluster: C01
3. Sub-group: `M15-E` (Deliberative planning, counsel, and purposive intent)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: research_not_required · Occurrences: 44
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=3334` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=not_done`
7. Retention note: Legacy evidential_status=confirmed mapped to research_not_required (M29 remap 2026-04-20)

**Verses:** 43 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (2)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1766 | 3334-001 | Term names the deliberate devising of harm — the inner formation of schemes, plots, or evil plans against another |  |
| 1767 | 3334-002 | Term names purposive planning — the inner formation of a deliberate purpose or plan, whether by God or human agents |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (8 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| 112-F004 | VERSE_PATTERN | Jer 29:11, Ps 140:2 | The cha.shav root documents that the same mental faculty produces artistic design, moral reasoning, and malicious plotting — there is no separate creative vs rational faculty in Hebrew anthropology. God's planning uses the same root as wicked scheming. |
| OBS-117-T2-002 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 29:11 | Yes — extensively. God possesses shalom as identity (YHWH-Shalom, OBS-117-148, OBS-117-001), gives shalom covenantally (Num 6:26, Isa 54:10, OBS-117-145), makes shalom in his high heaven (Job 25:2, prior session OBS-117-103), plans shalom for his people (Jer 29:11, OBS-117-149), and speaks peace to his saints (Psa 85:8, OBS-117-145). The significance for the human person: because shalom is grounded in God's own identity, the human inner condition of peace is not self-generated but participatory — the human person receives and inhabits a peace that belongs to God. Psa 4:8 ("for you alone, O Lord, make me dwell in safety") is the clearest statement: the shalom of the inner life depends entirely on God as its ground. Isa 26:3 (OBS-117-144) confirms: perfect peace for the mind stayed on God — the directionality is always from God-as-peace-source to human-as-peace-recipient. |
| OBS-117-T2-003 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 29:11 | Peace serves as the integrative condition of the human person's existence — the state in which the person is whole, functional, and in right relation with God, self, and others. The evidence from OBS-117-144, OBS-117-149, OBS-117-156 establishes this: shalom as wholeness of person (Isa 26:3 — mind, trust, peace as an integrated state), welfare of the whole person (Jer 29:11 — future, hope), soul's rest (Psa 4:8). The purpose is not merely psychological comfort but existential integrity — peace is the condition in which the person fulfils their creaturely purpose of walking with God (Mal 2:6, OBS-117-146: walked with me in peace and uprightness). Lam 3:17 ("my soul is bereft of peace; I have forgotten what happiness is") shows by negation: without shalom, the soul loses access to its own flourishing. |
| OBS-117-T2-005 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 29:11 | Yes — and more strongly than almost any other registry. The eschatological orientation of peace is the most developed in the programme's data. Evidence: (1) H7965G group 2508-004 (DHC) — messianic peace is inherently future-oriented: "Prince of Peace" (Isa 9:6), "of his government and of peace there will be no end" (Isa 9:7), eschatological community dwelling in peaceful habitation (Isa 32:18), OBS-117-147; (2) H7965G group 2508-002 — the eternal covenant of peace (Isa 54:10, Eze 37:26) is a future-facing promise given in the present; (3) Psa 72:7 (peace abounding till the moon be no more), Isa 66:12 (peace extended like a river); (4) Jer 29:11 (plans for welfare, future, and hope — OBS-117-149) — God's peace-intention is explicitly teleological: "a future and a hope." Peace is not only what a person presently is but what they are moving toward. |
| OBS-117-T2-014 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 29:11 | The core inner-being essence of shalom is integrative wholeness — the condition of the whole person (body, soul, mind, relationships, and orientation toward God) in which everything is in its proper place and functioning rightly. This is not a narrow emotional state but an existential condition. The data establishes this through convergence: (1) The etymology of shalom (completeness, soundness — OBS-117-009) names the absence of fragmentation or deficiency; (2) Lam 3:17 (OBS-117-144) — "my soul is bereft of peace; I have forgotten what happiness is" — shows that shalom-deprivation is total inner destitution, not merely sadness; (3) Jer 29:11 (OBS-117-149) — "welfare... future... hope" — the three outcomes of God's sha.lom-intention show peace as the condition that makes flourishing possible; (4) Isa 26:3 (OBS-117-144) — perfect peace for the mind stayed on God — shows that shalom is the integrative outcome of right orientation. The essence is best stated: peace is the condition of integrative wholeness in which the person is complete, secure, and in right relation — capable of their full creaturely purpose. |
| OBS-117-T2-021 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 29:11 | The data provides explicit antonyms: (1) War (milchamah) — Psa 120:7 ("I am for peace, but when I speak, they are for war"); the most direct antonym in the moral-orientation register; (2) Evil (ra'ah) — Jer 29:11 ("plans for welfare and not for evil"); the antonym in the divine-intention register; (3) Calamity/disaster — Isa 45:7 ("I make well-being and create calamity"); the antonym in the creational register; (4) Terror/panic — Jer 30:5 ("a cry of panic, of terror, and no peace"); the experiential antonym; (5) Stubbornness/iniquity — Deu 29:19, Mal 2:6 ("turned many from iniquity"); moral disorder as peace's contrary; (6) Anguish — Eze 7:25 ("when anguish comes they will seek peace, but there shall be none"). The antonym profile confirms that peace is not a neutral absence of distress but a positive condition with multiple contraries — war (relational), evil (moral), calamity (existential), terror (experiential), stubbornness (dispositional). |
| OBS-117-T2-090 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 29:11 | God's extension of peace reveals his constitutive orientation of goodwill and wholeness toward the human person. Three dimensions: (1) God delights in the welfare of his servant (Psa 35:27 — OBS-117-149) — peace-giving is the expression of divine delight, not merely duty; (2) God plans for welfare and not for evil (Jer 29:11) — the divine intention toward the human person is purposively peaceful; (3) God identified as peace (YHWH-Shalom, 406-001) — God's own identity includes peace as a constitutive attribute, meaning his extension of peace is not situational but essential to who he is. The convergence of these three dimensions reveals that God's peace-giving is not a response to human deserving but the overflow of his own identity — the God who IS peace gives peace because that is what he is. |
| OBS-117-T2-151 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jer 29:11 | Two frameworks are most directly relevant: (1) Positive psychology / wellbeing science: the concept of eudaimonia (flourishing/wellbeing) in Aristotle's ethics, and its contemporary development in positive psychology (Seligman's PERMA model — Positive emotion, Engagement, Relationships, Meaning, Accomplishment), maps most closely onto the shalom vocabulary. Shalom as comprehensive welfare and flourishing overlaps substantially with eudaimonia — both name the condition of the whole person thriving rather than merely experiencing pleasure; (2) Attachment theory (psychology): the Bowlby/Ainsworth framework of secure attachment producing a "safe base" from which the person can operate with confidence — this maps directly onto the peace-trust relationship (Psa 4:8 — resting in God's sole keeping; Isa 26:3 — mind stayed on God produces perfect peace). Secure attachment to God as the constitutional basis of inner peace is the closest psychological equivalent to the biblical structure. Neither framework is identical to the biblical concept — both illuminate without capturing it fully. |

<a id="t-H0998"></a>
### H0998 bi.nah — understanding (`mti_id=523`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `H0998` · Lang: Hebrew · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R174 wisdom · Legacy C-cluster: C02
3. Sub-group: `M15-B` (Understanding as inner perceptive faculty)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 38
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=523` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=not_done`

**Verses:** 38 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (3)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2471 | 523-001 | Term names Israel's wisdom and understanding as demonstrated through faithful obedience to God's statutes — the inner capacity for discernment made visible in the life of the covenant people (Deu 4:6). | Revised during Dimension Review Phase C/anchor consultation: description corrected — wisdom 523-001 |
| 2472 | 523-002 | Term names understanding as a human inner-being capacity — the gift from God enabling discernment of his ways and wise conduct |  |
| 704 | 816-002 | Term names understanding as the inner reception and grasp of revealed truth — the capacity to perceive insight into mystery, to receive what God gives, and to resist what God thwarts |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (13 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-52-002 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Job 39:17 | Five groups in the division registry are assigned Divine-Human Correspondence (2962-002, 5274-002, 5280-001, 824-001, 828-001). This is a significant concentration. The portion/lot vocabulary (chelek, chalaq, meros, meris) consistently operates in divine-human correspondence mode — God allots, human receives, inner states arise in response. Session B should examine whether this correspondence structure is intrinsic to the portion concept or a feature of the particular biblical corpus. |
| OBS-060-T2-024 | VERSE_PATTERN | Job 38:36 | Silence on immediate response is noted in three areas: (1) The endowing giving (Neh 9:20, Job 38:36 — group 872-003): no immediate human response to the giving of Spirit/wisdom/understanding is recorded in the anchor verses themselves. The effect (instruction, understanding) is named but not the experiential response. (2) The judicial giving-over (Hos 13:11, Jer 12:7 — group 872-004): no human response is recorded in the anchor verses — the human appears as the object of divine judicial giving, not as a responding agent. (3) The glory-withholding (Isa 42:8 — group 872-006): God's declaration that he will not give his glory to another records no human response. These silences are themselves informative: they suggest that endowing, judicial, and declarative giving are presented primarily from the divine side, with the human response either implicit or absent in the primary texts. |
| OBS-060-T2-041 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Job 38:36 | Yes — Job 38:36 (OBS-060-041) names the sekhwi (inner consciousness, understanding faculty, sometimes translated "mind") as the location to which God gives understanding: "Who has put wisdom in the inward parts or given understanding to the mind (sekhwi)?" The mind/inner consciousness is explicitly the target of endowing giving. Additionally, Isa 50:4 (group 872-003 non-anchor verse): "The Lord God has given me the tongue of those who are taught, that I may know how to sustain with a word him who is weary" — the giving of the taught tongue engages the intellectual-communicative faculty. The cognitive faculty is the target in group 872-003 broadly. |
| OBS-060-T2-042 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Job 38:36 | Mind-location in Job 38:36 and group 872-003 engages the understanding and discernment aspect of the mind's function, not merely information-processing. God gives wisdom to the "inward parts" (tukhoth — hidden inner seat of understanding) and understanding to the "mind" (sekhwi). The cognitive function engaged is the capacity to perceive rightly — wisdom and understanding as the inner ability to process and interpret reality from within. The giving of wisdom/Spirit-to-instruct (Neh 9:20) also confirms this: the giving targets the formative-cognitive capacity, producing the ability to understand God's ways. Mind-location therefore reveals that this characteristic can deposit cognitive capacity: giving is not only material or relational — it can constitute the person's ability to know and discern. |
| OBS-060-T2-043 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Job 38:36 | Yes — two additional soul-subset locations are present. (1) Tukhoth (inward parts/hidden inner being, Job 38:36, OBS-060-041): a relatively rare term for the hidden innermost location — distinct from leb (heart) and sekhwi (mind), tukhoth appears to name the most concealed level of inner being where wisdom is placed. This is not a standard tracked programme term and its relationship to leb/nephesh is analytically unclear from this data alone. (2) Rahamim (compassion/womb-feeling, Hos 11:8, OBS-060-038): the compassion that prevents giving-over is named rahamim — literally the "womb feelings," the deepest visceral attachment. Rahamim is a soul-level (or perhaps body-located-but-soul-expressed) location for the relational orientation that determines whether giving or withholding occurs. |
| OBS-060-T2-044 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Job 38:36 | Tukhoth reveals that the giving of wisdom reaches into the most concealed, hidden level of inner being — not the surface mind but the deepest interior where understanding is constituted. This is consistent with Ps 51:6 ("you delight in truth in the inward being; you teach me wisdom in the secret heart") — tukhoth as the locus of foundational inner-being formation. Rahamim reveals that the relational-emotional orientation that determines giving is located in the deepest visceral attachment — the womb-level of feeling. Rahamim is not a surface emotion but the most fundamental relational pull, placed at the level of physical-affective integration (viscera, womb). Together these two locations confirm that inner-being giving in this registry engages the full depth of the inner person: from the most concealed cognitive formation (tukhoth) to the most visceral relational attachment (rahamim). |
| OBS-060-T2-060 | OBSERVATION | Job 38:36 | Yes — most directly in group 872-003. Neh 9:20 (OBS-060-040): Spirit given to instruct (sakal — to give insight, understand prudently). Job 38:36 (OBS-060-041): wisdom given to the inward parts, understanding to the mind. The cognitive faculty is the direct target of endowing giving in this group: the giving of wisdom and understanding constitutes cognitive capacity in the human. This is the most direct engagement of the cognitive faculty in the registry. Cognition is not merely present as a background faculty — it is the specific object of giving in group 872-003, confirming the Cognition dimension assignment for that group (OBS-060-020). |
| OBS-060-T2-061 | OBSERVATION | Job 38:36 | It enables cognition in its endowing mode (Neh 9:20, Job 38:36) — the giving of Spirit, wisdom, and understanding constitutes the cognitive capacity that the person then possesses. It does not bypass or impair cognition in any evidenced instance. In the failure direction: Jer 13:16 shows that the failure to give glory produces darkening and stumbling — this is perceptive more than cognitive, but the loss of inner-being orientation (darkening) may imply cognitive disorder as well. The primary engagement is enabling: this characteristic, in its endowing mode, is constitutive of cognitive capacity. |
| OBS-060-T2-062 | OBSERVATION | Job 38:36 | The pattern reveals that this characteristic has a constitutive relationship with cognition in its endowing mode: inner-being giving can literally create cognitive capacity where it did not previously exist — or could not sustain itself. Job 38:36 frames this as divine prerogative ("who has given...?") — confirming that human cognition at its deepest level is not self-generated but received. The giving that constitutes cognition is therefore the most fundamental form of the characteristic: not giving something the person might use, but giving the capacity to use anything. This reveals that inner-being giving, at its deepest, is constitutive rather than supplementary. |
| OBS-060-T2-076 | OBSERVATION | Job 38:36 | The endowing giving (872-003) enables moral evaluation: wisdom and understanding given to the inward parts (Job 38:36) constitute the cognitive capacity for right moral discernment. Spirit given to instruct (Neh 9:20) forms the moral-cognitive faculty. The eschatological giving restores the inner-being orientation from which moral evaluation flows — "oaks of righteousness" (Isa 61:3) names a stable moral identity restored by the giving. Covenantal giving (872-001) deepens moral evaluation by grounding the person's identity in the narrative of divine faithfulness — the memory of God's reliable giving provides the moral framework against which actions are assessed. In the failure direction: the judicial giving-over (872-004) may represent a consequence of failed moral evaluation (Israel's failure to assess rightly what faithfulness required), though the data is more explicit about the consequence than the prior failure. |
| OBS-060-T2-077 | OBSERVATION | Job 38:36 | The pattern reveals that this characteristic operates within and upon the moral evaluation faculty in two directions: it is the object of moral evaluation (Jer 17:10 — God gives according to moral assessment of deeds) and it is the source of moral evaluative capacity (the endowing of wisdom and Spirit constitutes the faculty that evaluates). Giving-as-faithfulness therefore has a reflexive relationship with moral evaluation: it both expresses moral judgement (in the judicial mode) and constitutes the capacity for moral judgement (in the endowing mode). This reveals that the moral character dimension of the registry is not merely descriptive — inner-being giving is tied to the structure of moral reality itself. |
| OBS-060-T2-112 | VERSE_PATTERN | Job 38:36 | Yes — primarily through the endowing mode (872-003). The Spirit given to instruct (Neh 9:20) is a formative gift: its purpose is to shape the inner-cognitive-moral life over time, equipping the person for faithful walk. The wisdom given to the inward parts (Job 38:36) constitutes the deep cognitive capacity from which discerning character grows. The covenantal giving (872-001) participates in the formation of covenantal identity across generations — the Nehemiah corpus (Neh 9) is explicitly a rehearsal of divine giving as the ground of renewed covenantal commitment: the memory of God's giving shapes the present community's character orientation. The eschatological transformation ("oaks of righteousness," Isa 61:3) names the endpoint of the formation arc — the person shaped by divine giving reaches stable moral identity. |
| OBS-060-T2-143 | VERSE_ANNOTATION | Job 38:36 | The anchor verse corpus spans four literary forms. (1) Narrative (Gen 12:7, Gen 15:18, Gen 29:33, Job 1:21): responsible interpretation requires attention to plot and character — the giving is embedded in a specific relational and historical moment. (2) Prophecy (Isa 61:3, Isa 50:6, Hos 11:8, Hos 13:11, Jer 12:7, Jer 13:16): responsible interpretation requires attention to rhetorical address and the distinction between divine speech and prophetic interpretation. The first-person divine speeches ("I gave," "How can I give you up") require particular care about the nature of divine inner-being language. (3) Wisdom/divine speech (Job 38:36, Job 1:21): responsible interpretation requires attention to the dramatic frame — Job's blessing is within a frame where Satan has made a claim, and God's question from the whirlwind is within a legal disputation. (4) Liturgical/prayer (Neh 9:20): responsible interpretation requires attention to the retrospective narrative form — this is communal confession of divine faithfulness, not direct reportage. The diversity of literary forms means no single interpretive lens covers the whole corpus. |

<a id="t-G1014"></a>
### G1014 boulomai — to plan (`mti_id=509`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G1014` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R043 desire · Legacy C-cluster: C04
3. Sub-group: `M15-E` (Deliberative planning, counsel, and purposive intent)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 117
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=509` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=not_done`
6. Mounce short-def: to wish, will, desire; to choose, determine, plan

**Verses:** 34 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (2)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1647 | 509-001 | Term names the inner act of human willing, intending, or desiring — the volitional faculty in motion toward an object or action |  |
| 1648 | 509-002 | Term names God's or Christ's inner purposive will — sovereign divine willing that directs revelation, creation, and redemption |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (6 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-103-T2-013 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 4:4 | All three. Directional: love always has a direction — toward God, toward neighbour, toward the beloved, or toward disordered objects (money, pleasure, world). The moral question of love is always the question of its direction (OBS-103-017). Relational: love cannot exist in isolation — it is constitutively relational, requiring at minimum a lover and a beloved. The relational structure is not incidental but essential (OBS-103-035). Constitutional: love operates at the deepest level of the inner person — the great commandment (Mat 22:37) requires 'all your heart and soul and mind,' indicating that love engages the entire constitutional depth of the inner being (OBS-103-139). The enquiry is oriented from the outset toward a characteristic that is simultaneously moral (choice of direction), relational (requires another), and constitutional (engages the whole person). |
| OBS-103-T2-017 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 4:4 | The structural opposite is hatred (sane — H8130, 139 active verses, OWNER term, OBS-103-030). Sane is embedded in R103 as an OWNER term — the programme treats hate as constitutive of the love vocabulary's boundary, not external to it. Lev 19:17 ('you shall not hate your brother in your heart') places hate as the inner alternative to love of neighbour (OBS-103-114). Pro 10:12 ('hatred stirs up strife, love covers all offences,' OBS-103-097) places them as causal-opposite forces with opposed social effects. The secondary structural opposite at the ultimate level is enmity with God: friendship with the world is enmity (echthra) with God (Jam 4:4, OBS-103-058). But hatred is the primary inner-being opposite — it is the same inner mechanism as love (directed orientation) running in the opposed direction. |
| OBS-103-T2-018 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 4:4 | 1Cor 13:4-7 provides the programme's most explicit catalogue of what love excludes: envy, boasting, arrogance, rudeness, self-seeking, irritability, resentfulness, rejoicing in wrongdoing (OBS-103-127). The list names love's resistances across moral, relational, and emotional registers. At the structural level, love resists division of ultimate allegiance (Jam 4:4 — cannot love God and world simultaneously, OBS-103-058), resists self-assertion as the primary orientation (love-of-self as the first item in the last-days catalogue, OBS-103-137), and resists the withholding of self (Joh 15:13 — love's maximum is the giving of the soul/life, OBS-103-125). Love also explicitly resists concealment of inner truth from the beloved: Lev 19:17 contrasts hate-as-silence with love-as-frank-correction (OBS-103-114). |
| OBS-103-T2-059 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 4:4 | Yes — through the judgment of the love-object's worth and the assessment of love's hierarchy. Mat 10:37 (OBS-103-151) requires a judgment: 'whoever loves father or mother more than me is not worthy of me' — love operates through a comparative judgment of worth among competing objects. The ranking of loves (OBS-103-022 — love as four-mode structure) requires reason to navigate. Jam 4:4 (OBS-103-058): the reasoning that friendship with the world constitutes enmity with God is a logical judgment. Pro 10:12 (OBS-103-097): 'love covers all offences' — the judgment that covering is better than exposing is a reasoned moral assessment. Love requires and shapes the judgment-faculty at the level of determining what and whom to love, in what order, and how. |
| OBS-103-T2-128 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 4:4 | Yes — love is always directed. The grammar confirms this: agapaō always takes a direct object; the philo-compound system is entirely object-defining (love-of-X where X determines the compound). No instance of undirected love appears in the data — love is constitutively directional (OBS-103-017, OBS-103-009). The object determines love's moral character entirely: the inner mechanism is identical whether the object is God (filotheos — morally ordered) or pleasure (filēdonos — morally disordered) or money (filarguros). This is the data's most consistently evidenced structural feature: the same inner act of attachment produces radically different moral outcomes based solely on the object. The moral life in the biblical data is substantially a question of love's direction — not whether one loves but what one loves. This confirms L-004's anticipated finding (below). |
| OBS-103-T2-131 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Jam 4:4 | Yes — the philo-compound disordered forms constitute a taxonomy (OBS-103-036, OBS-103-017): filarguria/filarguros (love of money — 3 groups), filēdonos (love of pleasure), filoneikia/filoneikos (love of dispute), filoprōteuō (love of being first), filia tou kosmou (friendship with world — Jam 4:4), agapaō-darkness/world (Joh 3:19, 2Ti 4:10), agavah (lust — H5691), filautos (love of self — 2Ti 3:2). The structural logic organising the taxonomy: all disordered loves are the same inner mechanism (directed attachment) applied to one of three wrong object-classes: (a) **Material objects** — money, pleasure; (b) **Social-positional objects** — primacy, dispute-victory; (c) **The self** — self-love, friendship with the world as self-assertion. The taxonomy is not arbitrary — it maps the full range of objects that compete with God and the neighbour as the proper objects of love. The disordered loves are a systematic counter-ordering of the inner person's relational architecture. |

| 2343 | 1205-003 | Understanding as a practical inner act — discerning the will of God and one's situation |  |
| 1363 | 932-004 | Term names the failure, hardening, or absence of inner understanding — spiritual blindness, incapacity to perceive God or moral reality, and the rhetorical limits of human comprehension before God |  |

<a id="t-G0050"></a>
### G0050 agnoeō — be ignorant (`mti_id=1838`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G0050` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R026 conscience · Legacy C-cluster: C13
3. Sub-group: `M15-C` (Knowledge as inner content and covenantal knowing)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 30
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=1838` · `md_version=2` · `vc_status=vc_completed`
6. Mounce short-def: to be ignorant, not know, not understand

**Verses:** 21 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 360 | 1838-001 | Term names culpable spiritual ignorance — the inner condition of not knowing God's character, ways, or will, which becomes the basis of moral failure or self-deception |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (30 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-067-T2-009 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 2:4 | The covenantal typological dimension is present and clear: God's good word (884-005, Jos 23:14) and his chrēstotēs displayed supremely in Christ (Eph 2:7) both carry a Christological register — the fullness of divine goodness is displayed in Christ's person and work. Rom 2:4 names chrēstotēs as the mechanism through which God leads to repentance, which is structurally a Christological movement. However, the data does not contain an explicit typological argument (e.g., the good king who typifies the messianic king, or a creation-goodness → new-creation-goodness typology). The christological and eschatological dimensions are present in NT usage but not through formal typological structure. This is a partial finding — the direction is named but the full typological structure is not explicitly drawn in the verse evidence. (OBS-067-OBS-006, OBS-067-OBS-036) |
| OBS-067-T2-024 | VERSE_PATTERN | Rom 2:4 | The immediate response varies by context and subject. When the human encounters divine goodness: trust, praise, and receptive desire (as above). When the person encounters not-goodness (their own — Eze 36:31): the immediate response is self-loathing and moral awareness — the recognition of deeds as not-good immediately produces self-directed revulsion. When the person encounters divine chrēstotēs (Rom 2:4): the immediate inner movement is toward repentance — the goodness of God produces a first response of conviction and turning. The variation is structured: different registers of goodness encounter produce different first responses, but each response is coherent with the register. Divine goodness in its fullness produces trust; divine goodness as conviction produces repentance; human not-goodness recognised produces loathing. (OBS-067-OBS-035, OBS-067-OBS-025, SBF-067-Q031) |
| OBS-067-T2-074 | VERSE_PATTERN | Rom 2:4 | Conscience is engaged most directly at Eze 36:31: "you will remember your evil ways, and your deeds that were not good, and you will loathe yourselves for your iniquities and your abominations." The self-loathing described is a conscience response — the inner witness of moral failure producing conviction. The sequence (remember → recognise as not-good → loathe) is a conscience sequence: the moral inner witness activates, names the failure, and produces the appropriate inner response (loathing/conviction). Rom 2:4's chrēstotēs "leading to repentance" also implies a conscience engagement — God's kindness acts on the inner being's conscience to produce the movement toward repentance. The goodness-conscience connection operates through the negative register: the recognition of not-goodness activates conscience; the encounter with divine goodness produces the conditions in which conscience can operate truthfully. (OBS-067-OBS-035, OBS-067-OBS-049, SBF-067-Q049) |
| OBS-067-T2-075 | VERSE_PATTERN | Rom 2:4 | Goodness enables and deepens conscience — specifically through the not-goodness register. The recognition of not-goodness (884-006 and Eze 36:31) is a conscience operation enabled by the standard of the good: only a person who has access to the standard of genuine goodness can accurately recognise their own not-goodness and respond with appropriate conviction. The distortion form (Mal 2:17 / Jer 44:17) shows conscience impaired: when goodness is redefined, the conscience's witness becomes unreliable — it applies the wrong standard and produces false moral comfort. Divine chrēstotēs (Rom 2:4) deepens conscience by making repentance both possible and compelling — God's goodness creates the conditions in which the conscience can function toward its proper end (turning). (OBS-067-OBS-021, OBS-067-OBS-035, OBS-067-OBS-043) |
| OBS-067-T2-076 | VERSE_PATTERN | Rom 2:4 | The conscience engagement reveals that goodness functions as the calibrating standard for the conscience's operation. Conscience requires a standard against which it measures; goodness provides that standard. This means that the health of the conscience is tied to the health of the goodness-characteristic: a corrupted goodness standard produces a corrupted conscience witness; a genuine goodness standard enables a truthful conscience. The Eze 36:31 sequence — divine action producing moral self-recognition and loathing — suggests that the restoration of truthful conscience requires a prior restoration of the goodness standard. Goodness and conscience are structurally co-dependent: conscience witnesses accurately to the degree that the good it witnesses against is genuine. (OBS-067-OBS-021, OBS-067-OBS-035, SBF-067-Q049) |
| OBS-067-T2-084 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 2:4 | The evidence reveals a structural tension between the unconditional and conditional registers — and this tension is itself the analytical finding. On the unconditional side: Gen 1:31 establishes goodness as a creative-ontological act prior to any human response — God declares creation good without condition. The doxological refrain "for he is good, his steadfast love endures forever" names an enduring, non-contingent character attribute. On the conditional side: Rom 11:22 names a condition on the ongoing experience of divine chrēstotēs — "provided you continue in his kindness. Otherwise you too will be cut off." This is not a condition on God's character but on the human's ongoing reception of its benefits. Rom 2:4 implies responsiveness — God's kindness "is meant to lead you to repentance," with an implied expectation that repentance follows. The resolution: God's goodness as a character attribute is unconditional; the human's ongoing experience of its relational benefits is covenantal-responsive. This distinction is analytically significant and carries to SP-067-018 (the conditionality question). (OBS-067-OBS-006, OBS-067-OBS-043, OBS-067-OBS-044, SP-067-018) |
| OBS-067-T2-096 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 2:4 | The evidence supports both covenantal and cross-covenantal operation. Within covenantal contexts: 884-005 (God's covenantal word, Jos 23:14), 886-001 and 886-002 (Spirit-produced goodness in the new covenant community). These are explicitly covenantal. Cross-covenantal: H2896A in 884-002's moral character register applies to human moral life broadly — the prophetic verdict on not-good conduct (884-006) is addressed to covenant people but the ethical standard (Mic 6:8) has a universal logic. The creation-goodness of Gen 1:31 (884-007) is pre-covenantal — God's goodness extends to all creation before any covenant is formed. Rom 2:4 ("God's kindness leads you to repentance") is addressed to those outside the covenant community as well as within — the mechanism of divine chrēstotēs is not restricted to the covenant. (OBS-067-OBS-006, OBS-067-OBS-036, OBS-067-OBS-043) |
| OBS-067-T2-097 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 2:4 | The relational scope of goodness is wide but not fully mapped in the data. Inclusion evidenced: creation as a whole (Gen 1:31 — all that God made is good); covenant community (Jos 23:14; Col 3:12; Rom 15:14); those outside the covenant who receive divine chrēstotēs leading to repentance (Rom 2:4); the soul who seeks (Lam 3:25 — the seeking person, without boundary). Possible exclusion: Rom 11:22 names "those who have fallen" as the recipients of severity rather than chrēstotēs — but this is presented as a relational condition rather than a categorical exclusion. The data does not name any class of persons categorically excluded from the scope of divine goodness; the blocking conditions (Q&A-032) describe inner orientations that close reception, not divine decisions to exclude. Whether the human expression of goodness has specific scope limits is not directly addressed. Partial gap. (OBS-067-OBS-006, OBS-067-OBS-043, OBS-067-OBS-044) |
| OBS-067-T2-101 | VERSE_PATTERN | Rom 2:4 | Two distinct sequences are named. Sequence 1 — positive transformation arc (divine goodness → reception → reorientation): (a) Encounter with divine goodness — Psa 34:8 ("taste and see"); (b) Trust and refuge — "blessed is the man who takes refuge in him"; (c) Stable inner orientation — Psa 73:28 ("it is good to be near God; I have made the Lord God my refuge"); (d) Overflow toward others — "that I may tell of all your works" (Psa 73:28 conclusion). Sequence 2 — moral awareness arc (recognition → repentance → transformation): (a) Memory of deeds — Eze 36:31 ("you will remember your evil ways"); (b) Recognition as not-good — "and your deeds that were not good"; (c) Self-loathing — "you will loathe yourselves"; (d) Movement toward repentance — implied by context (Eze 36:26 God's Spirit given follows 36:31's loathing). Rom 2:4 names the divine chrēstotēs → repentance sequence directly. Both sequences are coherent and traceable in the verse evidence. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-035, OBS-067-OBS-029, SBF-067-Q031) |
| OBS-067-T2-103 | VERSE_PATTERN | Rom 2:4 | Three distinct mechanisms are evidenced, corresponding to the three transformation types identified at Q&A-123: (1) Encounter mechanism — Psa 34:8 / Psa 73:28: the mechanism is perceptual encounter with divine goodness. The change is produced by tasting and seeing — a direct experiential contact that reorients the inner being. This is neither discipline nor gradual formation but encounter-transformation. (2) Spirit-infusion mechanism — Gal 5:22 / Rom 15:14: the Spirit fills the person with goodness. The mechanism is pneumatological infusion — goodness as fruit is the Spirit's work in the inner being. This is not achieved by effort but received as Spirit-gift. (3) Recognition-confrontation mechanism — Eze 36:31 / Rom 2:4: the mechanism is confrontation with one's own not-goodness in the presence of the divine goodness standard. Memory + recognition + loathing = the mechanism of moral transformation toward goodness. All three mechanisms are present; they are not competing but operate in different registers and at different moments. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-035, OBS-067-OBS-039, SBF-067-Q031) |
| OBS-067-T2-113 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Rom 2:4 | Two sequential relationships are directly named: (1) Goodness → repentance (Rom 2:4): "God's kindness is meant to lead you to repentance" — chrēstotēs explicitly precedes and produces repentance. The sequence is causal: divine goodness is prior, repentance follows. (2) Goodness → faith/trust (Psa 34:8): encounter with divine goodness ("taste and see that the Lord is good") is followed by "blessed is the man who takes refuge in him" — goodness is the experiential prior that produces the faith/refuge response. Goodness precedes faith in the encounter sequence. A third sequence is implied by SBF-067-Q133 (prior analysis): goodness is prior to faith (encounter with goodness → faith response); 2Th 1:11 shows them as parallel co-products in the other direction. These sequential findings are confirmed and structured. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-043, SBF-067-Q127, SBF-067-Q133) |
| OBS-067-T2-114 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Rom 2:4 | The goodness → repentance sequence (Rom 2:4) is explicitly causal: "is meant to lead to" — divine chrēstotēs is the mechanism producing repentance. The goodness → faith sequence (Psa 34:8) is causal-enabling: the experiential encounter with goodness enables and produces the trust response. The goodness-and-faith parallel sequence (2Th 1:11: "every resolve for good and every work of faith") is correlational — they are co-produced by God's power rather than one causing the other. The three sequential relationships reveal three different structural relationships: causal (goodness produces repentance), enabling-causal (goodness enables faith-response), and correlational (goodness-resolve and faith-work as parallel Spirit-expressions). (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-043, SBF-067-Q127) |
| OBS-067-T2-115 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Rom 2:4 | Two producing relationships are directly evidenced: (1) Goodness produces repentance — Rom 2:4: divine chrēstotēs → repentance (mechanism: moral conviction and drawing). (2) Goodness produces trust/faith — Psa 34:8: encounter with divine goodness → trust-response (mechanism: experiential encounter enabling faith). A third is implied: goodness produces praise — the doxological group (884-001) is the response to divine goodness; the encounter produces the praise. These are all divine-to-human sequences: God's goodness produces these characteristics in the human inner being. The human expression of goodness in community (Rom 15:14) produces instruction-capacity in others — goodness produces formation-capacity. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-043, SBF-067-Q004) |
| OBS-067-T2-141 | VERSE_ANNOTATION | Rom 2:4 | Three key argument structures: (1) Mic 6:8 — definitional argument: if goodness is defined as X, and X consists of justice + kindness + humility, then goodness is not a simple quality but a tri-fold orientation. The logical structure is definitional-analytical. (2) Rom 2:4 — teleological argument: God's chrēstotēs has a purpose ("meant to lead you to repentance") — the argument is that the purpose of divine goodness is the human's turning. The logical structure is purposive. (3) Rom 11:22 — conditional argument: God's kindness operates under a conditional that requires human continuance ("provided you continue in his kindness"). The logical structure is conditional. These three argument structures (definitional, purposive, conditional) represent three different logical relationships between goodness and other inner-being realities. (OBS-067-OBS-027, OBS-067-OBS-043, OBS-067-OBS-044, SBF-067-Q042) |
| OBS-099-T2-001 | OBSERVATION | Rom 2:4 | The evidence reveals that kindness (chesed) is God's defining relational attribute — not one quality among many but the one he most characteristically names when he identifies himself. Exo 34:6 establishes the canonical self-disclosure: God is "merciful and gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness." The superlative grammar ("abounding") signals that chesed is characteristic of him in excess [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-089]. Lam 3:22 further establishes that God's chesed "never ceases" — permanence and inexhaustibility are definitional [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-085]. In the NT, chrēstotēs (G5544) is attributed directly to God in Rom 2:4 and Rom 11:22, where divine kindness is paired with severity as two expressions of the same covenantal faithfulness [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-091]. The character of God revealed: one whose inner disposition toward the creature is constitutively kind — stable, inexhaustible, and covenantally faithful. |
| OBS-099-T2-002 | OBSERVATION | Rom 2:4 | Scripture explicitly and extensively attributes kindness to God in three forms: (a) direct predication — "God is kind to the ungrateful and evil" (Luke 6:35); "the Lord is kind in all his works" (Psa 145:17 [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-095]); (b) superlative attribution — "abounding in steadfast love" (Exo 34:6), "steadfast love is better than life" (Psa 63:3 [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-084]); (c) instrumental attribution — "God's kindness is meant to lead you to repentance" (Rom 2:4, OBS-099-076). The significance for the human person: divine kindness is God's defining attribute; the call for human kindness is ontological grounding — to be kind is to be what the image-bearer was created to be. The absence of kindness is not merely behavioural failure but dimming of the image. |
| OBS-099-T2-005 | OBSERVATION | Rom 2:4 | Yes. Eph 2:7 places divine kindness in an eschatological frame: "in the coming ages he might show the immeasurable riches of his grace in kindness toward us in Christ Jesus" [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. Rom 2:4 establishes that divine kindness is purposively aimed at repentance — directed toward a future state not yet present [OBS-099-076]. Kindness has an eschatological trajectory: both in the recipient (transformation) and in the divine economy (immeasurable future display). |
| OBS-099-T2-016 | OBSERVATION | Rom 2:4 | Working description: Kindness is a stable inner disposition of loyal goodwill toward another, rooted in the heart, that orients the whole person toward the practical benefit and relational flourishing of its object, regardless of desert. Constituted by faithfulness (holds across time and through failure), compassion (feels with the other), and absence of self-serving motivation. In divine form: inexhaustible, unconditional in ultimate terms, purposively aimed at transformation. In human form: creaturely, finite, Spirit-formed, called to mirror the divine pattern. Primary constitutional location: heart. Primary expression: deliberate relational action. Counterfeit: pleasant speech covering self-serving motivation (chrēstologia). |
| OBS-099-T2-021 | OBSERVATION | Rom 2:4 | Yes — three contextual variations: (a) Divine vs. human scope: God's chesed inexhaustible; human chesed creaturely and can be withdrawn [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-083, Q&A-008]. (b) Comfort vs. affliction: Job 6:4 (afflicting presence) vs. Psa 23:4 (comforting presence) — same term, different mode by context and divine purpose [OBS-099-067/066]. (c) Purposive vs. sustaining: Rom 2:4 — kindness aimed at repentance; Luke 6:35 — kindness to ungrateful without stated aim [OBS-099-076, OBS-099-078]. Purposive dimension present in some contexts; absent as explicit feature in others. |
| OBS-099-T2-025 | OBSERVATION | Rom 2:4 | Three sustained effects: (a) Moral formation into chesed-character — the chasid is the outcome of a life shaped by chesed [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]. (b) Repentance and transformation — Rom 2:4 names repentance as the sustained purpose of received divine kindness [OBS-099-076]. (c) Hope in desolation — Lam 3:22–23 — sustained knowledge of God's unfailing chesed produces groundedness that does not depend on circumstances [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-085]. |
| OBS-099-T2-029 | OBSERVATION | Rom 2:4 | Three blocking conditions: (a) Pride and self-sufficiency — Jer 17:5 — trusting in man closes the person to divine chesed [OBS-099-047]. (b) Presumption — Rom 2:4 — receiving the benefit while rejecting its purpose (repentance) is the distortion mode [OBS-099-076]. (c) Hardness of heart — Exo 7:14 — Pharaoh's hardened heart refuses the relational movement that divine kindness invites [OBS-099-059]. Hardness is the inner condition most directly opposed to receiving kindness. |
| OBS-099-T2-030 | OBSERVATION | Rom 2:4 | Three modes evidenced: (a) Pharaoh — hardens further; non-reception intensifies inner rigidity [OBS-099-059]. (b) Rom 2:4 — presumes comfortably; self-deceived [OBS-099-076]. (c) Hos 6:6 — substitutes ritual for inner disposition; outwardly religious while inwardly chesed-absent [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-088]. Gap: the evidence does not directly address the psychological state of the person who recognises and refuses human kindness specifically. |
| OBS-099-T2-047 | OBSERVATION | Rom 2:4 | Yes — two modes: (a) As prerequisite: Hos 6:6 pairs chesed with "knowledge of God" — genuine kindness requires cognitive grasp of who God is [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-088]. (b) As denied: Rom 2:4 — failure of cognition preconditions presumption: "not knowing that God's kindness is meant to lead you to repentance" [OBS-099-076]. Kindness requires knowing God to flow genuinely, and knowing God's intention to be received correctly. |
| OBS-099-T2-052 | OBSERVATION | Rom 2:4 | Yes — structurally. (a) Gen 44:32 — Judah's surety burden anticipates guilt if he fails [OBS-099-063]. Inner cost of loving-kindness (bearing responsibility for another) is conscience-engaging. (b) Hos 6:6 — ritual without chesed produces a bad conscience in the theological sense: worship while withholding what God desires [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-088]. (c) Rom 2:4 — "not knowing" = moral cognitive failure that a properly functioning conscience should catch [OBS-099-076]. Kindness engages conscience as the inner witness monitoring whether one's orientation is aligned with what one knows to be right. |
| OBS-099-T2-060 | OBSERVATION | Rom 2:4 | Yes — both modes. (a) Change of orientation: Rom 2:4 — aimed at repentance (metanoia — change of mind/direction) [OBS-099-076]. (b) Change of condition (formation): the chasid formation arc — person whose character is shaped by chesed over time [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094]. Reversibility: not explicitly addressed. Hos 6:6 (withdrawal of chesed as communal failure) and Mic 7:2 (godly has perished) suggest communal formation can be lost — individual reversibility not directly addressed. |
| OBS-099-T2-061 | OBSERVATION | Rom 2:4 | Partial sequence visible in Rom 2:4: (a) encounter with divine kindness → (b) recognition of its purpose → (c) repentance (reorientation). Before-during-after structure for received divine kindness [OBS-099-076, Q&A-072]. Chasid formation arc implies longer sequence (encounter → formation → stable identity → eschatological preservation [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-094/095]) but individual steps are not explicitly sequenced in a single passage. |
| OBS-099-T2-065 | OBSERVATION | Rom 2:4 | Yes — three eschatological markers: (a) Eschatological escalation: Eph 2:7 — "in the coming ages he might show the immeasurable riches of his grace in kindness toward us in Christ Jesus" [XREG: G5544, OBS-099-093]. Future form exceeds present expression. (b) Preservation of the chasid: Psa 16:10 — "you will not abandon my soul to Sheol, or let your holy one see corruption" [XREG: H2623, OBS-099-095]. Chesed-shaped person eschatologically preserved; formation survives death. (c) Telos: Rom 2:4 (repentance) and Eph 2:7 (eschatological display) together — divine kindness is purposive from beginning to end. |
| OBS-099-T2-067 | OBSERVATION | Rom 2:4 | Two partial sequences: (a) Eph 4:32 cluster — kindness → tenderheartedness → forgiveness [OBS-099-077, SP-099-009]. Whether strictly sequential or co-present is not resolved by the verse — listed together without specified order. (b) Rom 2:4 — encounter with divine kindness → recognition → repentance — explicitly sequential [OBS-099-076, Q&A-072]. Kindness precedes repentance; it is the mechanism leading to it. Gap: explicit sequential relationships (A causes B, enables C) are partially implied but not extensively narrated. |
| OBS-099-T2-068 | OBSERVATION | Rom 2:4 | Three explicit causal relationships: (a) Divine kindness → repentance: Rom 2:4 — kindness is the causal agent aimed at producing repentance [OBS-099-076]. (b) Experienced divine kindness → praise: Psa 63:3 — chesed causes the praise response [XREG: H2617A, OBS-099-084]. (c) Received human kindness → mirrored kindness: 2Sa 10:2 — "as his father dealt loyally with me, so I will deal loyally with Hanun" — received kindness causes its reproduction [OBS-099-071]. Causal network: divine kindness → praise + repentance in recipient → motivates mirrored human kindness → sustains covenant relationships. |
| OBS-099-T2-077 | OBSERVATION | Rom 2:4 | Although the mind is not explicitly named as kindness's constitutional location, the verse evidence shows kindness engaging the mind's cognitive-discernment function in two specific ways. First, Hos 6:6 — "I desire steadfast love and not sacrifice, the knowledge of God rather than burnt offerings" — pairs chesed with knowledge of God (da'at Elohim). Genuine chesed requires a cognitive grasp of who God is; without this knowledge, the person substitutes ritual for the real inner disposition. The mind's function of knowing and discerning is therefore prerequisite to genuine kindness [OBS-099-088]. Second, Rom 2:4 — "not knowing that God's kindness is meant to lead you to repentance" — names cognitive failure as the mechanism by which received kindness is misappropriated. A correctly functioning cognitive faculty recognises the purpose of divine kindness; a failing one presumes on it [OBS-099-076]. What this reveals: kindness is not a mind-level characteristic but it requires the mind's knowing-function to operate correctly — it is cognitively dependent without being cognitively located. |

<a id="t-H7878"></a>
### H7878 si.ach — to muse (`mti_id=242`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `H7878` · Lang: Hebrew · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R051 distress · Legacy C-cluster: C05
3. Sub-group: `M15-F` (Meditative and reflective inner activity)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 20
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=242` · `md_version=2` · `vc_status=vc_completed`

**Verses:** 20 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (2)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 798 | 242-001 | Meditation and inner pondering — reflective contemplation of God's works and word |  |
| 799 | 242-002 | Complaint and lament as inner expression of distress |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (0 unique)**

(none)

**SD pointers — direct (1)**

| Pointer | Priority | Strong's ref | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inner-word / inner-speech dimension of meditation — does registry 108 capture the interior/exterior boundary of word and thought (hagah, siach)? | MEDIUM | H1897; H7878; H7881; H1900; H1902H | Session D Investigation Prompt — Registry 108 (Meditation) — Inner Word / Inner Speech Dimension During a pre-Session D exploratory discussion, the question was raised whether the Framework B programme adequately captures the concept of word / voice / speak / thought as phenomena that are at least partly located in the inner being, rather than being purely physical or communicative acts. The discussion identified a convergence across three domains: Biblical-theological: Hebrew dabar (דָּבָר) carries both word and reality/event, suggesting the spoken word is an extension of the speaker's inner being. Hebrew lev (heart) is the seat of inner speech and thought — "the heart says..." is a genuine idiom for interior thought-speech. Greek logos carries the Stoic distinction between logos endiathetos (inner word/reason) and logos prophorikos (uttered word). Augustine's verbum mentis (word of the mind) is his pre-linguistic inner word, located in the soul. Philosophical: From Plato onward, thought is treated as inner dialogue — "the soul talking to itself." The logos endiathetos / prophorikos distinction runs from Stoics through Augustine and remains philosophically significant. Psychological/cognitive: Inner speech is a well-established phenomenon (Vygotsky, modern neuroscience). It activates speech-production brain areas and is partly constitutive of conscious thought, not merely a report of it. Programme gap identified: The programme has no dedicated registry for word, voice, speak, or logos. This is probably a principled scope decision — the programme covers inner states and dispositions, not communicative acts. However, the gap raises a specific question for Session D analysis of meditation (108, C02): Does the meditation registry adequately capture the inner-speech dimension of its core terms — particularly הָגָה (hagah, to murmur/muse inwardly) and שִׂיחַ (siach, to muse/speak inwardly) — as terms that sit at the interior/exterior boundary of word and thought? Or does the registry treat these primarily as contemplative states, missing the inner-speech signal? Specific investigation questions for Session D: 1. Are הָגָה and שִׂיחַ present in the meditation term inventory, and are they glossed in a way that preserves their inner-speech quality? 2. Does the analysis distinguish meditation-as-inner-speech from meditation-as-quiet-reflection? 3. Is there any cross-reference to thought (160) or counsel (32) that picks up the inner-word dimension? Note (registry-state observation, 2026-04-25): In the current registry-108 inventory, H1897 hagah ("to mutter") and H7878 siach verb ("to muse") are present as XREF terms — owned elsewhere — while the meditation nouns H1900 hagut, H1902H higgayon, and H7881 sichah are OWNER. The inner-speech verbs that most directly carry the interior/exterior-boundary signal are therefore not OWNER in 108. Session D may need to consider whether this ownership pattern is itself part of the gap: the meditation registry holds the noun-form contemplative-state terms while the action-form inner-speech verbs sit in their OWNER registries. Cross-registry ownership of H1897 and H7878 should be confirmed when this pointer is taken up. |

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G5426` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R112 mind · Legacy C-cluster: C01
3. Sub-group: `M15-A` (Wisdom as holistic inner character and orientation)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: research_not_required · Occurrences: 36
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=996` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=not_done`
6. Mounce short-def: to reason,  think, regard, hold an opinion; to set one’s mind on; to have a (certain) attitude
7. Retention note: Legacy evidential_status=confirmed mapped to research_not_required (M29 remap 2026-04-20)

**Verses:** 19 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (2)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1825 | 996-001 | froneō does not name a single faculty but the orientation of the whole inner person — what one is set on, caring about, thinking about as a habitual posture. The Philippians verses are central: "have this mind which is in Christ Jesus" is the paradigmatic use. Reading the verses, 1825 (mind on divine vs earthly) and 1826 (relational/communal mindedness) are two expressions of the same characteristic: froneō as the inner orientation of the whole person, directed toward an object. They belong in one group. |  |
| 1826 | 996-002 | Term names the mind as the seat of communal unity and personal relationship — being of the same mind, sharing concern, pursuing agreement |  |

| 182-F010 | VERSE_PATTERN | Phi 2:2 | The soul is constitutively relational: to God (Psa 42:1, 62:1, 63:1), to other souls (1Sa 18:1, Act 4:32), to its own orientation (dipsuchos — inner division). Soul-to-soul bonding (sumpsuchos, isopsuchos) documents genuine union of orientation between persons — community is possible when souls share direction. |

4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 182
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=4458` · `md_version=2` · `vc_status=vc_completed`
6. Mounce short-def: wise; expert, skilled; (n.) a person who is skilled or expert, often as a class or kind, a wise man or woman

**Verses:** 19 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (3)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2466 | 4458-001 | sofia and sofos in 1 Corinthians 1–3, and in parallel passages, name human wisdom as a category that the cross overturns. God hides things from the wise; the wisdom of the world is folly with God. This is not wisdom inverted by the person claiming it (A-4) but wisdom as a category that stands in structural opposition to the wisdom of God. |  |
| 2467 | 4458-002 | Term names human wisdom as an inner disposition that can be self-deceiving — the danger of wisdom turned inward on itself |  |
| 2476 | 6696-001 | Term names wisdom as a divine attribute — belonging to God and bestowed by him as an inner capacity enabling understanding of mysteries |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (1)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-174-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Pro 1:4; Gen 3:1; Pro 3:7; Isa 52:13 | Registry 174 (wisdom) contains multiple directionally-determined term pairs: (1) a.rum (H6175) and or.mah (H6195) — prudence as cognitive faculty vs. craftiness as moral orientation toward deception (Sub-pattern B); (2) sofos/sofia (G4680/G4678) — wisdom toward God (4458-001, Moral/Conscience character type) vs. wisdom turned inward as self-deception (4458-002, Moral/Conscience distortion); (3) cha.kham (H2449/H2450) — wisdom from fear of Lord (528-003, Spiritual/God-ward) vs. wisdom self-attributed (528-003 note). The cluster reveals that wisdom vocabulary in Scripture is structurally shaped by the direction of the soul's orientation. Session B should treat wisdom terms as directionally-sensitive throughout. |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (2)**

| Finding ID | Type | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-127-002 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Mar 7:21; Rom 14:1 | Dialogismos (G1261) displays the full range of its directionally-determined character in C02: 1081-001 names corrupt inner reasoning originating in the heart (Moral/Conscience) and 1081-002 names the mind's legitimate deliberation on contested matters (Cognitive/Mind). | DIM-160-004 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Psa 139:17; Rom 2:15; Rom 4:9 | Registry 160 (thought) contains three instances of the directionally-determined inner faculty pattern: (1) re.a (H7454) — 3453-001 names thoughts known by God and God's thoughts precious to the psalmist, the same relational faculty operating from both sides of the divine-human boundary simultaneously (Sub-pattern C); (2) dialogismos (G1261) — 3409-001 names inner thoughts as site of spiritual warfare and accusation (Moral/Conscience) and 3410-001 names inner reckoning as the ground of faith (Cognitive/Mind) — same root, opposite orientation; (3) fronimos (G5429) pattern carries from C01: prudence and craftiness as the same perceptive faculty (Sub-pattern B). Session B must treat these as analytically distinct uses of the same terms. |

| DIM-69-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Rom 1:21 | All three gratitude terms converge on Character/Disposition — gratitude is not a transient affective response but a stable inner reorientation of the whole person. <a id="t-H2445"></a>
### H2445 chak.kim — wise (`mti_id=6668`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `H2445` · Lang: Hebrew · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R174 wisdom · Legacy C-cluster: C02
3. Sub-group: `M15-A` (Wisdom as holistic inner character and orientation)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 14
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=6668` · `md_version=2` · `vc_status=vc_completed`

**Verses:** 13 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3609 | 6668-001 | The chak.kim P-status verses all appear in Daniel 2–5. They name "the wise men of Babylon" — the institutional class of court wise men who cannot read divine mysteries. They form the consistent foil: what human wisdom (even at its most professional and prestigious) cannot do, the Spirit-given wisdom in Daniel can. The group's function is structural contrast — it makes visible what Spirit-given wisdom is by showing what it is not. | Created by M15 Phase 7 (sub-group A, analytical code A-13) |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (4 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-111-T2-060 | OBSERVATION | Dan 2:18 | Yes — mercy engages cognition through the knowing-of-need dimension. Dan 2:18 (seeking mercy from God concerning a mystery — OBS-111-061) shows mercy as access to divine knowledge — mercy enables cognitive access that human wisdom cannot achieve. Eze 16:63 ("that you may remember and be confounded" — OBS-111-039) engages the memory-cognitive dimension: received mercy activates retrospective cognition (remembering what was done). OBS-111-039, OBS-111-061. |
| OBS-111-T2-061 | OBSERVATION | Dan 2:18 | Mercy enables cognition in two ways: (1) Access to hidden knowledge — Dan 2:18 mercy-seeking produces revelation of the mystery (OBS-111-061). (2) Self-knowledge — received mercy activates genuine self-knowledge (Eze 16:63 — the shame that follows comprehensive mercy received is grounded in clear-eyed memory of one's own acts, OBS-111-039). *New finding: mercy enables both upward cognition (access to divine knowledge) and downward cognition (accurate self-knowledge) — mercy opens cognitive access in both directions simultaneously.* OBS-111-039, OBS-111-061. |
| OBS-111-T2-062 | OBSERVATION | Dan 2:18 | The cognitive engagement reveals mercy as an epistemically significant characteristic — not merely relational or affective. Mercy received opens the person cognitively: they know more about themselves (self-knowledge through shame) and have access to what they could not know without mercy (divine mystery). This suggests mercy has an inherent cognitive dimension — it clarifies reality rather than obscuring it. OBS-111-039, OBS-111-061. |
| OBS-111-T2-091 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Dan 2:18 | Yes — the supplication vocabulary (*techinah*, *atar*, *chanan* in the prayer sense) represents the human-to-God direction of mercy. Luk 18:13 (publican's mercy-cry, OBS-111-047), Mar 10:48 (Bartimaeus, OBS-111-054), Gen 25:21 (Isaac's prayer, OBS-111-060), Dan 2:18 (Daniel seeking mercy, OBS-111-061), Deu 21:8 (communal prayer for atonement, OBS-111-041). The H8467 *techinah* (24 active verses) and H6279 *atar* (19 active verses) vocabulary is systematically human-to-God petitionary. OBS-111-047, OBS-111-054, OBS-111-060. |

Session B should treat wisdom terms as directionally-sensitive throughout. |
