| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-186-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Psa 34:8 | Ya.tav (H3190) produces three materially distinct dimension patterns: Affective/Emotional (merry heart), Moral/Conscience (moral goodness), and Affective/Emotional (approval/pleasure). This bridges the moral and affective dimensions within a single gladness/goodness term. Session B should examine whether the Hebrew gladness/goodness vocabulary reveals an integration of moral and affective categories that the programme's dimension vocabulary treats separately. |
| OBS-067-T2-001 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Psa 34:8 | The verse evidence reveals that goodness is constitutive of God's character at its most fundamental level. Psa 119:68 ("You are good and do good") establishes an ontological prior — God's being-good precedes and grounds his doing-good. This is not goodness as one attribute among many but as a character description. The doxological assertion group (884-001) — "the Lord is good, his steadfast love endures forever" — names goodness as the summative confession of who God is in his covenantal relationship to creation. Gen 1:31 (884-007) extends this: the Creator's evaluative pronouncement declaring creation "very good" is an expression of his own goodness — he can only declare good what coheres with his character. God's goodness thus images itself in the human person as the capacity for moral assessment, relational orientation, and ontological alignment with what genuinely benefits. The human image-bearer who is good reflects the same character that God is, not by self-generation but by derivation. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-026, OBS-067-OBS-036, OBS-067-OBS-037) |
| OBS-067-T2-002 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Psa 34:8 | Scripture attributes goodness to God extensively and explicitly. Psa 119:68 ("You are good"), Psa 34:8 ("the Lord is good"), Psa 100:5 and Ps 106:1 / 107:1 / 118:1 (doxological refrain "for he is good"), Lam 3:25 ("the Lord is good to those who wait for him"), Rom 11:22 (God's chrēstotēs), Eph 2:7 (God's chrēstotēs in Christ). The attribution reveals that goodness in the human person is not an independent achievement but a participation in and reflection of an attribute that originates in God. Human goodness is derivative by design — it is what being made in the image of the good God looks like in a creature. This has direct significance: the human person who embodies goodness is expressing their created nature; the human person who fails to do so is in a condition of departure from original design. (OBS-067-OBS-001, OBS-067-OBS-006) |
| OBS-067-T2-012 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Psa 34:8 | H2896A (tov): the primary Hebrew term is an adjective of extremely wide semantic range. At the definitional level, it names what is genuinely and concretely beneficial, pleasing, and right — a quality observable in persons, things, actions, words, and states. It does not begin with an abstract ethical definition; it begins with the quality of things that genuinely satisfy and benefit. G0019 (agathōsunē): a NT-era abstract noun derived from agathos (good). At the definitional level, it names goodness as a stable inner virtue — a characterological quality that the NT isolates from the broader agathos adjective to name as a specific inner-being phenomenon. G5544 (chrēstotēs): derived from chrēstos (useful, serviceable, good). At the definitional level it names a relational quality — the disposition of generous goodwill and kindness — that is the outward-facing expression of inner goodness. The three terms thus reveal, even before lexical analysis, that goodness operates at multiple registers: experiential-phenomenological (tov), characterological-virtuous (agathōsunē), and relational-dispositional (chrēstotēs). (OBS-067-OBS-007, OBS-067-OBS-008, OBS-067-OBS-009, OBS-067-OBS-088) |
| OBS-067-T2-023 | VERSE_PATTERN | Psa 34:8 | Three immediate responses are named in the verse evidence: (1) Trust and refuge-taking — Psa 34:8 ("taste and see that the Lord is good; blessed is the man who takes refuge in him"). The immediate response to encountering divine goodness is the reflex of trust. (2) Praise and thanksgiving — 884-001 groups: the doxological refrain as immediate inner response to recognising God's goodness. (3) Receptive desire for instruction — Psa 119:68 ("you are good and do good; teach me your statutes"). The immediate response to recognising God's goodness is the desire to learn his ways — goodness encountered produces a pedagogical receptivity. These three immediate responses are consistent across the Hebrew evidence. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-026, SBF-067-Q004) |
| OBS-067-T2-024 | VERSE_PATTERN | Psa 34:8 | The immediate response varies by context and subject. When the human encounters divine goodness: trust, praise, and receptive desire (as above). When the person encounters not-goodness (their own — Eze 36:31): the immediate response is self-loathing and moral awareness — the recognition of deeds as not-good immediately produces self-directed revulsion. When the person encounters divine chrēstotēs (Rom 2:4): the immediate inner movement is toward repentance — the goodness of God produces a first response of conviction and turning. The variation is structured: different registers of goodness encounter produce different first responses, but each response is coherent with the register. Divine goodness in its fullness produces trust; divine goodness as conviction produces repentance; human not-goodness recognised produces loathing. (OBS-067-OBS-035, OBS-067-OBS-025, SBF-067-Q031) |
| OBS-067-T2-027 | VERSE_PATTERN | Psa 34:8 | Yes — there is a clear distinction. The immediate response to encountering divine goodness is trust/refuge (Psa 34:8) and praise (doxological group). The sustained effect is repositioned inner orientation (Psa 73:28) and community capacity (Rom 15:14). The immediate response is reactive — a first movement of the inner being toward the good encountered. The sustained effect is constitutive — a settled state that reshapes the person's character, orientation, and relational capacity over time. The distinction is most sharply drawn in Psa 73 where Asaph's encounter with divine goodness produces a sustained reorientation rather than a momentary response. 2Th 1:11's "fulfil every resolve for good" confirms the sustained/completion dimension — goodness works toward completion, not just initiation. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-029, SBF-067-Q031) |
| OBS-067-T2-044 | SOMATIC_EVIDENCE | Psa 34:8 | The "glad of heart" (tov-lev) compound at Est 5:9 uses lev (heart), which in Hebrew serves double duty as both the inner-being centre and the physical organ — though the phrase is clearly functioning at the inner-being level, not the physical cardiac level. No other specific body part is explicitly linked to goodness in the anchor verse evidence. The Psa 34:8 "taste and see" metaphor uses perceptual language (taste, see) that implies sensory or quasi-physical engagement, but this is figurative language for inner perception rather than a literal body-part linkage. The data is thin here. (OBS-067-OBS-007, OBS-067-OBS-041) |
| OBS-067-T2-045 | SOMATIC_EVIDENCE | Psa 34:8 | For "glad of heart" (tov-lev, Est 5:9): the heart-compound is emphatic — locating the inner well-being state specifically in the heart gives it a constitutive rather than circumstantial character. For Psa 34:8 ("taste and see"): the perceptual language is experiential and invitational — Scripture is using sensory vocabulary to name a quality of inner encounter that has a quasi-perceptual character. The "tasting" of goodness is not abstract cognition but participatory encounter. Scripture is inviting an embodied-in-principle inner perception — the kind of knowledge that comes from experience rather than inference. Beyond these two instances the somatic evidence is limited. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-041) |
| OBS-067-T2-046 | SOMATIC_EVIDENCE | Psa 34:8 | For the tov-lev (glad of heart) compound: the direction appears to run soul → expression — the inner well-being state manifests as gladness of heart (Est 5:9: Haman "went out joyful and glad of heart" — the inner state expressed in visible disposition). Psa 34:8 "taste and see" runs in the direction: divine goodness → inner perceptual encounter → inner being transformed. The direction is consistently from the divine or inner source outward/downward to embodied expression — not from the body upward to produce goodness. However, the tov-lev instance could also indicate a feedback loop: inner gladness of heart is both an inner state and expressed in visible comportment. The data is insufficient to fully resolve the directionality. (OBS-067-OBS-040, OBS-067-OBS-041) |
| OBS-067-T2-050 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Psa 34:8 | The ultimate origin (God's goodness) does not change. What changes is the delivery mechanism and the register of goodness received. In the Hebrew experiential register (884-003), the origin reaches the person through encounter and nearness. In the covenantal register (884-005), through God's word and promise-keeping. In the NT pneumatological register (885-001, 886-002), through the Spirit's production. These represent a progressive clarification and concentration of how goodness is delivered — from encounter and word in the OT to Spirit-production in the NT — but the source is constant. What shifts is not the origin but the mechanism of delivery and the specificity of naming. The NT's pneumatological precision is a clarification, not a change of origin. (OBS-067-OBS-016, OBS-067-OBS-099) |
| OBS-067-T2-053 | VERSE_ANNOTATION | Psa 34:8 | The perceptive faculty is significantly engaged. Psa 34:8 ("taste and see that the Lord is good") invokes two inner senses simultaneously: taste (a contact-sense implying intimacy and direct experience) and sight (a distance-sense implying recognition and discernment). The use of perceptual language for encountering goodness is deliberate — divine goodness is not known by inference but by experiential perception. The "tasting" of goodness is an inner-perception act: a direct, participatory encounter that produces inner-being knowledge. "Seeing" goodness implies a discernment of its reality. The shared anchor at Psa 34:8 with R49 (discernment) confirms that tasting goodness and exercising discernment are structurally connected (SP-067-007). The perceptive faculty is also engaged in the comparative wisdom mode (884-004): "seeing" which is better requires evaluative perception. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-007) |
| OBS-067-T2-054 | VERSE_PATTERN | Psa 34:8 | Goodness deepens and enables the perceptive faculty. Psa 34:8 presents the encounter with divine goodness as sharpening inner perception — those who "take refuge" and "taste" develop a refined capacity to recognise what is genuinely good. The comparative wisdom mode (884-004) requires and develops discernment — the better-than sayings train the evaluative perception to distinguish inner goods from lesser goods. Conversely, the absence or distortion of goodness impairs perception: Mal 2:17 / Jer 44:17 name the inversion of the moral perception faculty as the condition where evil is called good. This implies that goodness-present sharpens perception; goodness-absent or distorted corrupts it. The perceptive faculty and the goodness-characteristic are mutually reinforcing: genuine goodness cultivates perception; accurate perception recognises genuine goodness. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-021, SBF-067-Q065) |
| OBS-067-T2-055 | VERSE_PATTERN | Psa 34:8 | The perceptive engagement of goodness reveals that it is not primarily an abstract ethical category but an experiential reality that must be encountered and tasted. Goodness is known through contact, not inference. This is structurally consistent with the Hebrew tov's phenomenological root (what is genuinely pleasant, beneficial, and right — experienced as such). The pattern reveals that goodness has an inherently perceptual character — it calls for encounter, not merely assent. The Haman diagnostic (Est 5:9) confirms this: he experienced a surface perception of well-being without genuine perceptual encounter with goodness as rooted in God. Perception without genuine encounter produces the counterfeit. The nature of goodness is therefore inherently experiential and perceptually demanding. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-041, SBF-067-Q088) |
| OBS-067-T2-062 | VERSE_PATTERN | Psa 34:8 | The affective faculty is substantively engaged across multiple groups. The inner well-being group (884-009) is predominantly affective: tov-lev (glad of heart, Est 5:9) and the associated inner state of gladness, prospering, and well-being are emotional-experiential states. The doxological group (884-001) has an affective dimension: praise and thanksgiving are emotional responses to encountering divine goodness. The self-loathing of Eze 36:31 is a negative affective response to recognition of not-goodness. The trust-response to Psa 34:8 has an affective dimension. The experiential group (884-003) as a whole is oriented toward inner satisfaction and well-being — an affective register of goodness. (OBS-067-OBS-029, OBS-067-OBS-035, OBS-067-OBS-041) |
| OBS-067-T2-063 | VERSE_PATTERN | Psa 34:8 | Goodness enables and deepens affect when genuine. Genuine encounter with divine goodness produces trust, praise, and gladness rooted in God — these are authentic affective states produced by the encounter. The comparative wisdom mode also has an affective dimension: the person who genuinely values "better than" outcomes experiences a right ordering of their desires and affections. Distorted goodness impairs affect: Haman's gladness-of-heart (tov-lev, Est 5:9) is genuine affect but immediately destroyed — it is affect without its proper root, which renders it constitutionally fragile. The diagnosis is that goodness properly produces deep, stable, rooted affective states; goodness-mimicry produces shallow, fragile affective states. (OBS-067-OBS-040, OBS-067-OBS-041, OBS-067-OBS-029) |
| OBS-067-T2-086 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Psa 34:8 | Human-to-God operation is evidenced through three modes: (1) Doxological worship — the liturgical refrain "for he is good, his steadfast love endures forever" (group 884-001) is goodness operating as the content of the human's praise and confession toward God. The human declares God's goodness in worship — this is goodness moving upward from the human toward God as acknowledgement and adoration. (2) Seeking and trust — Psa 34:8 ("blessed is the man who takes refuge in him") and Lam 3:25 ("the soul who seeks him"): the human's movement toward God in trust and seeking is enabled by the encounter with divine goodness. Goodness produces the movement toward God. (3) Covenantal request — Psa 119:68 ("you are good and do good; teach me your statutes"): the human addresses God's goodness as the ground for petition. God's goodness is invoked as the basis of the human's request — "because you are good, teach me." (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-026, OBS-067-OBS-033) |
| OBS-067-T2-087 | VERSE_PATTERN | Psa 34:8 | Three inner postures are named for human-toward-God movement in goodness: (1) Waiting — Lam 3:25 ("the Lord is good to those who wait for him"). Waiting is the inner posture of active expectation without premature action — a stance of dependence and trust. (2) Seeking — Lam 3:25 ("the soul who seeks him"). Seeking is directional — the soul oriented actively toward God. (3) Trust and refuge — Psa 34:8 ("blessed is the man who takes refuge in him"): refuge-taking is the inner posture of surrender and reliance. These three postures share a common structure: they all involve the person relinquishing self-sufficiency and directing the inner being toward God. They contrast directly with the closing inner orientations (Q&A-032): self-direction, wilful refusal, and departure from faith. The enabling postures are fundamentally un-self-centred; the blocking postures are fundamentally self-centred. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-033, SBF-067-Q014) |
| OBS-067-T2-088 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Psa 34:8 | The human-to-God direction reveals that the relationship is one of responsive dependence grounded in prior divine initiative. The sequence is always: God's goodness is encountered or declared first → the human moves toward God in trust, praise, and seeking → God sustains and fulfils. The human's movement toward God in goodness is not autonomous spiritual achievement but a response enabled by prior encounter with divine goodness. Psa 119:68's "you are good... teach me" names the relationship structure directly: God's goodness is the basis; human receptive orientation follows. The relationship revealed is one of: initiated by God, received by the human, responded to in trust and worship, sustained in covenantal fidelity. The human's part is not passive but it is always derivative — the human's goodness-orientation toward God is itself produced by God's prior goodness toward the human. (OBS-067-OBS-026, OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-001) |
| OBS-067-T2-101 | VERSE_PATTERN | Psa 34:8 | Two distinct sequences are named. Sequence 1 — positive transformation arc (divine goodness → reception → reorientation): (a) Encounter with divine goodness — Psa 34:8 ("taste and see"); (b) Trust and refuge — "blessed is the man who takes refuge in him"; (c) Stable inner orientation — Psa 73:28 ("it is good to be near God; I have made the Lord God my refuge"); (d) Overflow toward others — "that I may tell of all your works" (Psa 73:28 conclusion). Sequence 2 — moral awareness arc (recognition → repentance → transformation): (a) Memory of deeds — Eze 36:31 ("you will remember your evil ways"); (b) Recognition as not-good — "and your deeds that were not good"; (c) Self-loathing — "you will loathe yourselves"; (d) Movement toward repentance — implied by context (Eze 36:26 God's Spirit given follows 36:31's loathing). Rom 2:4 names the divine chrēstotēs → repentance sequence directly. Both sequences are coherent and traceable in the verse evidence. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-035, OBS-067-OBS-029, SBF-067-Q031) |
| OBS-067-T2-102 | VERSE_PATTERN | Psa 34:8 | The two sequences reveal that goodness works through different mechanisms depending on its register. The positive arc (encounter → trust → reorientation → overflow) reveals that goodness works by producing a progressive deepening of inner orientation — encounter leads to trust, trust leads to stable orientation, orientation leads to outward expression. The characteristic builds from first contact to sustained character. The moral awareness arc (memory → recognition → loathing → repentance) reveals that goodness works through the confrontation with its own absence — the standard of the good, when encountered, produces awareness of not-goodness that is itself a mechanism of transformation. Goodness therefore works both through positive encounter (receiving divine goodness) and through negative recognition (confronting one's absence of goodness). The sequence also reveals the role of time: both arcs are extended through time, not instantaneous — goodness is a formative characteristic, not a momentary experience. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-029, OBS-067-OBS-035) |
| OBS-067-T2-103 | VERSE_PATTERN | Psa 34:8 | Three distinct mechanisms are evidenced, corresponding to the three transformation types identified at Q&A-123: (1) Encounter mechanism — Psa 34:8 / Psa 73:28: the mechanism is perceptual encounter with divine goodness. The change is produced by tasting and seeing — a direct experiential contact that reorients the inner being. This is neither discipline nor gradual formation but encounter-transformation. (2) Spirit-infusion mechanism — Gal 5:22 / Rom 15:14: the Spirit fills the person with goodness. The mechanism is pneumatological infusion — goodness as fruit is the Spirit's work in the inner being. This is not achieved by effort but received as Spirit-gift. (3) Recognition-confrontation mechanism — Eze 36:31 / Rom 2:4: the mechanism is confrontation with one's own not-goodness in the presence of the divine goodness standard. Memory + recognition + loathing = the mechanism of moral transformation toward goodness. All three mechanisms are present; they are not competing but operate in different registers and at different moments. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-035, OBS-067-OBS-039, SBF-067-Q031) |
| OBS-067-T2-104 | VERSE_PATTERN | Psa 34:8 | Yes — the mechanisms are context-differentiated. The encounter mechanism operates in experiential and worship contexts (Psalms — crisis, praise, seeking). The Spirit-infusion mechanism operates in the pneumatological-community context (NT epistolary — Gal, Eph, Rom, Col). The recognition-confrontation mechanism operates in prophetic and apostolic contexts that address moral failure (Eze, Lam, Rom 2). The differentiation is not random — each mechanism is appropriate to its context: the seeking soul receives through encounter; the redeemed community receives through Spirit; the morally blind person is reached through recognition-confrontation. The mechanisms together cover the full range of contexts in which goodness produces transformation. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-035, OBS-067-OBS-039) |
| OBS-067-T2-105 | VERSE_PATTERN | Psa 34:8 | Yes — the goodness-suffering connection is evidenced in two registers (SBF-067-Q048): (1) Divine goodness sustaining in affliction — Lam 3:25 ("the Lord is good to those who wait for him, to the soul who seeks him") is composed in the context of Lamentations — the most concentrated suffering in the Hebrew canon. God's goodness is named as accessible and sustaining precisely in the condition of profound affliction. Suffering does not negate goodness; it becomes the context in which waiting for God's goodness is practised and discovered. (2) Divine goodness available in crisis — Psa 34:8 ("taste and see that the Lord is good") is written by David in the context of his distress — Psalm 34 is a crisis psalm. The invitation to taste goodness is issued from within suffering. The goodness-suffering connection in R67 is not incidental — it is structurally embedded in the anchor verse contexts. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-033, SBF-067-Q048) |
| OBS-067-T2-113 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Psa 34:8 | Two sequential relationships are directly named: (1) Goodness → repentance (Rom 2:4): "God's kindness is meant to lead you to repentance" — chrēstotēs explicitly precedes and produces repentance. The sequence is causal: divine goodness is prior, repentance follows. (2) Goodness → faith/trust (Psa 34:8): encounter with divine goodness ("taste and see that the Lord is good") is followed by "blessed is the man who takes refuge in him" — goodness is the experiential prior that produces the faith/refuge response. Goodness precedes faith in the encounter sequence. A third sequence is implied by SBF-067-Q133 (prior analysis): goodness is prior to faith (encounter with goodness → faith response); 2Th 1:11 shows them as parallel co-products in the other direction. These sequential findings are confirmed and structured. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-043, SBF-067-Q127, SBF-067-Q133) |
| OBS-067-T2-114 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Psa 34:8 | The goodness → repentance sequence (Rom 2:4) is explicitly causal: "is meant to lead to" — divine chrēstotēs is the mechanism producing repentance. The goodness → faith sequence (Psa 34:8) is causal-enabling: the experiential encounter with goodness enables and produces the trust response. The goodness-and-faith parallel sequence (2Th 1:11: "every resolve for good and every work of faith") is correlational — they are co-produced by God's power rather than one causing the other. The three sequential relationships reveal three different structural relationships: causal (goodness produces repentance), enabling-causal (goodness enables faith-response), and correlational (goodness-resolve and faith-work as parallel Spirit-expressions). (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-043, SBF-067-Q127) |
| OBS-067-T2-115 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Psa 34:8 | Two producing relationships are directly evidenced: (1) Goodness produces repentance — Rom 2:4: divine chrēstotēs → repentance (mechanism: moral conviction and drawing). (2) Goodness produces trust/faith — Psa 34:8: encounter with divine goodness → trust-response (mechanism: experiential encounter enabling faith). A third is implied: goodness produces praise — the doxological group (884-001) is the response to divine goodness; the encounter produces the praise. These are all divine-to-human sequences: God's goodness produces these characteristics in the human inner being. The human expression of goodness in community (Rom 15:14) produces instruction-capacity in others — goodness produces formation-capacity. (OBS-067-OBS-025, OBS-067-OBS-043, SBF-067-Q004) |
| OBS-067-T2-124 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Psa 34:8 | Shared anchors are extensively documented (§6 of data package): Gen 1:31 shared with experience/R58; Est 5:9 shared with joy/R97; Psa 34:8 shared with hope/R78 and discernment/R49; Pro 16:32 shared with patience/R116, anger/R4, dominion/R199; Eze 36:31 shared with abomination/R1; Mic 6:8 shared with love/R103, kindness/R99, will/R173, humility/R80, compassion/R23, condemnation/R24 (6 registries); Gal 5:22 shared with love/R103, patience/R116, joy/R97, peace/R117, faith/R59 (5 registries). Total of 7 anchor verses shared across multiple registries. (OBS-067-OBS-021, SBF-067-Q138) |
| OBS-067-T2-125 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Psa 34:8 | Mic 6:8 (shared with 6 registries) reveals that goodness, justice, kindness, love, humility, compassion, and will are not independently operating characteristics that happen to co-appear — they are co-constitutive elements of a single comprehensive moral orientation. Mic 6:8 functions as the programme's primary cross-registry synthesis node for the moral-character cluster. Gal 5:22 (shared with 5 registries) reveals that goodness, love, joy, peace, patience, and faith are co-produced facets of Spirit-formed character — the Spirit-fruit cluster is a programme-defined synthesis unit. Psa 34:8 (shared with discernment/R49 and hope/R78) reveals that divine goodness, inner discernment, and hope are structurally connected at the moment of encounter — tasting goodness produces both discernment (seeing clearly) and hope (taking refuge). Each shared anchor names a structural relationship between the registries that share it, not merely a coincidence of vocabulary. (OBS-067-OBS-021, OBS-067-OBS-025, SBF-067-Q138, SP-067-004, SP-067-005, SP-067-007) |
| OBS-067-T2-129 | ETYMOLOGY | Psa 34:8 | H2896A (tov): root TOV with 10 sub-senses (SBF-067-Q088) — aesthetic pleasantness (1a), higher-nature pleasantness (1b), intellectual goodness (1h), gladness/well-being (1g), ethical rightness (1j). Conceptual origin is phenomenological: begins with concrete experience of what is agreeable and beneficial, rises to moral and covenantal categories. The root meaning reveals that goodness is not first an abstract ethical standard — it is first the quality of things that genuinely satisfy and benefit, recognisable in creation and in proximity to God. G0019 (agathōsunē): root AGATHOS — the classical Greek adjective for that which is functionally good/useful. The abstract noun suffix (-syne) creates a virtue-noun — naming goodness as a stable inner quality, not merely a predicate. The root reveals the NT's intentional move from adjective (applied to many things) to abstract virtue (naming a specific inner characteristic). G5544 (chrēstotēs): root CHRĒSTOS — serviceable, useful, good in a relational-functional sense. The root reveals a relational quality: what is useful and generous toward another. The NT elevates this from horizontal utility (serviceable) to vertical character (Spirit-produced generosity of disposition). (OBS-067-OBS-007, OBS-067-OBS-008, OBS-067-OBS-009, SBF-067-Q088) |
| OBS-067-T2-131 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Psa 34:8 | H2896A (tov) has the widest semantic range of any OWNER term in this registry: aesthetic pleasantness, moral rightness, covenantal reliability, comparative excellence, experiential benefit, evaluative good, ontological goodness (Gen 1 creation pronouncement), volitional preference, and inner well-being. The 12 distinct modes identified at Q&A-022 correspond to 12 distinct positions within this range. The breadth is confirmed by the 10 sub-senses of the TOV root and the 9 distinct H2896A groups. G0019 (agathōsunē): narrow range — goodness as Spirit-produced inner virtue/resolve (4 NT occurrences, all Pauline, all pneumatological). G5544 (chrēstotēs): medium range — kindness/goodness/gentleness as inner character quality and divine attribute (7 occurrences). The semantic range across the three OWNER terms covers breadth (H2896A's 12 modes) and precision (G0019 and G5544's specialised NT meanings). (OBS-067-OBS-007, OBS-067-OBS-008, OBS-067-OBS-009, OBS-067-OBS-048) |
| OBS-067-T2-138 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Psa 34:8 | The full vocabulary arc from TOV to agathōsunē to chrēstotēs reveals a three-stage development: (1) H2896A (Hebrew breadth) — goodness as a pervasive quality covering the full range of what is genuinely beneficial, right, and satisfying: aesthetic, moral, covenantal, volitional, experiential, evaluative, and ontological. This is goodness at its maximum semantic reach. (2) G0019 (NT precision — inner virtue) — goodness concentrated into a named Spirit-produced virtue: a characterological inner quality of the Spirit-filled person. From 10 sub-senses to one named category. (3) G5544 (NT precision — relational disposition) — goodness concentrated into its relational-dispositional expression: the generous goodwill that characterises both God's stance toward humanity and the Spirit-produced stance of the believer toward others. The arc is: phenomenological breadth (Hebrew) → virtuous precision (NT abstract noun) → relational precision (NT dispositional noun). The complete semantic range encompasses all three stages — they are not replacements but cumulative layers of specification. (OBS-067-OBS-007, OBS-067-OBS-008, OBS-067-OBS-009, OBS-067-OBS-048, SBF-067-Q099) |
| OBS-067-T2-140 | VERSE_ANNOTATION | Psa 34:8 | The anchor verse corpus spans multiple literary forms, each with distinct interpretive requirements: (1) Prophetic address (Mic 6:8; Eze 36:31): legal covenant-speech, including divine accusation and requirement. Mic 6:8 functions as the verdict of a covenant lawsuit (rib) — God's requirement is stated as the standard against which Israel's failure is measured. Requires: attention to covenantal frame; the "requirement" is not a new imposition but a restoration of what was always known. (2) Wisdom sayings (Pro 15:16; 16:32): comparative instruction for individual moral formation. Requires: attention to the rhetorical purpose of persuasion toward wise living; the better-than structure is pedagogical. (3) Psalms of lament and praise (Psa 34:8; 73:28; 34:8 — crisis context; Psa 119:68 — torah psalm): personal address and corporate declaration. Requires: attention to the experiential context from which the declaration arises. (4) NT epistolary (Rom 2:4; 11:22; Gal 5:22; Col 3:12): doctrinal instruction and paraenesis. Requires: attention to the theological argument in which the verse is embedded. (OBS-067-OBS-027, OBS-067-OBS-042, SBF-067-Q042) |
| OBS-067-T2-142 | VERSE_ANNOTATION | Psa 34:8 | The anchor verse corpus spans a notably broad contextual range: (1) Covenantal-judicial setting (Mic 6:8 — covenant lawsuit; Jos 23:14 — covenant farewell address): the primary human moral goodness verse is set in a judicial-covenantal frame — goodness is the standard of covenant obligation. (2) Liturgical-doxological setting (Psa 34:8; 119:68; doxological refrain): corporate worship and individual prayer as the context for declaring and encountering divine goodness. (3) Wisdom-instructional setting (Pro 15:16; 16:32): educational and formational context for human moral development. (4) Prophetic-restorative setting (Eze 36:31): post-exilic restoration context where goodness is encountered through recognition of its absence. (5) Pauline-pneumatological setting (Gal 5:22; Col 3:12): community formation in the new covenant context. The breadth of contextual settings confirms that goodness is not restricted to a single register of life — it operates across judicial, liturgical, instructional, prophetic, and community contexts. (OBS-067-OBS-027, OBS-067-OBS-042, SBF-067-Q042) |

<a id="t-G3540"></a>
### G3540 noēma — mind/thought (`mti_id=1188`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G3540` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R160 thought · Legacy C-cluster: C02
3. Sub-group: `M15-G` (Inner thought-content — the mind's formed thoughts)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 7
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=1188` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=not_done`
6. Mounce short-def: mind, thought, mind; scheme, design, plot

**Verses:** 6 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1748 | 1188-001 | Term names the inner faculty of thought — the mind's contents and its susceptibility to hardening, blinding, or corrupting by spiritual forces |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (21 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-117-T2-010 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | Yes — and structurally so. The OT vocabulary names peace as a condition to be given, a covenant to be kept, a king to come. The NT vocabulary names peace as a person present: G1515 group 426-003 (Eph 2:14 — "he himself is our peace," OBS-117-122 from prior session). The Greek eirēnē carries all the semantic range of shalom (established by the LXX translation pattern) and then names Christ as the one in whom all those dimensions converge. The NT fullness: (1) Peace with God (Rom 5:1) — the relational condition; (2) Peace of God guarding hearts and minds (Phili 4:7) — the inner condition; (3) Christ IS peace (Eph 2:14) — the person; (4) Spirit-fruit peace (Gal 5:22) — the gift; (5) cosmic peace-making through the blood of the cross (Col 1:20, G1517 group 430-001) — the mechanism. The OT's four shalom dimensions (felt state, relational condition, divine gift, eschatological reality) are not rejected in the NT but fulfilled in a single person. |
| OBS-117-T2-013 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | Strong continuity. The LXX translates shalom with eirēnē throughout the Hebrew Bible, establishing the semantic correspondence as the foundational datum of the NT peace vocabulary. The NT eirēnē carries the full shalom range: (1) Inner felt state (Phili 4:7 — "the peace of God... will guard your hearts and your minds," corresponding to shalom 2508-001); (2) Covenantal gift (Rom 5:1 — "peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ," corresponding to 2508-002); (3) Moral orientation (Eirēneuō, G1514 — "live peaceably with all," corresponding to 2508-003); (4) Messianic embodiment (Eph 2:14 — "he himself is our peace," corresponding to 2508-004). The correspondence is not merely semantic but structural — the NT's peace vocabulary is the OT's shalom vocabulary transposed into the key of Christ. No significant discontinuity; the NT deepens rather than revises. |
| OBS-117-T2-029 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | Yes — as established in Q&A-020. The data shows: absent (Lam 3:17 — soul bereft), present (Psa 4:8 — lying down in peace), great (Psa 119:165 — great peace for law-lovers), perfect (Isa 26:3 — shalom shalom for the trust-directed mind), eschatological-unlimited (Isa 9:7 — no end to his peace). The degrees are not always explicitly graduated but the comparative constructions confirm graduation is present: "great peace" (rav shalom) implies more-than-ordinary peace; shalom-shalom (doubled) implies maximal completeness. The NT adds: "peace of God which surpasses all understanding" (Phili 4:7) — the eschatological peace exceeds cognitive categories entirely. |
| OBS-117-T2-031 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | Yes — and the eschatological fullness is among the most extensively documented in the programme's data. Evidence: (1) H7965G group 2508-004 (DHC) — the messianic reign of peace: "of the increase of his government and of peace there will be no end" (Isa 9:7); (2) Eph 2:14 (G1515 426-003) — Christ IS peace, achieved once-for-all through the blood of the cross (Col 1:20); (3) Phili 4:7 — the peace that surpasses all understanding as the eschatological reality experienced in anticipation; (4) The covenant of peace that shall not be removed (Isa 54:10, Eze 37:26) — the eternal character of eschatological shalom; (5) Cosmic peace at the Lamb's seventh seal (Rev 8:1 — sigē, silence in heaven — G4602) — the eschatological threshold of total cosmic shalom. The present attainment of shalom (Isa 26:3, Psa 4:8) is real but partial; the eschatological fullness (Isa 9:7, Eph 2:14, Phili 4:7) is the unrestricted reality toward which the present experience points. |
| OBS-117-T2-040 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | Yes — with rich evidence. Key heart locations: (1) Deu 29:19 (2509-002 anchor, OBS-117-150) — "blesses himself in his heart [lev]" claiming shalom while walking in "stubbornness of my heart" — false peace is a heart-generated claim; the heart is the seat of the false shalom; (2) 1Ch 12:17 (2510-001 anchor, OBS-117-151) — "my heart will be joined to you" — the shalom of friendship is a heart-bond; (3) Joh 14:27 — "Let not your hearts [kardia] be troubled" — Christ's peace addresses the heart directly; the troubled heart and the peace are antithetical (G1515 426-001 anchor, prior session OBS-117-120); (4) Phili 4:7 — "the peace of God... will guard your hearts [kardia] and your minds" — the heart is the primary guarded territory; (5) Pro 12:20 — "those who plan peace have joy" — planning peace is a heart-act (prior session OBS-117-096 noted the heart-disposition of planning); (6) Psa 28:3 — "speak peace with their neighbors while evil is in their hearts" — heart as the locus of authentic or false orientation toward peace. The data shows 33 co-occurring verses with R183 (heart) — the highest co-occurrence count in the entire registry (G.2, OBS-117-016). |
| OBS-117-T2-041 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | The heart-location of peace engages all four of the heart's integrating functions: (1) Knowing (cognitive): Rom 8:6 ("to set the mind/heart on the Spirit is life and peace") — the heart's cognitive orientation toward God produces peace; the directed heart knows what produces peace; (2) Willing (volitional): Psa 120:7 ("I am for peace") — the heart's volitional orientation toward peace as identity; Isa 30:15 ("but you were unwilling") — the refusal of peace is a heart-act of will; (3) Feeling (affective): Joh 14:27 ("let not your hearts be troubled") — the troubled heart as the experiential opposite of peace; the heart as the seat of distress or rest; (4) Moral awareness (conscience): Deu 29:19 — the heart's stubbornness as the moral condition that generates false shalom; Psa 28:3 — evil in the heart contrasted with speaking peace. The heart is the integrating centre of peace's constitutional address: peace must be received, held, directed, and expressed from the heart. The heart-peace connection is the most comprehensive single-location engagement in the data. |
| OBS-117-T2-042 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | Yes — explicitly and centrally. The primary mind-location: Isa 26:3 ("You keep him in perfect peace whose mind [yetser — formed thought/disposition] is stayed on you, because he trusts in you") — OBS-117-144. This is the most direct mind-peace statement in the corpus: perfect peace (shalom shalom) is the condition of the mind that is directed toward and stayed upon God. The yetser is the formed inclination or disposition of the mind — its sustained directedness. In the NT: Phili 4:7 — "the peace of God... will guard your hearts and your minds [noema — thoughts, understanding]" — the mind is jointly named as the territory peace guards alongside the heart; Rom 8:6 — "to set the mind [phronema] on the Spirit is life and peace" — the mind's orientation determines whether peace is present. The mind is the second major constitutional location of peace after the soul, and the most explicitly causal: directing the mind toward God produces peace; directing the mind elsewhere forfeits it. |
| OBS-117-T2-043 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | The mind-peace connection primarily engages the mind's directional function — the capacity of the mind to fix itself upon an object. Isa 26:3 (yetser) — the "stayed" or "fixed" mind: peace requires the mind to be anchored, not drifting. The mental act that produces peace is not thought-content (what the mind thinks about) but mental orientation (what the mind is stayed upon). Rom 8:6 (phronema — the mind's settled disposition/inclination) — the mind's habitual orientation toward the Spirit produces peace; toward the flesh produces death. Phili 4:7 (noema — the products of thought, understanding) — the mind's thoughts are what peace guards: peace stands sentinel over the mind's outputs. Three aspects: (1) Orientation (Isa 26:3) — where the mind is directed; (2) Disposition (Rom 8:6) — what the mind is habitually inclined toward; (3) Output (Phili 4:7) — the contents of thought that peace protects. The mind's peace is a total engagement — orientation, disposition, and output are all in view. |
| OBS-117-T2-055 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | Yes — and this is the most analytically significant T2 finding for the peace registry. The constitutional movement of peace is extensively documented, operating in multiple directions: (1) Spirit → soul → body (downward cascade under loss of peace): The absence of peace radiates from the inner person outward: Deu 28:65 — peacelessness produces trembling heart (volitional/heart level) + failing eyes (body level) + languishing soul (soul level); Psa 32:3 — suppressed inner condition → bones wasting (body); Psa 38:3 — sin disrupting inner peace → no soundness in flesh, no health in bones. The movement under un-peace: inner disorder → somatic deterioration. (2) God → spirit → soul → body (downward cascade under the gift of peace): Gal 5:22 (Spirit produces peace as fruit) → Phili 4:7 (peace guards hearts and minds) → Psa 4:8 (peace enables the body to lie down and sleep). The gift moves from its divine source through the spirit/Spirit level into the heart and mind, and from there into somatic rest. (3) Body → inner (the redemptive reversal): Isa 53:5 — the Servant's wounded body → peace received in others' inner being; the movement against the normal direction, accomplished through the cross. |
| OBS-117-T2-056 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | Three patterns are identifiable: (1) Cascading loss pattern: inner peace → somatic peace is the normal condition; inner dis-peace → somatic deterioration follows the causal chain inner-to-body (Psa 32:3, 38:3, Deu 28:65). The sequence is consistent: first the inner person is disordered (by sin, by judgment, by absent trust), then the body registers the disorder. The body is the last to fail and the indicator of deep inner trouble. (2) Cascading gift pattern: God gives peace (Num 6:26, Joh 14:27) → heart and mind receive it (Phili 4:7, Isa 26:3) → body enacts it (Psa 4:8 — lying down in sleep). The sequence moves from divine source through inner-being faculties into somatic expression. The body's rest is the downstream expression of the inner peace received. (3) Redemptive reversal pattern: suffering body of the Servant (Isa 53:5) → inner peace produced in others; this reversal moves counter to the normal direction and is unique in the corpus. The three patterns together establish peace as a constitutionally mobile characteristic — it does not reside statically in one location but flows through the whole person, and flows in different directions under different conditions. |
| OBS-117-T2-060 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | Yes — centrally. The cognitive engagement of peace is established in T2.4: Isa 26:3 (yetser — the formed mental disposition/thought, "whose mind is stayed on you"), Rom 8:6 (phronema — mind's settled orientation), Phili 4:7 (noema — thoughts guarded by peace). The cognitive faculty is both the condition for peace (mind stayed on God → peace) and the territory peace guards (peace guards thoughts/minds). Two-directional engagement: cognition produces peace when rightly directed; peace guards cognition when received. |
| OBS-117-T2-067 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | Peace enables positive affect and resolves negative affect. Evidence: (1) Judg 6:23 — "Peace be to you. Do not fear" — divine peace directly displaces terror; (2) Est 9:22 — "sorrow turned into gladness and mourning into a holiday" (in the context of Purim rest/peace); (3) 1Sa 1:17-18 — Hannah went away and "her face was no longer sad" after Eli's peace-blessing; (4) Phili 4:7 — peace guards the heart against anxiety (Phili 4:6 context: "do not be anxious about anything... and the peace of God will guard"). Peace is the affective resolution of anxiety, fear, and sorrow. Its sustained presence does not bypass affect — peace-bearing people still weep, still feel — but it prevents affect from becoming debilitating or destructive. The person in peace can feel sorrow without being overwhelmed by it. |
| OBS-117-T2-068 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | The affective engagement reveals that peace is not emotional neutrality or the suppression of feeling but the condition in which affect functions rightly — neither overwhelming the person (anxiety, terror) nor being inaccessible to them (the peaceless Lam 3:17 — "forgotten what happiness is"). Peace is the affective ground: the person who has shalom can feel the full range of affect without being destroyed by negative affect or alienated from positive affect. This is a crucial distinction for the programme: peace is not the absence of feeling but the condition in which feeling is held and expressed rightly. |
| OBS-117-T2-113 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | All four relationships appear: (1) Produces: Heb 12:11 — discipline produces peaceful fruit in those trained; Isa 38:17 — bitterness was the path to welfare; suffering is the generative mechanism in certain contexts; (2) Tests: Lam 3:17 — the soul bereft of peace is the person whose peace is tested to its limit; the sustained peacelessness under catastrophe tests whether the person's peace has deep roots or is situational; (3) Reveals: the person who maintains peace through suffering reveals a peace grounded in God rather than circumstance (Phili 4:7 — "surpasses all understanding" suggests it transcends circumstantial explanation); (4) Deepens: the retrospective memory of peace-through-suffering (Isa 38:17) deepens understanding of God's ways; suffering that produces peace creates a richer experiential knowledge of shalom than suffering-free peace. The paradox of T1 (Q&A-023) is confirmed here: suffering is not peace's enemy but its formative companion in the data. |
| OBS-117-T2-116 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | Yes — the eschatological trajectory of peace is the most extensively documented in the programme's data. Established in T1 (Q&A-032): Isa 9:7 ("no end to his peace"), Isa 54:10 (eternal covenant of peace), Jer 29:11 (future and hope), Psa 72:7 (peace abounding till the moon be no more), Isa 32:18 (people dwelling in peaceful habitation), Isa 66:12 (peace extended like a river), Eph 2:14 (accomplished in Christ), Phili 4:7 (surpassing all understanding), Rev 8:1 (cosmic silence at the seventh seal). The eschatological trajectory is not merely hope but structurally grounded in what Christ has accomplished — the eschatological fullness has been inaugurated, not only anticipated. |
| OBS-117-T2-117 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | Present peace is explicitly described as anticipatory: (1) Phili 4:7 — "the peace of God which surpasses all understanding will guard your hearts and minds" — the present peace is already beyond cognitive comprehension but not yet the fullness; (2) Rom 5:1 — "peace with God" as the present justified state is the foretaste of the eschatological reconciliation; (3) 2Th 1:7 — "relief to you who are afflicted when the Lord Jesus is revealed" — present peace under affliction anticipates the eschatological relief-from-all-affliction; (4) Rev 8:1 — the half-hour silence in heaven is the eschatological fullness of the demamah-silence pattern (1Ki 19:12) — the present silence before God's voice anticipates the cosmic silence before the final unveiling. Present peace is a genuine foretaste of future fullness, not merely a symbol — it is the same peace (peace of God, Phili 4:7) experienced partially rather than a different or lesser peace. |
| OBS-117-T2-118 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | From §G.2 co-occurrence data (OBS-117-016): Top 10 co-occurring registries by shared verse count: R183 heart (33), R187 strength (28), R103 love (26), R43 desire (24), R197 authority (24), R61 fear (23), R78 hope (21), R97 joy (21), R51 distress (19), R182 soul (18). The top five by verse count: heart, strength, love, desire, authority. The quantitative data is confirmed by the anchor verse analysis: Isa 30:15 involves strength (R187); Phili 4:7 involves heart (R183) and mind (R112); Gal 5:22 involves love (R103), joy (R97); Deu 28:65 involves fear (R61), distress (R51). The distress and fear co-occurrences are expected (peace-by-contrast); the strength, love, and joy co-occurrences are more structurally significant. |
| OBS-117-T2-119 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | The co-occurrence pattern reveals that peace is a central hub characteristic — it co-occurs with both positive characteristics (joy, love, hope, strength) and negative ones (fear, distress, soul in crisis). This hub position reflects peace's nature as an integrative condition: it is the ground from which positive characteristics flower and the condition whose absence is accompanied by negative inner states. The heart co-occurrence (33 — highest) confirms T2.3's finding that the heart is the primary constitutional location and the most comprehensive relational address of peace. The strength co-occurrence (28) confirms the Isa 30:15 structural finding: peace and strength are causally related (quiet trust → strength). The soul co-occurrence (18) confirms T2.2. The overall picture: peace is not peripheral but structurally central in the inner-being ecosystem. |
| OBS-117-T2-131 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | Yes — extensively. §G.3 lists 59 shared anchor relationships across 43 other registries (OBS-117-017). Key structural anchors shared: Isa 30:15 (R76, R163, R187), Gal 5:22 (R59, R67, R97, R103, R116), Deu 28:65 (R4, R33, R102, R151), Isa 53:5 (R30, R62, R151), Isa 9:6 (R175, R199), Joh 14:27 (R61), 1Pe 3:4 (R80), Phili 4:7 (R183), Psa 32:3 (R187), Jer 29:11 (R78, R112), 1Ch 12:17 (R40, R167), Deu 29:19 (R153, R194). The shared anchor network is among the largest in the programme — 59 relationships across 43 registries. |
| OBS-117-T2-132 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Phili 4:7 | The highest-priority shared anchors for Session D, selected by analytical significance: (1) Isa 30:15 shared with R76 (holiness), R163 (trust), R187 (strength) — the most structurally dense shared anchor: it names peace-as-quietness as the instrument producing strength, within a salvation context initiated by "the Holy One of Israel." Three registries share this anchor, suggesting Session D will find a major structural cluster here (SP-117-012); (2) Gal 5:22 shared with 5 registries — the Spirit-fruit cluster; the question is constitutive interdependence (SP-117-015); (3) Isa 53:5 shared with R30 (contrition), R62 (fellowship), R151 (sorrow) — the Servant's peace-purchasing suffering connects peace with contrition and sorrow as the human experience surrounding the Servant's act; (4) Phili 4:7 shared with R183 (heart) — peace as the guardian of the heart; (5) Deu 29:19 shared with R153 (stubbornness) and R194 (blessing) — false-shalom connected to stubbornness (Session D structural finding). |
| OBS-117-T2-142 | OBSERVATION | Phili 4:7 | The LXX's consistent rendering of sha.lom with eirēnē is the foundational datum: the translation decision imports sha.lom's full semantic range into the Greek word, enabling the NT to use eirēnē with full shalom resonance. This is not a gap (the LXX-translation relationship is established and confirmed). However: the data flags NO_WORD_ANALYSIS for some terms, and LSJ (Greek lexicon) data is absent for the Greek terms in the readiness output — the §D lexical section showed no LSJ entries for the Greek eirēnē family. This means the LXX-development analysis is based on the translation relationship rather than full lexicographic comparison. Gap: a formal LXX analysis comparing sha.lom-eirēnē translation patterns (where sha.lom is not translated as eirēnē, and where eirēnē translates something other than sha.lom) would sharpen the boundary. This is beyond the current data's scope. |

### G0801 asunetos — senseless (`mti_id=1202`)

**Identity & meaning**

2. Registry: R166 understanding · Legacy C-cluster: C02
3. Sub-group: `M15-B` (Understanding as inner perceptive faculty)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 10
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=1202` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=not_done`
6. Mounce short-def: senseless, dull, without understanding, foolish

**Verses:** 5 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |

| 2338 | 1202-001 | Senselessness as an inner condition — failure of understanding as a heart-state |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (21 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-69-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Rom 1:21 | All three gratitude terms converge on Character/Disposition — gratitude is not a transient affective response but a stable inner reorientation of the whole person. The absence of gratitude as a diagnostic marker of inner corruption (5480-001) is a particularly significant finding. Session B should examine gratitude as a diagnostic indicator of inner-being health. |
| OBS-111-T2-008 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 1:31 | Mercilessness (*aneleēmōn*, *anileōs*) appears in vice catalogues marking constitutional inner collapse (Rom 1:31 — OBS-111-034; Jam 2:13 — OBS-111-070). The absence of mercy is not merely virtue-failure but image-defacement — the inner person has lost the capacity that most visibly reflects the divine character. Conversely, the Beatitude (Mat 5:7) and the new humanity character list (Col 3:12) present mercy as the marker of those in whom the image is being restored. OBS-111-034, OBS-111-032, OBS-111-069, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-111-T2-017 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | Two lexically explicit structural opposites: (1) *aneleēmōn* (G0415 — merciless/ruthless) in the moral-collapse catalogue (Rom 1:31, OBS-111-034); (2) *anileōs* (G0448 — without mercy) in the mercy-judgment contrast (Jam 2:13, OBS-111-070); (3) H3819 *lo ruchamah* (No Mercy — the prophetic name embodying mercy's withdrawal, Hos 1:6, OBS-111-058). The structural opposite operates at three levels: the inner character failure (mercilessness as constitution), the relational disposition (without mercy toward another), and the divine withdrawal (No Mercy as prophetic verdict). OBS-111-034, OBS-111-058, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-111-T2-036 | SPIRIT_SOUL_BODY | Rom 1:31 | If the spirit-soul interface classification is correct, mercy sits at the deepest level of the person — the interface between the created spirit (the capacity for relationship with God) and the soul (the personal self). This would explain mercy's dual orientation: toward God (spirit-level connection with the divine mercy that is its source) and toward others (soul-level expression in relational life). The classification also explains why mercilessness appears as constitutional collapse (Rom 1:31 — OBS-111-034) rather than merely behavioral failure — it names a spirit-level deficiency. OBS-111-034, OBS-111-051. |
| OBS-111-T2-067 | OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | Mercy deepens affect in both the seeking and receiving directions: seeking mercy is accompanied by intensified emotional expression (breast-beating, persistent cry against opposition); received mercy produces affective depth (joy, shame, wonder, silence). The mercilessness catalogue (Rom 1:31 — OBS-111-034) pairs mercilessness with *astorgos* (heartless/affectionless) — the absence of mercy is associated with affective incapacity. Mercy thus enables affect; its absence impairs it. OBS-111-034, OBS-111-047, OBS-111-054. |
| OBS-111-T2-068 | OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | Affect is mercy's native register — mercy is felt before it is expressed, and received mercy produces felt response. The affective engagement is constitutive of mercy: a mercy that leaves no affective trace is not fully mercy in the biblical sense. Mercilessness as affective incapacity (Rom 1:31) confirms this — the loss of mercy is inseparable from the loss of felt inner connection to the other. OBS-111-034, OBS-111-039, OBS-111-047. |
| OBS-111-T2-071 | OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | Mercy enables volition in the receiver: received mercy does not constrain future choice but expands the moral capacity to choose mercy toward others. Mat 18:33 implies that received mercy should have generated the volitional capacity to show mercy — the failure is in not exercising this enabled volition. Conversely, mercilessness (Rom 1:31 — OBS-111-034) appears alongside faithlessness — the merciless person's volitional capacity is compromised. *New finding: mercy is a volitional enabler — received mercy expands the person's capacity to make merciful choices; mercilessness marks a contracted volitional capacity for compassionate action.* OBS-111-034, OBS-111-055. |
| OBS-111-T2-083 | OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | Mercy deepens conscientiousness: the person who has received mercy and understands what it cost (atonement through blood, Lev 17:11 — OBS-111-037) has a deeper moral seriousness about the wrongdoing that required mercy. Mercilessness impairs conscientiousness — the merciless catalogue (Rom 1:31 — OBS-111-034) pairs mercilessness with moral recklessness. OBS-111-034, OBS-111-037, OBS-111-055. |
| OBS-111-T2-138 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | Yes — three terms: *aneleēmōn* (G0415 — merciless, Rom 1:31), *anileōs* (G0448 — without mercy, Jam 2:13), H3819 *lo ruchamah* (No Mercy — prophetic name, Hos 1:6). The structural opposite is lexically explicit at the character, relational, and prophetic-identity levels. OBS-111-014, OBS-111-034, OBS-111-058, OBS-111-070. |
| OBS-034-T2-008 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 1:31 | The verse evidence provides clear indicators. Presence of covenant-faithfulness: in the human, keeping covenant is an expression of moral integrity (3308-002 — OBS-034-053), alignment with God's own character (Deu 7:9 — OBS-034-039), and is associated with knowing God and living under his blessing. Absence or corruption: G0802 (asunthetos — covenant-breaking as inner character defect — OBS-034-018, OBS-034-057) appears in Paul's catalogue of the debased mind (Rom 1:31) — the morally degraded human who has rejected God becomes incapable of covenant faithfulness. Dan 9:11 (OBS-034-036) frames collective covenant breach as the inner moral failure of a people. The pattern: covenant faithfulness indexes the health of the divine image in the person; covenant-breaking indexes its degradation. The person who cannot keep covenant is described in terms of inner moral collapse. |
| OBS-034-T2-017 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | The structural opposite of covenant is covenant-breaking — the inner disposition and act of faithlessness that violates and severs the covenant bond. The data names this explicitly: G0802 asunthetos (untrustworthy, covenant-breaking — OBS-034-018, OBS-034-057) is Paul's term for the morally debased person who cannot hold covenants together. H0423 alah (oath/curse — OBS-034-007, OBS-034-036) shows that breach activates the self-invoked curse embedded in the oath — covenant-breaking is the self-destruction of the bond one has made. H3748 keritut (divorce as cutting of the marriage covenant — OBS-034-012, OBS-034-043) names the formal opposite: the "cut" that severs the covenant bond. Dan 9:11 (OBS-034-036) frames collective covenant breach as inner moral failure — transgression, turning aside, refusing to obey, sinning. The opposite is therefore: faithlessness (the character quality), covenant-breaking (the act), and divorce/cutting-off (the formal result). At the deepest level: the structural opposite of covenant is the severing of self from the relational bond through infidelity. |
| OBS-034-T2-018 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | From the verse evidence: (1) Covenant explicitly excludes false covenants — making covenants with Canaanite inhabitants (3304-003 — OBS-034-048) is prohibited as a binding to false loyalties that misdirects inner allegiance away from God. (2) Covenant resists unfaithfulness — the curse embedded in the oath (H0423 — OBS-034-007) is the built-in mechanism resisting breach. (3) Covenant excludes the hardened-mind/veiled cognitive condition (2 Cor 3:14 — OBS-034-072) — the old covenant produces a veil; the new covenant resists and removes cognitive hardening. (4) Covenant resists the inner condition of alienation and hopelessness (Eph 2:12 — OBS-034-060) — covenant inclusion is the antidote to covenantal exclusion/desolation. In summary: covenant excludes false loyalty, resists infidelity, opposes cognitive hardening, and displaces inner alienation. |
| OBS-034-T2-029 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 1:31 | The verse evidence identifies blocking conditions: (1) Hardened mind — 2 Cor 3:14: "their minds were hardened. For to this day, when they read the old covenant, that same veil remains unlifted" (OBS-034-072). Cognitive hardening blocks the reception of the covenant's transformative content — the veil prevents seeing through the old covenant to Christ. (2) Stubborn/evil heart — Jer 11:8: "everyone walked in the stubbornness of his evil heart... they did not obey or incline their ear" (group 765-002 context). Stubbornness and inclination of the will away from God blocks reception. (3) Covenant-breaking character — G0802 asunthetos: the person whose inner character is faithlessness is already disposed against covenant-reception (OBS-034-057). (4) False covenantal allegiance — making covenants with the wrong parties (3304-003 — OBS-034-048) misdirects the will and blocks genuine covenantal reception with God. (5) The veil of the old covenant (2 Cor 3:14-16 — OBS-034-072) — the old covenant itself becomes a blocking mechanism when read without Christ. |
| OBS-034-T2-030 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 1:31 | The data provides a clear picture: (1) Desolation — Eph 2:12: "having no hope and without God in the world" (OBS-034-060). The person who encounters the covenants of promise and remains a stranger to them lives in inner desolation — hopelessness, alienation, godlessness. (2) Bondage — Gal 4:24-25 (OBS-034-071): remaining under the Sinai covenant without entering the new covenant freedom is described as bearing children for slavery — the inner condition is one of bondage. (3) Moral collapse — Rom 1:31: the covenant-breaker (asunthetos) appears in the catalogue of moral and cognitive failure that characterises those who have suppressed the truth (OBS-034-057). Not receiving the covenantal bond produces inner moral dissolution. (4) Curse activation — Dan 9:11 (OBS-034-036): the person who encounters the covenant, receives its obligations, but breaks it experiences the activated curse — the inner consequence of breach. The common thread: not receiving covenant (or receiving it falsely/partially) produces inner conditions of desolation, bondage, moral dissolution, or judgment. |
| OBS-034-T2-074 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | Yes — moral evaluation is deeply engaged by covenant. (1) Covenant defines the moral standard against which inner fidelity is assessed: the covenant's terms (law, commandments, statutes) provide the content of moral evaluation (Deu 7:9 — OBS-034-039; 2Ki 23:3 — OBS-034-047). Living within covenant means living under a defined moral evaluative framework. (2) Covenant breach is a moral failure assessed by the moral evaluation faculty: Dan 9:11 — "we have sinned against him" (OBS-034-036) — Daniel's prayer of confession is a moral self-assessment in covenantal categories. (3) The oath/curse structure (H0423 — OBS-034-007) embeds a moral assessment mechanism in the covenant itself: the covenant includes its own evaluation criterion and sanction for breach. (4) G0802 (asunthetos — OBS-034-057): Paul's taxonomy in Rom 1:31 names covenant-breaking as a moral defect — the moral evaluation faculty of the apostle and of Scripture judges covenant-breaking as character vice. |
| OBS-034-T2-076 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 1:31 | The pattern reveals that covenant is intrinsically moral — it cannot be separated from moral evaluation because its structure embeds evaluation within it. The covenant oath (with its curse for breach — OBS-034-007) is not merely a relational bond but a morally evaluative structure: the person under covenant is always already under moral assessment relative to the covenant's terms. This means covenant is not a merely relational or emotional reality but a morally accountable one — being in covenant means being accountable to a standard. The new covenant's internalisation of the standard (law on the heart — Heb 8:10) means the moral evaluation faculty itself is transformed: the person evaluates from within a renewed inner standard rather than against an external one. The pattern confirms that the Moral Character dimension (05) is correctly assigned to multiple covenant groups — covenant-keeping is constitutively a moral-character reality. |
| OBS-034-T2-130 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Rom 1:31 | Yes — confirmed from §G.3 (OBS-034-032, OBS-034-033): Covenant shares 11 anchor verses with other registries. Key shared anchors: (1) Heb 8:10 — shared with R001 (abomination) and R112 (mind). (2) Neh 10:29 — shared with R019 (calling). (3) Eph 4:24 — shared with R076 (holiness). (4) Eph 2:12 — shared with R078 (hope) and R197 (authority). (5) Rom 1:31 — shared with R111 (mercy). (6) Dan 9:24 — shared with R147 (sin). (7) 2Ki 23:3 — shared with R159 (testimony). (8) 2Sa 23:5 — shared with R177 (worth). (9) Rev 2:17 — shared with R204 (name). (10) Isa 28:15 — shared with R078 (hope). These 11 shared-anchor relationships across 10 distinct registries confirm that covenant's anchor verses are structurally important to a wide range of programme registries — covenant is not isolated but serves as an anchor point for multiple inner-being characteristics. |
| OBS-034-T2-131 | CROSS_REGISTRY | Rom 1:31 | Three most analytically significant shared anchors: (1) Heb 8:10 shared with R001 (abomination) and R112 (mind): the new covenant's constitutional claim (law written on mind and heart) is an anchor for both the abomination and mind registries — revealing that the new covenant's inner inscription touches the cognitive inner-being territory that both registries engage. The fact that this verse anchors the abomination registry is unexpected and suggests that the abomination registry's engagement with this verse may be through the contrast between the abomination of old-covenant violation and the new-covenant remedy. Requires Session D investigation (SP-034-001 direction). (2) Eph 2:12 shared with R078 (hope) and R197 (authority): covenant and hope share this anchor, revealing that covenantal standing is the ground of hope (OBS-034-060) — and that authority-structures also anchor on this verse, suggesting that covenant intersects with authority in the domain of inclusion/exclusion from divine authority-structures. (3) Rom 1:31 shared with R111 (mercy): the covenantal defect (asunthetos) and mercy share an anchor — suggesting that the mercy registry engages the covenant-breaking context of Rom 1:31 as a site where mercy is absent, and that the covenant-mercy relationship requires cross-registry synthesis (SP-034-005). |
| OBS-034-T2-139 | ETYMOLOGY | Rom 1:31 | Yes — explicitly. (1) H3748 keritut (divorce — the cutting of the marriage covenant — OBS-034-012): the formal opposite of covenant-union is named by a term from the same cutting root. Divorce is the covenant-opposite explicitly named by covenant's own vocabulary. (2) G0802 asunthetos (untrustworthy/covenant-breaker — OBS-034-018): the NT names the covenant-lacking character type with a specific term — asunthetos = not-placed-together = unable to hold covenants. The structural opposite (covenant-breaking as character defect) has its own lexical item in the NT. (3) H3772J karat (to cut: lack — OBS-034-015): the negative of covenantal continuity — being cut off is the covenant-opposite of being in continuous covenant relationship. (4) H0423 alah (the curse embedded in the oath — OBS-034-007): the sanction for covenant-absence is named within the covenant vocabulary itself. The vocabulary is internally comprehensive — it names both the positive reality and its negation. |
| OBS-034-T2-140 | MEANING_OBSERVATION | Rom 1:31 | The data contains one explicit person-type term adjacent to the covenant vocabulary. G0802 asunthetos (OBS-034-018) — the covenant-breaker as a character type — is a person-type term for the habitual absence of covenant faithfulness. However, a positive person-type term for the covenant-keeper (the habitually faithful covenant person) is not directly evidenced in the programme's vocabulary with a single dedicated term. The data does contain person-descriptions that function as person-type indicators: "those who love him and keep his commandments" (Deu 7:9), "those who keep his covenant and his testimonies" (Psa 25:10) — but these are phrases, not dedicated lexical items. Gap: a dedicated positive person-type term for the covenant-faithful person is not identified in the programme's term inventory. The negative person-type (asunthetos) is named; the positive is described but not lexically fixed. |
| OBS-034-T2-142 | ETYMOLOGY | Rom 1:31 | G0802 (asunthetos — untrustworthy/covenant-breaker — OBS-034-018) appears to be a distinctive NT-period term for the covenant-breaking character quality. While the word's components are classical Greek, its appearance in Paul's vice-catalogue (Rom 1:31) as a moral character term within a NT theological context is the word's most prominent biblical use. It represents the NT's naming of covenant-faithlessness as a specific moral character defect — giving an explicit lexical label to what the OT described through narrative and prophetic denunciation. The other NT-specific development is not a newly coined term but a newly deployed term: diathēkē in its testamentary sense (Heb 9:16-17) exploits an existing Greek legal term for theological purposes — not a coinage but a semantic deployment that generates a new theological meaning-range for an existing word. |
