<a id="t-G1128"></a>
### G1128 gumnazō — to train (`mti_id=7419`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `G1128` · Lang: Greek · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R206 vulnerability · Legacy C-cluster: C22
3. Sub-group: `BOUNDARY` (Functional, supporting, and cluster-reassignment candidates)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 4
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=7419` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=not_done`
6. Mounce short-def: to train, exercise

**Verses:** 4 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (2)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3334 | 7419-001 | Term describes the inner training of the person toward spiritual maturity — habituating discernment, righteousness, and godliness |  |
| 3335 | 7419-002 | Term describes the inner habituation of the heart toward vice — the trained disposition toward greed |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (13 unique)**

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|
| OBS-117-T2-028 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Heb 12:11 | Yes — and this is theologically significant. The forming dimension: (1) Mal 2:6 — walking in peace as the character-across-time of the faithful priest; the formed person whose sustained walk is peaceful; (2) G1516 eirēnikos — peaceableness as the fruit of discipline (Heb 12:11 — "the peaceful fruit of righteousness to those who have been trained by it"); (3) Gal 5:22 — peace as Spirit-fruit — the Spirit forms peace as a characteristic quality in the believer over time; (4) G2272 hēsuchios — the gentle and quiet spirit (1Pe 3:4) as the "hidden person of the heart" — a formed inner quality precious to God. The evidence confirms: sustained shalom is not merely experienced but formed — it becomes a character quality, an inner orientation, and eventually the "hidden person" of the heart. The transformative dimension connects directly to T5 (formative and developmental). |
| OBS-117-T2-096 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Heb 12:11 | The data implies that genuine peace-extension requires prior reception and formation: (1) Prior reception: the person who has not received peace cannot authentically give it — the greeting-blessing (1Sa 25:6) is an invocation of God's peace upon another, not a self-generated gift; the extension is mediated from the divine source; (2) Character formation: G1516 eirēnikos — peaceableness as quality of heavenly wisdom and of discipline (Heb 12:11 — "the peaceful fruit of righteousness to those who have been trained by it") — the person who extends peace has been trained; (3) Identity-level commitment: Psa 120:7 ("I am for peace") — the giver of peace is a person whose identity is peace; the extension flows from being, not merely from choosing. The data does not require perfect peace in the giver — Jer 29:7 (exiles seeking the welfare of their captor city) shows peace-extension is possible even from a position of suffering — but it requires genuine orientation. |
| OBS-117-T2-097 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Heb 12:11 | Yes — but the data for human-from-human reception is less explicit than for God-from-human or human-to-human giving. Evidence: (1) The greeting-blessing: the recipients of the sha.lom blessing (1Sa 25:6's addressees) receive peace from the messenger; (2) The apostolic blessing-recipients: every Pauline letter recipient receives "grace and peace" in the greeting — the community receives peace through the apostolic proclamation; (3) G1516 — the discipline produces eirēnikos fruit in "those who have been trained by it" (Heb 12:11) — the person who submits to a trainer/community receives peace as a fruit of that relationship; (4) The woman healed in Luk 8:48 — "go in peace" — she receives peace from Christ's word; (5) Children of Zion taught by God: Isa 54:13 ("great shall be the peace of your children") — the covenant community's children receive peace through being taught. |
| OBS-117-T2-108 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Heb 12:11 | Yes — the data documents several sequences: (1) Lam 3 sequence: soul bereft of peace (3:17) → remembering God's hesed (3:21-22) → hope restored (3:24) → quiet waiting (3:26) — the sequence from peaceless despair through memory to hope through waiting; (2) Isa 30:15 sequence: returning → rest → quietness → trust → strength/salvation — a five-stage sequence where each stage enables the next; (3) Psa 32 sequence: sin → suppressed conscience → somatic deterioration → confession → divine forgiveness → peace; (4) Gift-to-fruit sequence: God gives peace (Num 6:26) → received in the mind/heart (Isa 26:3) → enacted in the body (Psa 4:8) → expressed in the community (Rom 12:18) — the reception-to-expression sequence; (5) Formation sequence: discipline → trained → peaceful fruit (Heb 12:11) — the formative sequence through suffering into peace. |
| OBS-117-T2-109 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Heb 12:11 | The sequences converge on three structural insights: (1) Peace is not arrived at directly but through precondition states — trust, returning, quietness, confession, discipline are all precondition stages that enable peace's reception; (2) Peace received produces enabling states — strength, hope, stability, and community expression flow from peace received; peace is both an end-state (received) and an enabling-state (productive); (3) The Lam 3 sequence is the most instructive for the peaceless person: the route from peacelessness back to peace is through memory of God's faithfulness (not through achieving peace directly). The sequence reveals that peace has a pathway structure — it is neither instantaneous (always) nor arbitrary in how it comes and goes. |
| OBS-117-T2-110 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Heb 12:11 | The data identifies multiple mechanisms: (1) Divine word/proclamation: Judg 6:23 ("Peace be to you") — the spoken word of peace from God or his messenger produces the inner shift from terror to peace; Joh 14:27 ("my peace I give to you") — Christ's word-gift; the proclamation is a mechanism; (2) Conscience-activation: Psa 32:3-5 — the mechanism is confession activating what suppressed conscience had blocked; the act of acknowledgment opens the peace-channel; (3) Cognitive re-direction: Isa 26:3 — the mechanism is the deliberate directing of the mind toward God; (4) Discipline and training: Heb 12:11 — the mechanism is the sustained experience of discipline by a trainer, producing peace as fruit over time; (5) Cross-accomplishment: Col 1:20 — the mechanism is Christ's blood of the cross making peace; a historical accomplished act; (6) Spirit indwelling: Gal 5:22 — the Spirit's presence produces peace as fruit through the mechanism of indwelling and progressive formation. |
| OBS-117-T2-111 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Heb 12:11 | Yes — the mechanisms differ by context: (1) Crisis/encounter context: divine word is the immediate mechanism (Judg 6:23, Dan 10:19, Joh 14:27) — peace comes through an encounter with divine presence or word; (2) Moral failure context: conscience-activation and confession are the mechanism (Psa 32:3-5) — the route back from peacelessness through sin is through honest acknowledgment; (3) Cognitive orientation context: sustained mind-direction is the mechanism (Isa 26:3, Rom 8:6) — for the ongoing inner condition of peace, the mechanism is daily cognitive orientation; (4) Formative context: discipline over time is the mechanism (Heb 12:11) — peace-as-character is formed through sustained training rather than instantaneous receipt; (5) Eschatological/cosmic context: Christ's cross is the mechanism (Col 1:20, Isa 53:5) — for the foundational peace-with-God and cosmic peace, the mechanism is unique historical accomplishment. No single mechanism covers all contexts. |
| OBS-117-T2-112 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Heb 12:11 | Yes — and the peace-suffering relationship is one of the most analytically significant in the entire registry. Evidence: (1) Isa 53:5 — "upon him was the chastisement that brought us peace, and with his wounds we are healed" — peace purchased through the Servant's suffering; (2) Isa 38:17 — "it was for my welfare that I had great bitterness" — Hezekiah's welfare arrived through his suffering; (3) Heb 12:11 — "for the moment all discipline seems painful rather than pleasant, but later it yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness" — peace is the outcome of endured discipline (suffering); (4) Lam 3:17 — "my soul is bereft of peace" — the peaceless condition in suffering; (5) 2Cor 2:13 — Paul's spirit not at rest amid his afflictions — the tension between suffering and peace; (6) 2Th 1:7 — "relief [anesis] to you who are afflicted... when the Lord Jesus is revealed" — eschatological peace as relief from present suffering. |
| OBS-117-T2-113 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Heb 12:11 | All four relationships appear: (1) Produces: Heb 12:11 — discipline produces peaceful fruit in those trained; Isa 38:17 — bitterness was the path to welfare; suffering is the generative mechanism in certain contexts; (2) Tests: Lam 3:17 — the soul bereft of peace is the person whose peace is tested to its limit; the sustained peacelessness under catastrophe tests whether the person's peace has deep roots or is situational; (3) Reveals: the person who maintains peace through suffering reveals a peace grounded in God rather than circumstance (Phili 4:7 — "surpasses all understanding" suggests it transcends circumstantial explanation); (4) Deepens: the retrospective memory of peace-through-suffering (Isa 38:17) deepens understanding of God's ways; suffering that produces peace creates a richer experiential knowledge of shalom than suffering-free peace. The paradox of T1 (Q&A-023) is confirmed here: suffering is not peace's enemy but its formative companion in the data. |
| OBS-117-T2-114 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Heb 12:11 | Yes — and this is explicitly evidenced: (1) 1Th 5:23 — "the God of peace himself sanctify you completely; your whole spirit and soul and body be kept blameless" — peace and sanctification are explicitly paired; the God of peace is the agent of complete sanctification; (2) Heb 12:11 — "the peaceful fruit of righteousness to those who have been trained" — peace is the outcome of the formative discipline that constitutes sanctification; (3) Gal 5:22 — Spirit-fruit peace — peace is a product of the Spirit's sanctifying work in the person over time; (4) Mal 2:6 — "he walked with me in peace and uprightness, and turned many from iniquity" — the long-arc portrait of the sanctified priest; peace is both the condition and the outcome of his sustained walk with God. |
| OBS-117-T2-115 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Heb 12:11 | Peace plays a dual role in sanctification: (1) Condition for sanctification: 1Th 5:23 — the God of peace sanctifies; the peace-ground is the condition under which sanctification proceeds; (2) Fruit of sanctification: Gal 5:22 (Spirit-fruit), Heb 12:11 (fruit of discipline) — peace is what sanctification produces. This dual role (condition and fruit) makes peace both the environment of growth and its outcome — uniquely integrative. The implication: a person cannot be sanctified while persistently refusing peace (the refusal-pattern of Isa 30:15 is simultaneously a refusal of the conditions for sanctification); and when sanctification proceeds, peace is among its visible fruits. |
| OBS-117-T2-123 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Heb 12:11 | Yes — documented: (1) Trust produces peace: Isa 26:3 (causal — "because he trusts in you"), Psa 4:8; (2) Righteousness produces peace: Isa 32:17 ("the effect of righteousness will be peace"); (3) Confession produces peace: Psa 32:5 (confession → forgiveness → peace); (4) Discipline produces peace: Heb 12:11 (training → peaceful fruit); (5) The Spirit produces peace: Gal 5:22 (Spirit-fruit); (6) Wisdom produces peace: Pro 3:17 ("all her paths are peace"); (7) Love of God's law produces peace: Psa 119:165 ("great peace have those who love your law"). The inputs to peace are numerous: trust, righteousness, wisdom, discipline, the Spirit, confession, love of God's law. This confirms that peace is not a simple direct gift but a multi-factored condition — multiple inner and relational realities contribute to its presence. |
| OBS-117-T2-154 | OBSERVATION | Heb 12:11 | Two gaps surfaced by the human science comparison: (1) Physiological regulation: the verse evidence shows somatic consequences of peacelessness (bones wasting, failing eyes, languishing soul) but does not address the physiological mechanisms connecting inner peace to somatic health — neuroscience of the stress response (cortisol, autonomic nervous system) would complement the biblical somatic evidence but is outside the verse data; (2) Developmental trajectory: attachment theory describes how secure attachment in early childhood shapes the capacity for adult peace and trust — the biblical data addresses formed peace in adults (Heb 12:11 — "those who have been trained") but does not address childhood formation of peace-capacity. Both gaps are relevant for Session C prose and for a comprehensive understanding of shalom's human experience, but they cannot be grounded in the biblical evidence directly. |

2. G1131 gumnos → naked

<a id="t-H7881"></a>
### H7881 si.chah — meditation (`mti_id=975`)

**Identity & meaning**

1. Strong's: `H7881` · Lang: Hebrew · Owner: OWNER
2. Registry: R108 meditation · Legacy C-cluster: C02
3. Sub-group: `M15-F` (Meditative and reflective inner activity)
4. Term status: extracted · Evidential: — · Occurrences: 3
5. Patch-authoring refs: `mti_id=975` · `md_version=1` · `vc_status=not_done`

**Verses:** 3 distinct locations (see §2 for the verse table grouped by sub-group).

**Group memberships (1)**

| Group ID | Code | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1623 | 975-001 | Term names meditation as the sustained inner engagement with God's law and presence — the all-day practice of the devoted heart that produces understanding |  |

**Findings — direct (Strong's mention) (0)**

(none)

**Findings — via anchor verse (0 unique)**

(none)

**SD pointers — direct (1)**

| Pointer | Priority | Strong's ref | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inner-word / inner-speech dimension of meditation — does registry 108 capture the interior/exterior boundary of word and thought (hagah, siach)? | MEDIUM | H1897; H7878; H7881; H1900; H1902H | Session D Investigation Prompt — Registry 108 (Meditation) — Inner Word / Inner Speech Dimension During a pre-Session D exploratory discussion, the question was raised whether the Framework B programme adequately captures the concept of word / voice / speak / thought as phenomena that are at least partly located in the inner being, rather than being purely physical or communicative acts. The discussion identified a convergence across three domains: Biblical-theological: Hebrew dabar (דָּבָר) carries both word and reality/event, suggesting the spoken word is an extension of the speaker's inner being. Hebrew lev (heart) is the seat of inner speech and thought — "the heart says..." is a genuine idiom for interior thought-speech. Greek logos carries the Stoic distinction between logos endiathetos (inner word/reason) and logos prophorikos (uttered word). Augustine's verbum mentis (word of the mind) is his pre-linguistic inner word, located in the soul. Philosophical: From Plato onward, thought is treated as inner dialogue — "the soul talking to itself." The logos endiathetos / prophorikos distinction runs from Stoics through Augustine and remains philosophically significant. Psychological/cognitive: Inner speech is a well-established phenomenon (Vygotsky, modern neuroscience). It activates speech-production brain areas and is partly constitutive of conscious thought, not merely a report of it. Programme gap identified: The programme has no dedicated registry for word, voice, speak, or logos. This is probably a principled scope decision — the programme covers inner states and dispositions, not communicative acts. However, the gap raises a specific question for Session D analysis of meditation (108, C02): Does the meditation registry adequately capture the inner-speech dimension of its core terms — particularly הָגָה (hagah, to murmur/muse inwardly) and שִׂיחַ (siach, to muse/speak inwardly) — as terms that sit at the interior/exterior boundary of word and thought? Or does the registry treat these primarily as contemplative states, missing the inner-speech signal? Specific investigation questions for Session D: 1. Are הָגָה and שִׂיחַ present in the meditation term inventory, and are they glossed in a way that preserves their inner-speech quality? 2. Does the analysis distinguish meditation-as-inner-speech from meditation-as-quiet-reflection? 3. Is there any cross-reference to thought (160) or counsel (32) that picks up the inner-word dimension? Note (registry-state observation, 2026-04-25): In the current registry-108 inventory, H1897 hagah ("to mutter") and H7878 siach verb ("to muse") are present as XREF terms — owned elsewhere — while the meditation nouns H1900 hagut, H1902H higgayon, and H7881 sichah are OWNER. The inner-speech verbs that most directly carry the interior/exterior-boundary signal are therefore not OWNER in 108. Session D may need to consider whether this ownership pattern is itself part of the gap: the meditation registry holds the noun-form contemplative-state terms while the action-form inner-speech verbs sit in their OWNER registries. Cross-registry ownership of H1897 and H7878 should be confirmed when this pointer is taken up. |

**Auxiliary data**

| OBS-111-T2-005 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 3:25 | The propitiation vocabulary points forward: Rom 3:25 (*hilastērios* received by faith) and the typological fulfillment of the mercy seat in Christ (OBS-111-066, OBS-111-067) establish mercy's eschatological trajectory through Christ. The Beatitude in Mat 5:7 ("they shall receive mercy") carries a future-tense promise that points toward the completion of mercy's logic. Heb 8:12's new covenant citation ("I will remember their sins no more") is eschatological in character — it names the final state of divine-human relationship under mercy. The evidence does not name a specific future fullness of mercy beyond these. OBS-111-032, OBS-111-035, OBS-111-066. |
| OBS-111-T2-009 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 3:25 | Yes — mercy has explicit typological architecture. The *kapporet* (mercy seat, H3727) is the physical OT locus of divine-human meeting in mercy; *hilastērios* (G2435) names Christ as the fulfillment of the mercy seat (Rom 3:25). The typological movement is explicit: physical mercy seat → Christ as living mercy seat received by faith (OBS-111-066, OBS-111-067). The Day of Atonement (*kippurim*, H3725) similarly finds typological fulfillment in Christ's once-for-all sacrifice (Heb 9–10, though the Heb 9:5 verse notes this trajectory). OBS-111-045, OBS-111-066, OBS-111-067. |
| OBS-111-T2-010 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 3:25 | The typological direction is human institution → divine person. The mercy seat as physical object (Heb 9:5) was the divine-established institution within Israel's worship; it points forward to Christ as the person in whom all that the mercy seat represented is fulfilled (Rom 3:25). The direction is not human → divine analogy (as if the human institution generates the divine reality) but divine establishment of the type followed by divine fulfillment in Christ. OBS-111-066, OBS-111-067. |
| OBS-111-T2-110 | OBSERVATION | Rom 3:25 | Yes — mechanism varies by context: OT ritual context uses blood-sacrifice; new covenant context uses faith-reception of the living mercy seat (Rom 3:25 — OBS-111-066); prayer context uses petition-response; Christ's priestly context uses incarnational solidarity (Heb 2:17). The mechanisms are contextually differentiated but share a common logic: the mercy mechanism is always specific, embodied, and costs something (blood, incarnation, divine speech, answered petition). OBS-111-033, OBS-111-037, OBS-111-060, OBS-111-066. |
| OBS-111-T2-115 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Rom 3:25 | Yes — three eschatological pointers: (1) Heb 8:12 (new covenant mercy — "I will remember their sins no more") is the eschatological state of the divine-human relationship as mercy-constituted. (2) Mat 5:7 ("they shall receive mercy") points forward — the promise is future-tense. (3) The typological fulfillment structure (mercy seat → Christ as living mercy seat, OBS-111-066) points to the completed mercy-reality in Christ that the OT types anticipated. OBS-111-032, OBS-111-035, OBS-111-066. |

**SD pointers — direct (0)**

(none)

The act is morally neutral in itself; direction determines its character. This is Sub-pattern A of the directionally-determined inner faculty pattern identified across C02. |

| Finding ID | Type | Matched anchor refs | Excerpt |

The act is morally neutral in itself; direction determines its character. This is Sub-pattern A of the directionally-determined inner faculty pattern identified across C02. |

**SD pointers — direct (1)**

| Pointer | Priority | Strong's ref | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inner-word / inner-speech dimension of meditation — does registry 108 capture the interior/exterior boundary of word and thought (hagah, siach)? | MEDIUM | H1897; H7878; H7881; H1900; H1902H | Session D Investigation Prompt — Registry 108 (Meditation) — Inner Word / Inner Speech Dimension During a pre-Session D exploratory discussion, the question was raised whether the Framework B programme adequately captures the concept of word / voice / speak / thought as phenomena that are at least partly located in the inner being, rather than being purely physical or communicative acts. The discussion identified a convergence across three domains: Biblical-theological: Hebrew dabar (דָּבָר) carries both word and reality/event, suggesting the spoken word is an extension of the speaker's inner being. Hebrew lev (heart) is the seat of inner speech and thought — "the heart says..." is a genuine idiom for interior thought-speech. Greek logos carries the Stoic distinction between logos endiathetos (inner word/reason) and logos prophorikos (uttered word). Augustine's verbum mentis (word of the mind) is his pre-linguistic inner word, located in the soul. Philosophical: From Plato onward, thought is treated as inner dialogue — "the soul talking to itself." The logos endiathetos / prophorikos distinction runs from Stoics through Augustine and remains philosophically significant. Psychological/cognitive: Inner speech is a well-established phenomenon (Vygotsky, modern neuroscience). It activates speech-production brain areas and is partly constitutive of conscious thought, not merely a report of it. Programme gap identified: The programme has no dedicated registry for word, voice, speak, or logos. This is probably a principled scope decision — the programme covers inner states and dispositions, not communicative acts. However, the gap raises a specific question for Session D analysis of meditation (108, C02): Does the meditation registry adequately capture the inner-speech dimension of its core terms — particularly הָגָה (hagah, to murmur/muse inwardly) and שִׂיחַ (siach, to muse/speak inwardly) — as terms that sit at the interior/exterior boundary of word and thought? Or does the registry treat these primarily as contemplative states, missing the inner-speech signal? Specific investigation questions for Session D: 1. Are הָגָה and שִׂיחַ present in the meditation term inventory, and are they glossed in a way that preserves their inner-speech quality? 2. Does the analysis distinguish meditation-as-inner-speech from meditation-as-quiet-reflection? 3. Is there any cross-reference to thought (160) or counsel (32) that picks up the inner-word dimension? Note (registry-state observation, 2026-04-25): In the current registry-108 inventory, H1897 hagah ("to mutter") and H7878 siach verb ("to muse") are present as XREF terms — owned elsewhere — while the meditation nouns H1900 hagut, H1902H higgayon, and H7881 sichah are OWNER. The inner-speech verbs that most directly carry the interior/exterior-boundary signal are therefore not OWNER in 108. Session D may need to consider whether this ownership pattern is itself part of the gap: the meditation registry holds the noun-form contemplative-state terms while the action-form inner-speech verbs sit in their OWNER registries. Cross-registry ownership of H1897 and H7878 should be confirmed when this pointer is taken up. |

| 182-F009 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | Heb 4:12 | Heb 4:12 — the Word of God divides soul and spirit as a sword divides joints and marrow. The soul cannot reliably distinguish itself from the spirit by introspection alone. The soul-spirit distinction is real but requires divine speech to make it visible — it is not self-evident to the person. |
| DIM-52-002 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | Heb 4:12 | Five groups in the division registry are assigned Divine-Human Correspondence (2962-002, 5274-002, 5280-001, 824-001, 828-001). This is a significant concentration. The portion/lot vocabulary (chelek, chalaq, meros, meris) consistently operates in divine-human correspondence mode — God allots, human receives, inner states arise in response. Session B should examine whether this correspondence structure is intrinsic to the portion concept or a feature of the particular biblical corpus. |

| Inner-word / inner-speech dimension of meditation — does registry 108 capture the interior/exterior boundary of word and thought (hagah, siach)? | MEDIUM | H1897; H7878; H7881; H1900; H1902H | Session D Investigation Prompt — Registry 108 (Meditation) — Inner Word / Inner Speech Dimension During a pre-Session D exploratory discussion, the question was raised whether the Framework B programme adequately captures the concept of word / voice / speak / thought as phenomena that are at least partly located in the inner being, rather than being purely physical or communicative acts. The discussion identified a convergence across three domains: Biblical-theological: Hebrew dabar (דָּבָר) carries both word and reality/event, suggesting the spoken word is an extension of the speaker's inner being. Hebrew lev (heart) is the seat of inner speech and thought — "the heart says..." is a genuine idiom for interior thought-speech. Greek logos carries the Stoic distinction between logos endiathetos (inner word/reason) and logos prophorikos (uttered word). Augustine's verbum mentis (word of the mind) is his pre-linguistic inner word, located in the soul. Philosophical: From Plato onward, thought is treated as inner dialogue — "the soul talking to itself." The logos endiathetos / prophorikos distinction runs from Stoics through Augustine and remains philosophically significant. Psychological/cognitive: Inner speech is a well-established phenomenon (Vygotsky, modern neuroscience). It activates speech-production brain areas and is partly constitutive of conscious thought, not merely a report of it. Programme gap identified: The programme has no dedicated registry for word, voice, speak, or logos. This is probably a principled scope decision — the programme covers inner states and dispositions, not communicative acts. However, the gap raises a specific question for Session D analysis of meditation (108, C02): Does the meditation registry adequately capture the inner-speech dimension of its core terms — particularly הָגָה (hagah, to murmur/muse inwardly) and שִׂיחַ (siach, to muse/speak inwardly) — as terms that sit at the interior/exterior boundary of word and thought? Or does the registry treat these primarily as contemplative states, missing the inner-speech signal? Specific investigation questions for Session D: 1. Are הָגָה and שִׂיחַ present in the meditation term inventory, and are they glossed in a way that preserves their inner-speech quality? 2. Does the analysis distinguish meditation-as-inner-speech from meditation-as-quiet-reflection? 3. Is there any cross-reference to thought (160) or counsel (32) that picks up the inner-word dimension? Note (registry-state observation, 2026-04-25): In the current registry-108 inventory, H1897 hagah ("to mutter") and H7878 siach verb ("to muse") are present as XREF terms — owned elsewhere — while the meditation nouns H1900 hagut, H1902H higgayon, and H7881 sichah are OWNER. The inner-speech verbs that most directly carry the interior/exterior-boundary signal are therefore not OWNER in 108. Session D may need to consider whether this ownership pattern is itself part of the gap: the meditation registry holds the noun-form contemplative-state terms while the action-form inner-speech verbs sit in their OWNER registries. Cross-registry ownership of H1897 and H7878 should be confirmed when this pointer is taken up. |

**Auxiliary data**

_Related words (4):_

2. H1899 he.geh → moaning
3. H1902G hig.ga.von → Higgaion
4. H1902H hig.ga.von → meditation

<a id="s4"></a>
## §4. Appendix — items linked at registry or collection level

<a id="s4-1"></a>
### §4.1 Findings from contributor registries with no term-level link (28)

These findings sit on a registry that contributes terms to this cluster, but their text and anchor verses don't resolve to any specific cluster term. Candidates for term-level enrichment.

| Finding ID | Type | Registry | Anchor verses | Excerpt |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-128-002 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R128 rebellion |  | The sur (to turn aside) sub-gloss groups in Reg 128 show a consistent pattern: turning aside can be from God (6076-001 — Spiritual/God-ward negative), from idols toward God (6075-001 — Spiritual/God-ward positive), or toward God (6076-003 — Spiritual/God-ward positive). The same volitional act (turning) is the mechanism for both rebellion and repentance — only the direction differs. Session B should examine whether the turning vocabulary illuminates rebellion as fundamentally a directional problem of the inner being, and what this implies for understanding repentance as the reversal of the same inner movement. |
| DIM-001-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R001 abomination |  | The new covenant transformation group (3272-001: diatithēmi — the covenantal act writing divine law on the inner mind and heart) appears in the abomination registry. This places the new covenant's inner transformation as the divine answer to abomination and inner defilement. Session B should examine whether the abomination cluster's verse evidence shows the new covenant specifically as the remedy for abomination — and whether this pattern recurs across the cluster. |
| DIM-149-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R149 slander |  | The slander registry contains three foot-related groups (6253-001: moral life direction; 6253-002: relational submission/devotion; 6256-001: teaching to walk) whose connection to slander is via the XREF lexical path rather than conceptual affinity. Session B should assess whether these groups contribute meaningfully to the slander analysis or whether they represent peripheral inner-being vocabulary that should be noted as structurally incidental. |
| DIM-26-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R026 conscience |  | The conscience (suneidesis/sunoida) groups in Reg 26 show a consistent pattern: moral self-awareness operating under divine authority — conscience as inner witness that remains subordinate to God's judgment. Session B should examine the relationship between conscience-as-moral-witness and divine judgment as the higher court. |
| DIM-43-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R043 desire |  | Registry 43 (desire) shows a consistent divine/human register split: divine desire, will, and longing = Theological/Divine-Human; human desire and will = Volitional/Will or desiderative. These two registers appear throughout the vocabulary. Session B should read them in parallel and assess how Scripture's account of divine desire relates to human desire. |
| DIM-43-002 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R043 desire |  | Misdirected desire dominates a large segment of Registry 43 — 10+ groups (464-001, 469-001, 480-001, 481-001, 481-003, 492-003, 497-001, 498-001, 504-001, 505-001) name desire as moral disorder. Session B should examine whether Scripture treats desire's moral valence as its primary theological concern, and how the disordered and rightly-ordered forms relate. |
| DIM-051-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R051 distress |  | H7379 (riv, strife/contention) has 2 healthy active groups in distress (#51): 234-001 (strife/contention, 19 verses) and 234-002 (pleading one's cause, 26 verses). A third group was created for the indictment/controversy sense — God's legal case against his people in the prophetic tradition — but was never populated with verse_context records and has been delete_flagged. Session B analyst should assess whether this prophetic lawsuit dimension of H7379 belongs within distress and whether the verse evidence warrants a third group. Candidate verses would be found in prophetic lawsuit texts (Isaiah, Jeremiah, Micah) where H7379 names God's formal contention with Israel. |
| DIM-051-002 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R051 distress |  | Group 235-002 (H7451A ra, harmful spirit afflicting Saul): assigned Theological/Divine-Human. This group sits on the boundary between demonological, psychological, and theological dimensions — the harmful spirit is theologically constituted (sent by God per 1 Sam 16) but produces what functions as an inner-psychological affliction. The existing dimension vocabulary may be inadequate to name this precisely. Session B should examine the verse evidence and consider whether a note on the vocabulary gap is warranted. |
| DIM-051-003 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R051 distress |  | Registry 51 (distress) contains a significant cluster of outlier groups arriving via XREF terms that are outside the suffering domain: 242-001 (meditation/contemplation, Cognitive/Mind), 3600-001 (divine forming of the inner person, Theological/Divine-Human), 5129-002 (opened ear as inner receptivity, Spiritual/God-ward), 5168-001 (embrace as expression of fulfilment, Affective/Emotional), 5124-001 and 5126-001 (amazement/astonishment). These should be treated as peripheral in Session B analysis of Registry 51 and may warrant a vocabulary or cluster note. |
| DIM-52-003 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R052 division | Mark 1:10 | Mark 1:10 (heavens torn open at Jesus's baptism) appears as a related verse in group 5272-001 (schizō). This is a cosmic/physical use of schizō with no inner-being subject in the term itself. Its presence in the inner-being corpus may reflect an over-broad Verse Context classification. Session B should assess whether the heavens-torn-open image carries inner-being significance through its context (divine approval, identity revelation) or whether it represents a classification gap. |
| DIM-57-002 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R057 evil |  | Group 851-004 ('the impossibility of inner self-knowledge — the heart's moral corruption exceeds human capacity to understand') is a theologically significant statement about the opacity of the heart to itself. Session B should treat this as a key inner-being anthropological claim and examine its relationship to groups in other registries (e.g. conscience, self-knowledge, moral awareness). |
| DIM-187-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R187 strength | 1 Sam 15:22 | Group 7013-001 (a.yil, ram) carries a verse-level valuation in which obedience (H8085H sha.ma) and attentive listening (H7181 qashav) are declared greater than sacrifice — greater than the fat of rams (1 Sam 15:22). Neither term is an OWNER in Registry 187; their inner-being weight is present in the verse but unregistered in the programme. Session B for Registry 187 should explore: (1) what the comparative structure of 1 Sam 15:22 reveals about the hierarchy of inner-being orientations toward God — obedience and attentive listening as the higher register; (2) how this valuation relates to the strength/capacity cluster's broader understanding of inner-being agency before God; (3) the relationship between sha.ma and qashav as paired inner-being acts that together constitute the responsive orientation of the person toward God. |
| DIM-191-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R191 doubt |  | Group 1286-001 (H2963 ta.raph, tearing) was assigned Emotion — Negative / HUMAN to capture the human experience of violent inner states: wrath, predatory character, destructive anger, and the suffering of being torn. The same group contains significant GOD-dimension content — divine wrath and fierce resolve to fight for his people. Session B should study both dimensions: (1) the human inner-being experience of violent states and of being torn under judgment or hostile power; and (2) the divine inner-being reality of wrath and fierce protective resolve, exploring whether these are the same phenomenon viewed from different subjects or whether they name distinct inner-being realities. |
| DIM-201-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R201 image | Genesis 1:26-27; 1 Corinthians 11:7 | Three groups in the image registry (1359-001 tse.lem, 1360-001 de.mut, 1361-001 eikōn) all assign Divine-Human Correspondence to the imago Dei statement under three different terms. This convergence across Hebrew tse.lem, Hebrew de.mut, and Greek eikōn is analytically significant: all three terms independently arrive at the same dimension for the same verse (Gen 1:26-27). Session B should examine the imago Dei as the ontological foundation of Divine-Human Correspondence throughout the programme — whether the image-bearing of the human person is the root from which all other divine-human correspondences in the inner being flow. |
| DIM-201-002 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R201 image | 2 Corinthians 3:18; Romans 8:29; Colossians 3:10 | Group 1361-002 (eikōn — 2 Cor 3:18, 'transformed into the same image from glory to glory') is assigned Transformation. This group is structurally adjacent to the three imago Dei groups but names a process rather than a state — the inner person being actively changed toward the divine image. Session B should examine the relationship between bearing the image (static ontological correspondence) and being transformed toward it (dynamic process) — these may be two aspects of a single inner-being reality that need to be held together. |
| DIM-206-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R206 vulnerability |  | Genesis 3:10 (group 7414-001 — e.rom) is the foundational inner-being text for the vulnerability cluster: 'I was afraid, because I was naked, and I hid myself.' This encodes the primary inner-being sequence of moral failure: nakedness-awareness → fear → self-concealment. Session B should examine how this three-stage sequence (exposure of inner condition → negative emotional response → volitional self-concealment from God) structures the anthropology of inner vulnerability across the OT and NT. The contrast with Gen 2:25 (naked and not ashamed) establishes the pre-fall / post-fall inner-being contrast as a structural framework for the vulnerability cluster. |
| 112-F002 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | R112 mind | Rom 1:28, Rom 12:2, Eph 4:17, Col 1:21, 1Cor 2:16 | The mind is a moral organ before it is an intellectual one. It can be debased, blinded, corrupted, renewed, and transferred (mind of Christ). Romans 12:2 presents renewal of the nous as the locus of personal transformation. |
| 112-F003 | ETYMOLOGY | R112 mind | Gen 6:5, Gen 8:21, Isa 26:3 | The ye.tser (formed inclination) is the OT anthropology's closest approach to a foundational moral disposition. Gen 6:5 and 8:21 establish it as bent toward evil from youth — the dispositional substrate generating particular thoughts and plans. |
| 112-F008 | TERM_BEHAVIOUR | R112 mind | 1Cor 14:14, 1Cor 14:15 | The 1 Cor 14:14-15 nous/pneuma distinction is the sharpest NT terminological boundary between mind-level and spirit-level inner operation — directly relevant to the Framework B spirit-soul-body boundary investigation. |
| 112-F009 | VERSE_PATTERN | R112 mind | Jas 1:8, Jas 4:8, Psa 119:113 | Double-mindedness (dipsuchos/se.eph) documents inner division as a cross-testament pathology. James presents inner unity as a condition for receiving from God and for effective action; Ps 119:113 makes it a moral stance. |
| 112-F010 | THEOLOGICAL_NOTE | R112 mind | Heb 8:10, Heb 10:16 | The Hebrews new covenant quotation (Heb 8:10; 10:16) names both dianoia (mind) and kardia (heart) as sites of internalized law — anticipating the structural relationship between Registries 112 and 183 for Session D investigation. |
| DIM-112-001 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R112 mind |  | sha.mar family (H8104) spans four dimensions across Registry 112: Volitional/Will (G, covenantal obedience), Theological/Divine-Human (H, God's guarding), Moral/Conscience (H subset self-guarding; I attentive observation), Character/Disposition (J, vigilance). Session B: map the full sha.mar semantic range across all inner-being functions. |
| DIM-112-002 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R112 mind |  | se.eph (4198-001, H5588) and dipsuchos (1398-001, G1374) form an OT/NT pair documenting inner division as a cross-testament moral pathology. Both assigned Moral/Conscience. Session B: analyse together as witnesses to the same inner-being phenomenon — the split allegiance that makes the person unstable before God. |
| DIM-112-003 | DIMENSION_REVIEW | R112 mind |  | ye.tser (936-001/936-002, H3336) spans Moral/Conscience (formed moral inclination toward evil or good) and Theological/Divine-Human (creaturely formation known and addressed by God). The OT's closest approach to a foundational moral disposition is simultaneously a moral reality and a creaturely-relational one. Session B: the ye.tser as the dispositional substrate of moral life. |
| DIM-112-004 | DIMENSIONAL_PATTERN | R112 mind |  | Covenant-renewal convergence across registries — Dimension 11 cluster. Five groups across r112 and r183 describe God's inner-being covenant act renewing the human person: dianoia law-writing (995-003, Heb 8:10/10:16), mnaomai non-remembrance (4413-003, Heb 8:12/10:17), le.vav circumcision/renewal (179-009, Deut 30:6), qe.rev new covenant writing (577-003, Jer 31:33), enkainizō inaugural renewal (602-001, Heb 10:20). Coordinated Dimension 11 event across multiple inner-being terms and registries. |
| DIM-112-005 | DIMENSIONAL_PATTERN | R112 mind |  | sha.mar root family dimensional breadth. Five sense-splits in r112 occupy five different dimensions: 4379-001 (obedience) → 04 Volition; 4380-001 (divine guarding) → 11 D-H Correspondence GOD; 4380-002 (self-guarding) → 11 D-H Correspondence HUMAN; 4381-001 (moral observation) → 03 Cognition; 4382-001 (vigilance) → 05 Moral Character. Illustrates directionally-determined inner-faculty principle. |
| DIM-112-006 | DIMENSIONAL_PATTERN | R112 mind |  | Divine reckoning and divine remembrance as paired Dimension 11 patterns. cha.shav 3335-001 (imputation, Gen 15:6/Rom 4) and mnaomai 4413-002 (divine remembrance producing effect) both name divine inner acts determining human standing. Both Dimension 11 GOD. Foundational pair for Session B studies of reckoning/imputation vocabulary. |
| DIM-112-007 | DIMENSIONAL_PATTERN | R112 mind |  | Memorial vocabulary as Dimension 11. Three AZKARAH groups (3498-001 zikkaron institutional, 3498-002 zikkaron priestly, 3499-001 az.ka.rah memorial portion) plus anamnēsis 4426-001 (eucharistic) all engage divine-human correspondence. The memorial is a structure of mutual remembrance between God and community — unifies OT offering vocabulary and NT eucharist vocabulary on the dimension axis. |

<a id="s4-2"></a>
### §4.2 SD pointers from contributor registries with no term-level link (47)

| Pointer | Priority | Strong's ref | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| DIM-001-SD001 | MEDIUM |  | The new covenant transformation group (3272-001) appears in the abomination registry and salvation vocabulary groups appear in the debauchery registry (Reg 39) — both placing redemptive vocabulary within moral-failure/defilement registries through the XREF mechanism. Session D should examine whether this is a programme-wide pattern: are there other clusters where redemptive/salvation vocabulary appears in moral-failure registries via XREF, and what does this reveal about the inner-being logic connecting defilement and redemption in Scripture? |
| SP-067-006 | HIGH |  | Eze 36:31 is a shared anchor between R67 (Group 884-006 — not-good moral verdict) and R1 (abomination). The verse places recognition of deeds as not-good alongside 'iniquities and abominations' — self-loathing for the latter follows from recognition of the former. Session D should examine: (a) whether the not-good and abomination registers share an inner-being mechanism (both produce self-directed moral revulsion); (b) whether Eze 36:31 establishes a causal chain from not-good recognition to self-loathing that connects R67 and R1; and (c) whether Rom 2:4 (divine chreestotees leads to repentance) extends this sequence to a goodness-abomination-repentance arc. |
| DIM-26-SD001 | MEDIUM |  | Reg 26 includes G4267 proginōskō (Identity/Selfhood) alongside conscience terms — this brings together the epistemic cluster (knowing, perception) with the identity-constituting dimension of divine foreknowing. Session D should examine whether being-known-by-God and knowing-oneself-morally form a coherent inner-being pairing that constitutes a distinct dimension of conscience. |
| DIM-180-SD002 | MEDIUM |  | The water groups ([6836-001] through [6836-008]) cluster around inner transformation, inner thirst/satisfaction, and conscience cleansing. Session D should examine whether the water imagery vocabulary belongs in the yielding registry or has stronger affinity with inner transformation vocabulary in Reg 26 (conscience, C13) and the guilt/repentance registries. |
| DIM-160-SD001 | MEDIUM |  | Directionally-determined inner faculty pattern — a recurring structural principle identified across C02 registries (108, 127, 160, 174, 49, 126): the same inner faculty or act appears in morally or directionally opposite forms within a single term. Three sub-patterns: (A) directional opposition — same reflective act, Godward vs. against another; (B) capacity and its corruption — prudence vs. craftiness as the same perceptive faculty; (C) relational symmetry — a term operating from both sides of the divine-human boundary simultaneously. Open question for Session D: is this a new dimension of the inner life, or a structural principle about how inner dimensions work? Cannot be resolved from C02 data alone. Requires cross-cluster synthesis to assess scope and implications. |
| DIM-068-SD048 | MEDIUM |  | Thought (Reg 160, C02) cooccurrence 4 verses. Pass 1 identified the inference-structure of grace (Rom 8:32). For Session D: do the 4 co-occurring verses encode the inner-being act of drawing grace-inferences — thinking through what received grace implies? Is there a grace-thought pattern where right thinking about grace is itself an inner-being act enabled by grace? |
| 112-SD003 | MEDIUM | H5068 | H5068 na.dav (willing) likely shared with desire and will registries. Volitional inner movement of na.dav borders desire and will clusters. |
| 182-SD002 | MEDIUM | H5315L | H5315L ne.phesh:appetite is a SHARED_TERM with desire registry (043). The soul IS the desiring capacity — ne.phesh and ta.a.vah used in synonymous parallelism in Deu 14:26. |
| DIM-43-SD001 | MEDIUM |  | C04 desiderative density is 62% — 65 of 172 groups carry non-desiderative primary content. Question: is the desiderative vocabulary of Scripture proportionally smaller than the volitional-relational vocabulary, or are there programme coverage gaps? Session D should cross-check C04 terms against other clusters and assess whether key desiderative terms are absent from the programme or misassigned. |
| DIM-068-SD046 | MEDIUM |  | Desire (Reg 43, C04) cooccurrence 12 verses. Grace and desire co-occur substantively. The inner-being question: is desire the faculty through which grace is sought and received? Does grace reorder desire — so that the person formed by grace desires differently? Or is disordered desire the counter-condition to grace-receiving? For Session D: examine the 12 co-occurring verses to determine what relationship the data shows between the disposition of desire and the reception of grace. Does grace redirect desire from within, or does desire need to be redirected before grace can operate? |
| DIM-103-SD005 | MEDIUM | G5377 | 2Ti 3:4 — philotheos vs philēdonos antithesis. Lovers of God directly contrasted with lovers of pleasure in the last-days catalogue. Cross-registry: love (103), desire/pleasure (43), C15 (God-orientation cluster). Question: does the philo- compound family structure reveal that love and desire share the same inner mechanism of attachment — with the moral question being solely the object toward which the attachment is directed? |
| DIM-064-SD021 | MEDIUM | G0863G | 8 shared verses between forgiveness (064) and desire (043). The leave/abandon groups (5376) contain verses about leaving behind earthly attachments for discipleship — an act that implies a reordering of desire. When forgiveness is extended, the offended party must forgo the inner satisfaction of retained grievance — itself a form of desire (the desire for vindication, the desire for the account to be settled). Session D question: does the desire corpus (043) address the inner-being act of relinquishing a desire as a dimension of forgiveness? When forgiveness requires releasing the satisfaction of the retained claim, is there a desire-level reordering taking place that the programme should map? Confirmed by cooccurrence (8 shared verses). |
| SP-067-007 | MEDIUM |  | Psa 34:8 ('taste and see that the Lord is good') is a shared anchor with R49 (discernment). The 'taste and see' metaphor names an inner perception act — an act of spiritual discernment. Session D should examine whether tasting divine goodness is analytically an act of inner discernment (R49) and whether the encounter with divine goodness cultivates or confirms discernment capacity in the inner being. Also shared with R78 (hope) — refuge-taking as hope's shelter posture. |
| DIM-52-SD001 | MEDIUM |  | C18 produces 9 groups assigned Divine-Human Correspondence across division (5), rejection (1), strife (1), and unity (2) — 20% of the cluster. This is the first systematic appearance of dimension 11 in the programme. Session D should examine whether Divine-Human Correspondence groups cluster around particular semantic domains (belonging/portion, rejection, abundance) or whether they represent a cross-domain phenomenon. The C18 data suggests that relational vocabulary — division, unity, belonging — is intrinsically prone to divine-human correspondence structure. |
| DIM-57-SD001 | HIGH |  | The Affective/Emotional dimension appears extensively in C11 (15 groups assigned) but was absent from C10 assignments, despite groups in C10 (e.g. innocence 930-007: anguish/dread/hope) that appear to name affective states. The dimension exists in the programme vocabulary and is now confirmed active in C11. Session D should address whether C10 groups should be revisited for Affective/Emotional re-assignment in a subsequent dimension review pass, and whether the automated classifier's keyword triggers for this dimension need adjustment. |
| DIM-57-SD002 | HIGH |  | The Sin & Vice / Moral/Conscience boundary was systematically refined in C11: Sin & Vice = inner condition of moral failure (wickedness, deceit, corruption as states); Moral/Conscience = evaluative/judicial dimension (verdict, condemnation, moral act). This distinction should be reviewed as a programme-wide vocabulary standard in Session D, as the same boundary question applies to C10 and potentially all clusters. |
| DIM-068-SD007 | MEDIUM |  | C11 moral failure cluster cooccurrence: evil (57, 12 verses), sin (147, 6 verses), transgression (162, 6 verses), deceit (40, 5 verses). Also xref_sharing with guilt (73, 9 shared terms) and guilt cooccurrence (10 verses). Grace appears most fully where the creature's moral condition is most exposed. Session D should examine whether grace consistently operates in proximity to moral failure vocabulary, whether the recognition and naming of sin is a structural prerequisite for receiving grace, and what the inner-being sequence looks like — guilt named → grace extended → standing restored. |
| DIM-064-SD022 | LOW | G0859 | 6 shared verses between forgiveness (064) and evil (057). Forgiveness is structurally the response to evil done — the two registries are counterparts. Session D question: does the evil corpus (057) address the inner experience of the perpetrator who has committed evil and received forgiveness — specifically, what the inner-being grammar is for the one who has done wrong and has been released? The forgiveness corpus addresses the inner state of the forgiver and the recipient, but the inner-being experience of moving from evildoer to forgiven person has its own grammar. Lower priority given the cooccurrence strength and structural rather than dynamic nature of the connection. |
| DIM-187-SD001 | HIGH |  | Programme-level registry validation gap identified during C20 Dimension Review (2026-04-08). Root cause: STEP Bible treats suffix variants of a root (e.g. H8085G vs H8085H for sha.ma) as sub-glosses rather than as independently linked related terms. The Session A extraction pipeline follows STEP's relational architecture and consequently does not surface sub-gloss variants as candidates for independent extraction. H8085H (sha.ma — to hear/obey) was not extracted; H7181 (qashav — to attend/listen) was extracted but STEP shows its related-word set as identical to H8085G, masking the sha.ma/qashav relationship. These are foundational inner-being terms — the hearing/obeying and attending/listening cluster is central to the biblical account of the inner person's orientation toward God. Session D must initiate a backward validation sweep: for every registered term, check whether its STEP sub-gloss variants were independently assessed for extraction eligibility. Priority: terms where the H/G/I suffix pattern produces distinct semantic content. This sweep should be scoped and executed before Session D synthesis conclusions are drawn on the hearing/obeying dimension. |
| DIM-187-SD002 | MEDIUM |  | The oikos/oikia family in Reg 187 generates groups across at least seven distinct inner-being dimensions (Moral Character, Transformation, Agency/Power, Relational Disposition, Volition, Divine-Human Correspondence, Vitality/Existence). The household vocabulary functions as a structural metaphor for the inner life rather than naming a single characteristic. Session D should assess whether the household/dwelling family constitutes a distinct metaphorical register in the biblical account of the inner person. |
| DIM-187-SD003 | MEDIUM |  | The AMTS root family (am.mits, am.tsah, o.mets in Reg 187) is closely cognate with the CHAZAQ family (Reg 33/C08) in the biblical courage formula chazaq ve-amats (be strong and courageous). The split of these two roots across C08 (courage, verb forms) and C20 (strength, adjective/noun forms) is analytically significant. Session D should synthesise the full courage formula by reading the C08 and C20 groups together, addressing the verb/adjective split within the same root complex. |
| DIM-187-SD004 | MEDIUM |  | The directionally-determined pattern appears with particular density in C20 strength vocabulary. The same Hebrew root generates groups in opposite registers — divine greatness and human pride (GADAL), strength as divine gift and strength as misplaced trust (OZ, KO.ACH, CHAYIL), sure-footedness and collapse (TSAV). This pattern, established as a structural principle across earlier clusters, is most concentrated in C20. Session D should assess whether the strength/power cluster adds a specific contribution to the directionally-determined principle beyond what C08 (virtue/vice) and C09 (pride/majesty) already established. |
| DIM-068-SD003 | MEDIUM |  | C20 power cluster dimension overlap: strength (187), power (196), authority (197), might (198), dominion (199) — all share all 3 grace dimensions (Relational Disposition 9 groups, Moral Character 1, Dependence/Creatureliness 1). 2Cor 12:9 makes the structural connection explicit: power made perfect in weakness, grace as its modality. Session D should examine whether grace consistently names the relational mode through which divine power reaches the creature precisely where human power fails, and whether Dependence/Creatureliness is the inner condition that opens the person to both grace and power. |
| DIM-111-SD001 | HIGH |  | Strength/power/authority/dominion registries (Reg 187/196/197/198/199) share all 4 of mercy dimensions. Structural question: is mercy the moral direction of power toward the vulnerable? Session D to examine whether power and mercy form a structural inner-being pair. |
| DIM-103-SD026 | MEDIUM | G0025 | C20 cluster (strength/might/authority/dominion — Regs 187/198/197/199) shares all 8 dimensions with love and co-occurs in 69-105 verses. This may be a registry-breadth effect, but the co-occurrence is genuine and large. Question: does the biblical vocabulary connect love and power structurally? Is love the form that power takes in the kingdom of God, and power the form that love takes in creation? The 1Cor 13 sequence — love as greater than gifts of power and knowledge — suggests love and power are in deliberate tension in the NT. |
| DIM-064-SD019 | MEDIUM | G0859 | 19 shared verses between forgiveness (064) and strength (187) is the second-highest cooccurrence signal in this registry's corpus. The forgiveness passages frequently invoke divine power, exaltation, and authority in the same context as forgiveness: Acts 5:31 ('God exalted him at his right hand as Leader and Savior to give repentance and forgiveness of sins'), Num 14:19 ('according to the greatness of your steadfast love'). Session D question: does the programme's strength/power cluster (C20) address the relationship between divine omnipotence and the capacity to forgive at the scale Scripture describes? Is there an inner-being statement about divine power embedded in the forgiveness corpus that the C20 synthesis should address? Confirmed by 19 shared verses (strongest unexpected cooccurrence signal in this registry). |
| DIM-068-SD050 | LOW |  | Doubt (Reg 191, C16) cooccurrence 3 verses. Doubt is the inner oscillation between trust and distrust. Grace is received through faith (Eph 2:8). For Session D: do the 3 co-occurring verses show grace encountering the doubting person — and if so, does grace precede the resolution of doubt (grace given to the doubter) or follow it (doubt must resolve before grace is received)? Connects to the faith question (SD006) and to Dan 9:23 (grace responding before the plea is complete — SD028). |
| DIM-201-SD001 | HIGH | H6754; H1823; G1504 | The imago Dei groups (tse.lem 1359-001, de.mut 1360-001, eikōn 1361-001) across Registries 201 and 209 are all assigned Divine-Human Correspondence and converge on Gen 1:26-27. These groups may constitute the ontological foundation of Divine-Human Correspondence as a dimension across the entire programme — the human person as image-bearer is the ground from which all other divine-human inner-being correspondences arise. Session D should examine whether dimension 11 (Divine-Human Correspondence) ultimately rests on the imago Dei, and what this means for the programme's account of the human inner person as a whole. |
| DIM-19-SD001 | MEDIUM |  | C19 produces 37 of 97 groups (38%) assigned Divine-Human Correspondence — the highest cluster-level concentration in the programme so far (C18 was 20%). The concentration is highest in calling (54%) and image/likeness (imago Dei groups). Together with C18's 9 Divine-Human Correspondence groups, the programme now has a substantial body of data on this dimension. Session D should examine whether Divine-Human Correspondence is distributed across all semantic domains or concentrated in particular types of vocabulary (calling, identity, image, dignity), and what this distribution reveals about the structure of the inner being in relation to God. |
| DIM-206-SD001 | MEDIUM |  | Reg 206 (vulnerability) contains the most comprehensive nakedness vocabulary in the programme — twelve Hebrew and Greek terms across 34 groups, distributing across seven dimensions: Emotion — Negative (12 groups), Dependence/Creatureliness (5), Relational Disposition (5), Identity/Selfhood (4), Theological/Divine-Human (4), Moral Character (4), Transformation (2), Vitality/Existence (1). The dimension distribution reveals that nakedness in Scripture is not primarily an emotional concept but a multi-dimensional inner-being condition. Key structural findings: (1) the pre-fall / post-fall contrast (Gen 2:25 vs. Gen 3:10) as a structural framework; (2) nakedness serving both divine judgment (shame-exposure) and divine love (covenantal covering); (3) the apostolic identity-through-vulnerability pattern (1 Cor 4:11). Session D should examine whether vulnerability constitutes a coherent inner-being theological category or is an English-word artefact grouping distinct Hebrew/Greek inner-being phenomena. |
| DIM-068-SD008 | HIGH |  | Vulnerability (Reg 206, C22) and deadness (Reg 210, C21) both share all 3 grace dimensions and cooccur at 4 and 7 verses respectively. The Eph 2:1-5 pattern: 'you were dead in trespasses... but God, rich in mercy... even when we were dead, made us alive.' Grace addresses not merely the guilty person but the incapacitated person — the one without inner resource, dead, vulnerable. Session D should examine whether grace specifically constitutes the inner-being reconstitution of the person in conditions of deadness and vulnerability, and whether this represents the deepest inner-being claim of the grace registry: not favour toward the undeserving, but life toward the dead. |
| 112-SD001 | MEDIUM | G3340 | G3340 metanoeō, G3341 metanoia, G3338 metamellomai, and H5162H na.cham (relent) all present in Reg 112 and Reg 135 (repentance). Structural overlap at intersection of cognitive reorientation and mind-change. |
| 112-SD002 | MEDIUM | H5162G | H5162G na.cham (comfort) likely shared with Reg 071 (grief) and Reg 135. Na.cham root operates across mind-change (112), comfort of the grieving (071), and divine relenting (135). |
| 112-SD004 | MEDIUM | G4893 | G4893 suneidēsis has no Hebrew counterpart in this registry. Intersection with heart (183) and spirit (184) registries for Session D investigation of conscience as NT/Hellenistic category. |
| 112-SD005 | MEDIUM | H8104G | H8104G sha.mar/obey (195v) has significant overlap with will/obedience registries. Cross-registry boundary review recommended — may belong primarily to a different cluster. |
| 112-SD006 | MEDIUM | G1271 | Heb 8:10 and 10:16 name both dianoia (mind) and kardia (heart) as sites of new covenant law-writing. Structural overlap between Reg 112 and Reg 183. |
| 112-SD007 | MEDIUM | H2142 | H2142 za.khar operates across memory, worship, and ritual domains. Possible overlap with worship or praise registries (1 Chr 16:4 Levitical invoke/remember; Ps 38/70 memorial offering context). |
| 112-SD008 | MEDIUM | G3340 | Spirit-mind distinction in 1 Cor 14:14-15 is the sharpest NT terminological boundary between nous and pneuma. Session D: map against Reg 184 (spirit). |
| 112-SD009 | MEDIUM | H3336 | Ye.tser (formed inclination) in Gen 6:5 and 8:21 is the OT closest equivalent to fundamental moral disposition. Session D: relationship between ye.tser and will cluster. |
| 182-SD005 | MEDIUM | G1374 | G1374 dipsuchos shared with mind registry (112). Inner division documented in both soul and mind vocabularies — double-minded (soul) and double-minded (nous). Cross-registry pattern of inner unity vs division. |
| DIM-112-SD001 | MEDIUM |  | C01 Theological/Divine-Human density: 62 of 297 groups (21%) reflect a structural reality — the primary inner-being terms are constitutively relational to God. Session D: the structural vocabulary of the inner being is a theo-relational construct, not a neutral anthropological one. This pattern should be tested against all 22 clusters. |
| DIM-112-SD002 | MEDIUM |  | Absence of Sin & Vice from all six C01 structural registries. The inner-being structural terms (mind, soul, heart, spirit, flesh, being) name the capacity and theatre of sin — not sin itself. Session D: structural vocabulary vs characterological vocabulary as an analytical distinction across the programme. |
| DIM-103-SD021 | MEDIUM | G5384 | Joh 15:15 — friendship with Christ defined by shared knowledge of the Father's purposes. 'I have called you friends, for all that I have heard from my Father I have made known to you.' Love takes the form of disclosure: God's inner purposes made known. Cross-registry: love (103), knowledge/thought (160), mind (112), calling (19). Question: is there a structural link between love and knowledge in the programme — does love require knowing and being known, not merely feeling? |
| DIM-112-SD003 | MEDIUM |  | CHASHAV root family cross-registry structural pattern. Hebrew root chashav spans Reg 112 (mind), Reg 126 (purpose), Reg 160 (thought). Functions split: act of devising/thinking/counting (mind), plan as product (purpose), calculation/explanation (thought). r112 groups predominantly 03 Cognition + 04 Volition. Session D synthesis should test dimensional profile of r126 and r160 groups. |
| DIM-112-SD005 | MEDIUM |  | Inner-division theme across Greek and Hebrew vocabularies. dipsuchos G1374 (1398-001), se.eph H5588 (4198-001), dianoia-corrupted (995-002), nous-corrupted (994-002), isopsuchos G2473 (1402-001 — positive counterpart). Multiple terms from different roots/languages naming divided loyalty / split mind / darkened understanding. Distribution across 05 Moral Character, 03 Cognition, 06 Relational Disposition. Session D should trace across C17 and C22. |
| DIM-112-SD006 | MEDIUM |  | Spiritual/God-ward vocabulary gap — potential Dimension 12 (DR-13). Phase A identified 25 C01 groups with legacy 'Spiritual/God-ward' label having no clean current-vocabulary counterpart. r112 Phase C resolved these mostly to Dimension 11 (where divine-human correspondence structure was present) or 03/04/06. No r112 group required new dimension. But the label may indicate distinct 'God-ward Orientation' dimension — human's directedness toward God without correspondence structure. 995-001 dianoia 'love God with all your mind' is clearest candidate. Will be more significant for r183 where 581-006 lev God-ward orientation is UNCLASSIFIED. |
| DIM-112-SD007 | MEDIUM |  | False-positive cross-registry roots — CC rootfamily extract pipeline observation. Three of five 'cross-registry' roots for C01 appear to be string-match false positives: AT (root_code None, personal pronouns vs mutterer); YATSA (offspring vs come-out homonym); TAAM (perceive vs be-double homonym). Researcher may wish to refine tool's matching logic to exclude None-root and zero-semantic-overlap pairs. Pipeline observation, not dimension finding. |

God hides things from the wise; the wisdom of the world is folly with God. This is not wisdom inverted by the person claiming it (A-4) but wisdom as a category that stands in structural opposition to the wisdom of God. |  |
| 2467 | 4458-002 | G4680 | Term names human wisdom as an inner disposition that can be self-deceiving — the danger of wisdom turned inward on itself |  |

| 2487 | 4486-002 | H6191 | Term names craftiness or cunning as an inner disposition of deceptive scheming |  |
| 2484 | 4487-001 | H6175 | Term names prudence as an inner capacity of discernment — enabling the person to perceive situations clearly and govern speech and conduct with care |  |
| 2485 | 4487-002 | H6175 | Term names craftiness as an inner disposition of cunning — an orientation toward deceptive or self-serving ends |  |
| 1643 | 454-001 | G4894 | Term names a conscious inner awareness — moral complicity or cognitive recognition — that informs volitional response. The moral-complicity pole (shared inner co-knowing of one's participation in a morally charged act) is the primary conscience characteristic; the cognitive-recognition pole (becoming aware of a situation) represents the term's broader semantic range within the same faculty. |  |
| 1647 | 509-001 | G1014 | Term names the inner act of human willing, intending, or desiring — the volitional faculty in motion toward an object or action |  |
| 1648 | 509-002 | G1014 | Term names God's or Christ's inner purposive will — sovereign divine willing that directs revelation, creation, and redemption |  |
| 705 | 5099-001 | H2938 | Term names taste as a figure for inner discernment — the palate's capacity to test and evaluate food as the analogy for the inner faculty of testing words, truth, and the goodness of God |  |
| 706 | 5099-002 | H7919A,H2803H,H6175,H2938 | Term names the abstaining from taste as an expression of inner grief or penitence — the voluntary refusal to eat as the outward form of an inward state of mourning or contrition |  |
| 2490 | 519-001 | H7919A | Term names prudent wisdom as an inner capacity — understanding, considering, and perceiving wisely as an orientation toward God and truth |  |
| 2491 | 519-002 | H7919A | Term names the inner wisdom that produces wise conduct and success — the capacity for prudent discernment in action that governs how the person acts (Isa 52:13); wisdom as the inner quality expressed in competent, God-aligned conduct. | Revised during Dimension Review Phase C/anchor consultation: description corrected — wisdom 519-002 |
| 2471 | 523-001 | H0998 | Term names Israel's wisdom and understanding as demonstrated through faithful obedience to God's statutes — the inner capacity for discernment made visible in the life of the covenant people (Deu 4:6). | Revised during Dimension Review Phase C/anchor consultation: description corrected — wisdom 523-001 |
| 2472 | 523-002 | H0998 | Term names understanding as a human inner-being capacity — the gift from God enabling discernment of his ways and wise conduct |  |
| 2493 | 524-001 | H8394 | Term names understanding as a divine attribute — the infinite discernment by which God creates, orders, and governs all things |  |
| 2494 | 524-002 | H8394 | Term names understanding as a human inner-being capacity — enabling wisdom, right conduct, and discernment, received from God |  |
| 2492 | 525-001 | H7922 | se.khel names a practical inner quality of good sense, prudence, and wise conduct. It produces outcomes — favour, success, slow anger. It can be given (1Ch 22:12), recognised (1Sa 25:3), or taught (Neh 8:8). Its absence closes hearts (Job 17:4). It has a dark use (Dan 8:25 — cunning serving deceit). |  |
| 2495 | 527-001 | H8454 | tu.shiy.yah names wisdom in its effectiveness — sound wisdom, effective knowledge, the capacity to produce good outcomes. It can be God's attribute (with him is sound wisdom) or a human resource (keep sound wisdom). Reading the verses shows these are the same characteristic in different registers, not two separate groups. |  |
| 2496 | 527-002 | H8454 | Term names sound wisdom as an inner resource available to the upright — a substantive inner capacity that God stores for those who walk with integrity |  |
| 2473 | 528-001 | H2450 | cha.kham in Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, and historical narrative names wisdom as a constituted inner quality of the person — not a role or a skill but who they are. The wise person is characterised by this quality: they receive instruction, they are cautious, they hold back their spirit, their heart is in the right place. The quality is relational — it shows in how they relate to others, to rebuke, to advice. |  |
| 2474 | 528-002 | H2450 | Term identifies wisdom as a quality that God gives to rulers and leaders — a divine endowment of inner discernment for governance, judgment, and leadership |  |
| 2475 | 528-003 | H2450 | cha.kham here names wisdom turned back on itself — the person who claims wisdom without the fear of the Lord, whose wisdom serves evil, or whose self-assessment of wisdom is the very sign of its absence. |  |
| 3607 | 528-004 | H2450,H2803G | cha.kham and cha.shav H2803G name an inner capacity for skilled craft that is explicitly Spirit-given. The skill resides in the heart/mind and is directed toward sacred construction. This is not general competence — it is an endowment for holy work. | Created by M15 Phase 7 (sub-group A, analytical code A-1) |
| 3608 | 528-005 | H2450 | cha.kham here names wisdom as the qualification for a role — the wise man sought for appointment, the wise men of the court, those who have the inner capacity for governance, law, and counsel. The focus is on wisdom as a capacity that qualifies persons for institutional responsibility. | Created by M15 Phase 7 (sub-group A, analytical code A-3) |
| 2465 | 530-001 | G4679 | Term names the act of making wise — the transformative work of Scripture on the inner person, imparting wisdom |  |
| 2470 | 531-001 | G5428 | Term names understanding as an inner-being capacity — the comprehension of God's will that enables transformed living |  |
| 2462 | 532-001 | G4678 | Term names wisdom as a divine attribute — the infinite and incomprehensible understanding belonging to God alone |  |
| 2463 | 532-002 | G4678,H2452 | Wisdom as a gift — God gives it, persons receive it, it enables what the recipient could not produce from themselves. The giving and receiving is the characteristic dynamic of this group. | Revised during Dimension Review Phase C/anchor consultation: description corrected — wisdom 532-002 |
| 2464 | 532-003 | G4678 | sofia naming wisdom as something visible in a particular person — their teaching, their speech, their deeds, their growth. This is not wisdom in the abstract but wisdom that can be seen and marvelled at in someone. |  |
| 1364 | 5662-001 | H7920 | Term names the inner act of contemplative attention — the focused consideration of what is perceived in a revelatory vision |  |
| 1613 | 5864-001 | G2058 | Term names interpretation as a Spirit-given inner capacity — a gift enabling the community to understand and be built up |  |
| 1610 | 5865-001 | G1328 | Term names the interpreter whose absence requires the speaker to keep inner communion with God rather than public expression |  |
| 1621 | 5883-001 | H1902H | Term names the inner meditation or thought — the heart's offering to God and the inner scheming of adversaries |  |
| 2138 | 6085-001 | H6592 | Term names the interpretive capacity as an expression of inner wisdom — the ability to discern meaning is a quality of the inner person whose wisdom transforms even outward bearing |  |
| 2190 | 6256-001 | H8637 | Term names the intimate act of teaching to walk — evoking the inner-relational dynamic between the caring teacher-parent and the dependent learner, with the poignancy of unrecognised nurture |  |
| 3609 | 6668-001 | H2445 | The chak.kim P-status verses all appear in Daniel 2–5. They name "the wise men of Babylon" — the institutional class of court wise men who cannot read divine mysteries. They form the consistent foil: what human wisdom (even at its most professional and prestigious) cannot do, the Spirit-given wisdom in Daniel can. The group's function is structural contrast — it makes visible what Spirit-given wisdom is by showing what it is not. | Created by M15 Phase 7 (sub-group A, analytical code A-13) |
| 2460 | 6676-001 | G0781 | Term names the absence of wisdom as an inner-being deficit — the foolish person's lack of understanding as a moral and relational failure |  |
| 2476 | 6696-001 | G4678,G4680,H2452 | Term names wisdom as a divine attribute — belonging to God and bestowed by him as an inner capacity enabling understanding of mysteries |  |
| 2477 | 6696-002 | H2452 | Term names wisdom as an inner capacity received from God — enabling reception and interpretation of divine revelation |  |
| 2843 | 7085-001 | G1226 | Diabebaioō as outward insistence arising from inner conviction |  |

| 1360 | 932-001 | H0995 | bin as the inner perceptive faculty in its constituted form — the quality of the wise, discerning person. The faculty is seated in the heart (1Ki 3:9, Psa 49:20), enables right judgment (1Ki 3:9,11), receives instruction (Pro 9:8), perceives situations rightly (2Sa 12:19, Job 6:30), and is the inner capacity from which wise conduct flows. It is morally conditioned — it enables covenant-keeping (Deu 4:6) and is blocked by the absence of the fear of the Lord. |  |

| 1825 | 996-001 | G5426 | froneō does not name a single faculty but the orientation of the whole inner person — what one is set on, caring about, thinking about as a habitual posture. The Philippians verses are central: "have this mind which is in Christ Jesus" is the paradigmatic use. Reading the verses, 1825 (mind on divine vs earthly) and 1826 (relational/communal mindedness) are two expressions of the same characteristic: froneō as the inner orientation of the whole person, directed toward an object. They belong in one group. |  |
| 1826 | 996-002 | G5426 | Term names the mind as the seat of communal unity and personal relationship — being of the same mind, sharing concern, pursuing agreement |  |

<a id="s4-5"></a>
### §4.5 Cross-cluster orphan findings/pointers

Findings + SD pointers that mention M-cluster vocabulary but originate from registries that do NOT contribute terms to this cluster — see [wa-cluster-m15-finding-orphans-v1-…](../../../outputs/markdown/) for the full scan.
